_meta.description: Complete merge: v0.4.0 (Watchers, Giants, Wandjina, Celtic, Norse Jotnar, Greek Titans, Vedic mothers, Ugaritic rpum, 99 canonical parallels, global-giants-complex framework) + v0.5 expansion (Buddhist, Zoroastrian, Taoist-Chinese, Yoruba, Andean, Polynesian, Slavic, Japanese, Celtic additions, dragon-slayer formula, dying-rising-god formula).

tier_definitions.1: Unmanifest Source (Monad, Ein Sof, Brahman-Nirguna, Tao, The One)
tier_definitions.2: True Most High / Divine Council (El Elyon, Pleroma, Aeons)
tier_definitions.4: Archons / Planetary Rulers (Seven Archons, Watchers, Olympians-as-rulers)
tier_definitions.cross-tier: Mediators / Light-Bringers / Sacrificed Gods (Christ, Sophia, Prometheus, Hermes, Quetzalcoatl)

traditions.name: Norse / Germanic
traditions.earliest_attestation: Roman era; Eddic material 13th c. CE

traditions.name: Aztec / Mesoamerican
traditions.earliest_attestation: c. 13th c. CE (earlier in Toltec, Teotihuacan)

traditions.name: Proto-Indo-European (Reconstructed)
regions: ancestral source of Indo-European language family
key_texts: No surviving texts — reconstructed via comparative method from Vedic, Avestan, Greek, Latin, Hittite, Old Norse, Old Irish, Baltic, Slavic, Tocharian cognates.
traditions.cosmological_structure: Sky-father (*Dyḗus Ph₂tḗr) presides over a divine council. Storm-father (*Perkʷunos) slays a serpent-of-the-waters (*H₂epom Nepōts / the cosmic dragon). Dawn (*H₂éwsōs) emerges daily. Hero-twin (*Trito) recovers the cattle stolen by the three-headed serpent. Fire (*H₁n̥gʷnis) is the messenger between gods and humans.
traditions.scholarly_note: PIE deities are linguistic reconstructions, not historically attested beings. They are the back-projected common ancestors of attested gods across daughter traditions. Inclusion here is for genealogical comparison — the structural trunk from which Greek, Roman, Vedic, Iranian, Norse, Celtic, Slavic, Baltic, Hittite, and Anatolian pantheons branched.

traditions.name: Second Temple Apocryphal / Enochic
key_texts: 1 Enoch (Book of the Watchers, Astronomical Book, Book of Parables, Animal Apocalypse)
traditions.cosmological_structure: Two hundred Watchers (*'irin*, 'wakeful ones') under Shemyaza and Azazel descend to Mount Hermon, take human wives, beget the Nephilim — giant hybrid offspring. The Watchers teach forbidden knowledge (metallurgy, weaponry, cosmetics, sorcery, astrology). Their offspring devour humanity, leading to the Flood. The Watchers are bound in the abyss; the spirits of the dead Nephilim become the unclean spirits / demons of the present age.

traditions.name: Canaanite / Ugaritic
traditions.geographic_origin: Levantine coast; primary corpus from Ugarit (Ras Shamra), c. 14th–12th c. BCE
traditions.earliest_attestation: Ugaritic tablets c. 1400–1200 BCE; older oral strata
traditions.cosmological_structure: El (high god, father of the gods) heads a divine council on Mount Lel; Baal-Hadad as storm-god / cosmic-orderer; Mot (death) and Yam (sea) as chaos powers; the rpum (Rephaim) as the deified royal dead / ancestral warrior-shades inhabiting Bashan and the underworld.
traditions.scholarly_note: This tradition is the immediate substrate for Hebrew religion. The El of Ugarit and the El Elyon of the older Hebrew layer are the same high god; the Hebrew Bible's divine council language (Psalm 82, Deut 32:8-9) is intelligible only against this background.

traditions.name: Celtic — Old Irish (Lebor Gabála + mythological cycle)
traditions.geographic_origin: Ireland (with broader Insular Celtic and continental Gaulish parallels)
traditions.earliest_attestation: Christian-era manuscripts (11th–12th c. CE) preserving older oral material; deities attested earlier in Romano-Celtic inscriptions
key_texts: Lebor Gabála Érenn (Book of the Taking of Ireland)
Cath Maige Tuired (Battle of Mag Tuired)
traditions.cosmological_structure: The Tuatha Dé Danann — 'Peoples of the Goddess Danu' — descend into Ireland and fight the Fomorians (chaos-powers / sea-giants). After defeat by the Milesians they retreat into the sídhe (otherworld mounds). The structure is recognizably the IE divine-vs-chthonic-powers conflict, with Danu / Danann as the divine mother.
traditions.scholarly_note: Old Irish Danu is reconstructed largely from the ethnonym Tuath Dé Danann; her independent attestation is thin. The Vedic-Celtic Danu parallel is linguistic (both descend from PIE *deh₂nu, 'river / flowing water') and was first proposed in the 19th c.; it remains widely cited in IE comparative mythology (e.g., David Frawley, Calvert Watkins's *How to Kill a Dragon*, Mallory & Adams's *Encyclopedia of IE Culture*).

traditions.name: Aboriginal Australian — Wandjina-Wunggurr (Kimberley)
traditions.geographic_origin: Kimberley region, Western Australia (Worrorra, Ngarinyin, Wunambal peoples)
traditions.earliest_attestation: Wandjina rock-art tradition dates to c. 4,000 years before present (Gwion Gwion / Bradshaw figures in the same region are much older, possibly 17,000+ years); the broader cultural continuity is at least 60,000 years
key_texts: The rock-art galleries themselves are the primary text (an active, living, repainted tradition)
Oral Law transmitted by senior custodians
Mowanjum Art and Culture Centre archives (contemporary continuation)
traditions.cosmological_structure: Idjair, the first Wandjina, lives in the Milky Way and is the father of all Wandjinas. His son Wallungunder created the Earth and all life on it. Wallungunder created the first humans, the Gyorn Gyorn (Gwion Gwion) people. He returned to Idjair to bring more Wandjinas down to give the Gyorn Gyorn the Law. Each of the three Mowanjum peoples has its own primary Wandjina: Namarali (Worrorra), Wodjin/Wanalirri (Ngarinyin), Rimijmarra (Wunambal). The Rainbow-Serpent-analogue creator-being Wunggurr is a related but distinct figure. Wandjina control rain, lightning, cyclones, fertility, and the Law itself.
traditions.scholarly_note: This tradition is alive, sacred, and held by specific custodial communities. The Wandjina figures may be painted only by authorized individuals; repainting on rock walls is a ceremonial act of cosmic renewal, not artistic restoration. The representations in this database are descriptive and respectful and should be read as pointing TO the tradition rather than as containing it. The mouthless face is theologically loaded: a speech-organ is absent because the Wandjina's power is so great that it acts directly by will, without verbal mediation. Cross-tradition parallels here are noted with caution; the Worrorra/Ngarinyin/Wunambal frameworks have their own integrity and should not be reduced to instances of imported categories.

traditions.name: Northern Paiute (Great Basin)
traditions.geographic_origin: Great Basin — Nevada, eastern California, southern Oregon, southwestern Idaho
traditions.earliest_attestation: Oral tradition; first written attestation in Sarah Winnemucca's *Life Among the Piutes* (1883)
key_texts: Sarah Winnemucca Hopkins, Life Among the Piutes: Their Wrongs and Claims (1883)
L.L. Loud & M.R. Harrington, Lovelock Cave (1929)
Ongoing oral tradition of the Pyramid Lake Paiute Tribe and other Paiute communities
traditions.cosmological_structure: Paiute creation narratives speak of an earlier age inhabited by 'beautiful giants' who lived between the Sierra Nevada and the Rockies. After they mistreated a disfigured child, the Great Spirit made the land hot and allowed enemies to conquer them. The Si-Te-Cah ('tule-eaters') tradition specifically describes a hostile red-haired people the Paiute warred against and ultimately destroyed at Lovelock Cave.
traditions.scholarly_note: The Si-Te-Cah tradition is doubly contested: (1) some Paiute accounts describe them as ordinary humans hostile to the Northern Paiute, while later versions describe them as red-haired giants and cannibals; (2) the 1911 Lovelock Cave discovery was partly sensationalized by tourist-display entrepreneurs, and academic re-examination (Loud & Harrington, and a 1970s University of Nevada study) found skeletons of normal stature. The 'reddish hair' is now generally attributed to post-mortem pigment degradation. Adrienne Mayor's *Fossil Legends of the First Americans* offers the most balanced treatment. The tradition deserves to be in a comparative-giants database with these caveats clearly attached.

traditions.name: Wider Insular Celtic + Continental Celtic (cross-cultural references)
traditions.earliest_attestation: Romano-Celtic inscriptions; medieval Insular Celtic manuscripts
traditions.cosmological_structure: Pan-Celtic divine figures with reflexes across Insular and Continental traditions: Lugus / Lugh / Lleu; Brigantia / Brigid; Cernunnos; Epona. Many Continental Celtic deities were syncretized with Roman ones (interpretatio romana).
traditions.scholarly_note: Used as a reference tradition for pan-Celtic parallels beyond the Old Irish core.

traditions.name: Jainism (Digambara / Shvetambara)
traditions.earliest_attestation: c. 599-527 BCE (Mahavira traditional); older Parsvanatha tradition

traditions.name: Mayan (Classic / Postclassic)
traditions.earliest_attestation: c. 2000 BCE (pre-Classic begins); Popol Vuh written c. 1550 CE

traditions.name: Dogon (Mali)
traditions.earliest_attestation: Griaule field research 1930s; tradition much older

traditions.name: San / Bushmen
traditions.earliest_attestation: oral tradition; among oldest living traditions

traditions.name: Korean Shamanic (Musok / Sindo)
traditions.earliest_attestation: oral tradition; Dangun myth recorded c. 1281 CE (Samguk Yusa)

traditions.name: Mongolian Tengrism / Mongolian Shamanism
traditions.earliest_attestation: oral tradition; texts c. 13th c. CE

traditions.name: Baltic (Lithuanian / Latvian)
traditions.earliest_attestation: oral tradition; recorded 19th c.; oldest Lithuanian preserved PIE religion

traditions.name: Finnish / Karelian (Kalevala tradition)
traditions.earliest_attestation: oral tradition; Kalevala compiled 1835 CE by Elias Lonnrot

traditions.name: Basque (Euskara)
traditions.earliest_attestation: oral tradition; Basque language pre-IE; tradition very ancient

traditions.name: Chinese Folk Religion (popular / syncretistic)
traditions.earliest_attestation: documented Song dynasty onward; roots much older

traditions.name: Welsh (Mabinogion tradition)
traditions.earliest_attestation: written c. 1100-1400 CE; oral much older

traditions.name: Thai Buddhism / Theravada Southeast Asian
traditions.earliest_attestation: c. 200 CE (arrival); Theravada dominant c. 800 CE

traditions.name: Kabbalah (Jewish mysticism)
traditions.earliest_attestation: c. 1175 CE (Sefer ha-Bahir); Sefer Yetzirah c. 300 CE; Zohar c. 1280 CE

traditions.name: Greek Chthonic / Pre-Olympian
traditions.earliest_attestation: Hesiod c. 700 BCE; Orphic c. 600 BCE

traditions.name: Georgian / Kartvelian
traditions.earliest_attestation: oral tradition; pre-Christian elements preserved in Christian period

traditions.name: Haudenosaunee (depth expansion)
traditions.earliest_attestation: oral tradition; League founded c. 1450 CE

deities.etymology: Greek 'monas' — unity, oneness
deities.summary: The unknowable, ineffable Source beyond all categories. Not a creator-being but the ground of being itself, from which all emanation proceeds.

primary_sources: On the Origin of the World (NHC II,5)
deities.etymology: Possibly 'child of chaos' or 'begetter of Sabaoth'
deities.summary: Abortive offspring of Sophia, the Demiurge who fashioned the material cosmos in ignorance of the Pleroma above him. Declares himself the only God; rebuked by the voice from above as 'Samael, blind god.'

deities.etymology: From Hebrew root H-W-H, 'to be'; 'He causes to be' or 'I AM'
deities.summary: The covenantal God of Israel. In orthodox reading: the one true God, creator and lawgiver. In Gnostic reading: the Demiurge mistaken for the Most High.

deities.etymology: Hebrew/Canaanite: 'God Most High'
deities.summary: In the older Canaanite-Hebrew layer, El Elyon is the head of the divine council who divides the nations among his sons. Yahweh is one of those sons, assigned to Israel. Later orthodoxy collapses El Elyon and Yahweh into one being; Gnostics keep them distinct, mapping El Elyon to the true Father and Yahweh to the Demiurge.

deities.etymology: Possibly 'pillar' or 'instigator'
deities.summary: Morally ambivalent Egyptian god. Early periods: protector of Ra against Apophis. Later periods: murderer of Osiris, emblem of chaos.

deities.etymology: Sumerian AMAR.UTU — 'calf of the sun'
deities.summary: Chief god of Babylon. Slays Tiamat, the primordial chaos-mother, and fashions the world from her dismembered body. Establishes cosmic order and is exalted above all other gods.

deities.etymology: Sumerian Nin-an-ak, 'Lady of Heaven'
deities.summary: Goddess of love and war, of fertility and destruction. Crosses every boundary including life/death; descends to the underworld and returns transformed.

deities.etymology: From PIE *dyḗws — 'sky', 'shining heaven'
deities.summary: King of the Olympian gods. Sky-father, storm-god, lawgiver, oath-keeper, philanderer. Carries the contradictions of cosmic kingship.

deities.etymology: Greek 'forethought'
deities.summary: Titan who steals fire from the gods to give to humanity, against Zeus's will. Punished by being chained to a rock where an eagle eats his liver daily.

deities.earliest_attestation: Roman-era Germanic; Eddic material 13th c. CE
deities.etymology: Proto-Germanic *Wōðanaz, 'fury / inspiration / poetic frenzy'
deities.summary: All-Father of the Norse pantheon. Restless wisdom-seeker who sacrifices himself to himself on Yggdrasil. Wanderer in disguise. Carries knowledge of his own doom at Ragnarök.
Trickster of the Norse pantheon. Helper and adversary of the gods. Father of Hel, Fenrir, and Jörmungandr. Eventually bound for the killing of Baldr; will break free at Ragnarök.

deities.etymology: Sanskrit 'auspicious'
deities.summary: Destroyer-transformer of the Hindu trimurti. Encompasses contradictions: ascetic and erotic, creator and destroyer, terrible and benevolent.

deities.earliest_attestation: Mahabharata; full theological development by c. 1st millennium CE
deities.etymology: Sanskrit 'dark', 'black'
deities.summary: Eighth avatar of Vishnu. Cosmic teacher (Bhagavad Gita), divine lover (Vrindavan), warrior-counselor (Mahabharata). Reveals himself as the supreme Source.

deities.etymology: 'Feathered serpent' — quetzal feathers + coatl
deities.summary: Feathered serpent. Union of sky and earth. Civilizing teacher who gave humans corn, calendar, writing. Opposes blood sacrifice. Exiled by Tezcatlipoca; prophesies his return.

deities.etymology: 'Smoking Mirror'
deities.summary: Most complex god of the Aztec pantheon. Night sky, sorcery, memory, fate, war, rulership. Quetzalcoatl's shadow-twin and adversary.

deities.etymology: Greek 'wisdom'
deities.summary: Wisdom — the last and youngest of the Aeons. Acts without her consort and produces Yaldabaoth. Descends into matter to recover the divine sparks. Both the cause of the fall and the means of return.

deities.earliest_attestation: c. 8th c. BCE (Hesiod, Homeric Hymn to Demeter)
primary_sources: Eleusinian Mysteries (ritual context, c. 1500 BCE – 4th c. CE)
deities.etymology: Greek, possibly 'bringer of destruction' or pre-Greek origin
deities.summary: Daughter of Demeter and Zeus. Abducted by Hades to the underworld; her mother's grief brings winter to the earth. Eats pomegranate seeds and is therefore bound to return to the underworld for part of each year. The descended-and-returning goddess at the center of the Eleusinian Mysteries — the most important mystery cult of the ancient Mediterranean.

deities.earliest_attestation: c. 50 CE (earliest Pauline letters); historical figure c. 4 BCE – c. 30 CE
primary_sources: Gospel of John (esp. prologue, Logos theology)
Gospel of Thomas, Gospel of Philip (esoteric)
deities.etymology: Greek Christos 'anointed' = Hebrew Mashiach (Messiah)
deities.summary: Central figure of Christianity. Read on multiple levels: historical Jewish teacher executed under Pontius Pilate; theological Logos incarnate; Gnostic Christ-Aeon descending to wake the sparks of light; esoteric Solar-Logos. Combines the dying-rising god, the descended teacher, the sacrificed king, and the returning judge in a single figure.

deities.earliest_attestation: c. 1st–3rd c. CE (Corpus Hermeticum); rooted in earlier Egyptian Thoth tradition
primary_sources: Discourse on the Eighth and Ninth (Nag Hammadi)
deities.etymology: Greek Hermês Trismegistos 'Thrice-Greatest Hermes'
deities.summary: Syncretic figure combining Greek Hermes (messenger, psychopomp, guide of souls) with Egyptian Thoth (scribe of the gods, master of wisdom and writing). The legendary author of the Hermetica — texts presenting a path of gnosis through cosmic understanding. The tutelary figure of Renaissance Hermeticism, alchemy, and the entire Western esoteric tradition. 'As above, so below.'

deities.earliest_attestation: Reconstructed; root attested in daughter languages by c. 2000 BCE
primary_sources: Reconstructed via cognate set: Vedic Dyaus Pita, Greek Zeus Pater, Latin Iūpiter (Dieu-piter), Illyrian Deipaturos, Hittite Šiu, Luwian Tiwaz, Norse Týr (Proto-Germanic *Tīwaz), Albanian Zojz.
deities.etymology: *dyḗws ('shining sky, daylight heaven') + *ph₂tḗr ('father'). Root *dyew- 'to shine, day-sky' — same root that gives Latin diēs, Sanskrit div, and the English word 'day'.
iconography: (daughter traditions add: thunderbolt, oak, eagle, throne)
deities.summary: The reconstructed sky-father at the root of the Indo-European pantheon. The shining daytime heaven personified, addressed as father. Every daughter tradition's high god — Zeus, Jupiter, Tyr/Týr, Dyaus, Tiwaz — descends from this single linguistic and religious root. The clearest single piece of evidence that the scattered Indo-European peoples once shared a common cult.

deities.earliest_attestation: Reconstructed; cognates attested from c. 1500 BCE
primary_sources: Reconstructed via: Vedic Parjanya, Lithuanian Perkūnas, Latvian Pērkons, Slavic Perun, Norse Fjörgynn (Thor's mother's name; Thor inherits the function), Celtic Hercynia (oak-forest), Hittite Pirwa, Albanian Perëndi.
deities.etymology: From PIE *per- 'to strike' (or *perkʷu- 'oak'). The Striker — he who strikes with the thunderbolt — and/or the god of the oak (the tree most often struck by lightning).
deities.summary: The reconstructed storm-warrior of the Indo-European pantheon. Wields a stone axe or hammer (later: thunderbolt). Slays the serpent-of-the-waters who has dammed the cosmic rivers — the Chaoskampf myth that recurs in every IE daughter tradition. Inherited by Thor (hammer slays Jörmungandr), Indra (vajra slays Vritra), Zeus (thunderbolt slays Typhon), Perun (axe slays Veles).

primary_sources: Reconstructed via: Vedic Uṣas, Greek Ēōs, Latin Aurōra, Lithuanian Aušrinė, Latvian Auseklis, Anglo-Saxon Ēostre (origin of 'Easter'), Germanic *Austrōn, Hittite (likely related) Šiwat.
deities.etymology: From PIE *h₂ews- 'to shine, dawn'. Same root that gives Latin aurum (gold — the dawn-colored metal) and the cardinal direction 'east' (the place of dawning).
iconography: drawn by horses across the sky
veil that lifts as she rises
deities.summary: The reconstructed dawn-goddess. Daughter of the sky-father. Eternally young, eternally rising, the bringer of daylight. Her name survives in 'Easter' — the springtime resurrection feast absorbed by Christianity retains the PIE dawn-goddess's name. One of the most securely reconstructed PIE deities.

deities.earliest_attestation: Reconstructed myth; cognate narratives from c. 1500 BCE
primary_sources: Reconstructed via comparative mythology: Vedic Trita Āptya, Avestan Thraētaona (slays the three-headed Aži Dahāka), Norse Thor (vs. Jörmungandr), Roman Hercules (vs. Cacus, who stole his cattle), Greek Heracles (vs. Geryon), Hittite Tarhunna (vs. Illuyanka). Reconstructed by Bruce Lincoln, Calvert Watkins, and others.
deities.etymology: PIE *tri- 'three' — 'the Third' or 'the Third Man'.
iconography: warrior with a club, hammer, or thunderweapon
deities.summary: The reconstructed PIE warrior-hero. A three-headed serpent (or the monster Trikephalos / Triśiras / Aži Dahāka) steals the primordial cattle. Trito, often with the help of the storm-god, slays the serpent and recovers the herd — establishing the warrior's right to take back what has been wrongly taken. The ur-myth of the Indo-European warrior class; ancestor of every dragon-slaying hero from Heracles to Thor to St. George.

primary_sources: Reconstructed via: Vedic Agni, Latin ignis, Lithuanian ugnis, Slavic ogn'/ogon', Hittite Agniš. The deity-status is clearest in Vedic; the cognate set establishes the PIE root.
deities.etymology: PIE *h₁n̥gʷnis 'fire'. Latin ignis; English 'ignite'.
deities.summary: The reconstructed PIE personification of sacrificial fire. The fire on the altar that carries the offering up to the gods is itself divine — the visible messenger between human and heavenly realms. The cognate set is linguistically secure; whether the PIE speakers worshipped fire as a personal deity (as the Vedic Indians clearly did) or only as a sacred element is debated.

deities.earliest_attestation: Rigveda, c. 1500–1200 BCE (minor god); full development by c. 1st millennium CE
deities.etymology: Sanskrit, possibly from *viś- 'to pervade' — 'the All-Pervader'
iconography: reclining on Shesha the cosmic serpent on the ocean of milk
deities.summary: Preserver of the trimurti. The cosmic principle that maintains dharma across the yuga cycles. Descends as a series of avatars (traditionally ten: Matsya, Kurma, Varaha, Narasimha, Vamana, Parashurama, Rama, Krishna, Buddha, and the future Kalki) whenever dharma declines. In Vaishnava theology, the supreme Source itself, with Brahma and Shiva as his expressions.

deities.earliest_attestation: Rigveda, c. 1500–1200 BCE (the most-invoked god of the Rigveda)
primary_sources: Rigveda (roughly 250 hymns to Indra — more than any other deity)
deities.etymology: Sanskrit; possibly related to *indu- 'drop' (of soma) or to PIE *h₂nḗr 'man, virile'
deities.summary: Storm-king of the Vedic pantheon. Slayer of Vritra the serpent who dammed the cosmic waters. Drinker of soma. King of Svarga (the heavenly realm). In the Vedic period, the supreme warrior-god; in the Puranic period, demoted to chief of the lesser devas. Direct cognate of the broader Indo-European storm-warrior.

deities.etymology: Sanskrit; possibly from *wer- 'to cover, encompass' (the all-encompassing sky). Often paired with PIE *Werunos; cognate proposals link to Greek Ouranos.
deities.summary: Vedic sovereign of cosmic order (rta) and of the oath. Originally the all-encompassing sky, paired with Mitra as the Mitra-Varuna dyad — Mitra the friendly contract, Varuna the dread oath. Sees all wrongdoing; binds the false-swearer with his noose. Later demoted to god of waters and oceans. The clearest Vedic parallel to El, Yahweh-as-covenantal, and Zeus Horkios.

deities.earliest_attestation: Rigveda, c. 1500–1200 BCE; also in the Mitanni treaty c. 1380 BCE
primary_sources: Mitanni-Hittite treaty (1380 BCE — Mitra invoked as oath-witness)
Roman Mithraic liturgies and reliefs (for Mithras)
deities.etymology: From Indo-Iranian *mitra- 'contract, alliance, friend' — possibly from PIE *mei- 'to bind, exchange'
deities.summary: The contract-deity. Mitra is the sworn agreement made visible. Paired with Varuna in Vedic religion; independent in Iranian as Mithra (mediator between Ahura Mazda and humanity). The Roman mystery-cult of Mithras absorbed his iconography in the late antique period. One of the oldest continuously-attested Indo-Iranian deities — present in the 1380 BCE Mitanni treaty.

deities.etymology: Sanskrit 'twin' (paired with his sister Yami); cognate with Avestan Yima
deities.summary: First mortal — the first being to die — who became king of the dead and judge of the deceased. Lord of the southern direction. His Avestan cognate Yima (Jamshid in Persian) is the first king of the golden age, not a death-god — a clue to the figure's shifting role across Indo-Iranian tradition. Closest Vedic parallel to Osiris, Hades, Hel, and Anubis.

deities.earliest_attestation: Rigveda, c. 1500–1200 BCE (the very first hymn of the Rigveda is to Agni)
deities.etymology: Sanskrit; cognate with Latin ignis, Slavic ogon', Hittite Agniš — all from PIE *h₁n̥gʷnis 'fire'
iconography: two heads (the household fire and the sacrificial fire)
deities.summary: Personified fire — both the household hearth and the sacrificial altar-flame. Messenger between humans and gods: the offering burned on Agni's altar is carried by him to the recipient deity. Mediates every Vedic sacrifice. Direct cognate of the broader Indo-European fire-god root.

deities.earliest_attestation: Devi Mahatmya, c. 5th–6th c. CE; antecedents in earlier Tantric and tribal traditions
deities.etymology: Sanskrit, feminine of kāla 'time, black' — 'She who is Time' or 'The Black One'
iconography: four arms with sword, severed head, and gestures of fearlessness and boon-giving
deities.summary: Goddess of time, destruction, and ultimate liberation. Born from Durga's brow in the fight against the demon Raktabija. Drinks the blood of her enemies before it touches the ground. Stands on Shiva — when she steps on him she pauses her destruction. Most fearsome and most liberating of the Hindu goddesses: in Tantric theology, the supreme reality itself in its destructive aspect, and the most direct path to moksha for those who can bear her face.

deities.earliest_attestation: Mahabharata (mentioned); Kalki Purana, c. 17th–18th c. CE
deities.etymology: Sanskrit, possibly 'destroyer of foulness' or 'eternity-bearer'
iconography: (sometimes:) Kalki with the head of a horse
deities.summary: The tenth and future avatar of Vishnu. Will appear at the end of the Kali Yuga, riding a white horse and wielding a flaming sword, to destroy adharma and inaugurate the next Satya Yuga (golden age). Parallel of every returning-savior figure: the second-coming Christ, the Mahdi, the Maitreya Buddha, the hidden Imam, the returning Quetzalcoatl, the once-and-future Arthur, Saoshyant.

alternate_names: The Sons of God (bene ha-elohim, Genesis 6)
deities.earliest_attestation: Genesis 6:1–4 (canonical, dated to the Yahwist/Priestly source layers, c. 6th c. BCE redaction); 1 Enoch Book of the Watchers, 3rd c. BCE
deities.etymology: Aramaic ʿīr 'wakeful, watchful one'. The name reflects their function: angels who never sleep, who keep watch over the cosmos. Hence 'Watchers' in English.
iconography: (in later art:) bound in chains in the abyss
deities.summary: Two hundred angelic beings under Shemyaza and Azazel who descended to Mount Hermon, swore a mutual oath, took human wives, and begat the Nephilim. They taught humanity forbidden arts: Azazel taught metallurgy and weaponry and cosmetics; Shemyaza taught enchantments; others taught astrology, divination, pharmacology. For these transgressions they were bound in the earth until the day of judgment. In Enochic tradition they are the source of the wickedness that brought on the Flood.

primary_sources: Leviticus 16:8–10 (the scapegoat 'for Azazel' in Day of Atonement)
1 Enoch 10 (Azazel bound in the desert until judgment)
deities.etymology: Disputed. Possibly 'strength of God' (ʿazaz + ʾēl), or 'goat-god' (ʿez + ʾēl), or a place-name. In Leviticus the name refers either to a destination or to a recipient — the high priest releases the sin-bearing goat 'to Azazel' in the wilderness.
iconography: bound in the wilderness with rocks heaped upon him
deities.summary: Chief Watcher (alongside Shemyaza). In 1 Enoch he is the one who taught humans to make swords, daggers, shields, and breastplates, and taught women to paint their faces and wear ornaments. For this God commands Raphael to bind him hand and foot and cast him into a hole in the desert called Dudael, where he must remain until the final judgment. The Levitical scapegoat ritual is — on one reading — the annual symbolic transfer of Israel's sins back to the angelic source of human corruption.

primary_sources: Numbers 13:32–33 (the Anakim seen by Israelite spies in Canaan)
1 Enoch 7, 9, 15 (origin and fate of the Nephilim)
deities.etymology: From Hebrew *naphal* 'to fall' — 'the fallen ones'. Alternative reading: from Aramaic *nephila* 'giant'. Most translations render 'giants', following the Septuagint *gigantes*.
iconography: giants of enormous height (Numbers 13:33: 'we were like grasshoppers in our own sight')
(in Book of Giants:) named individuals like Ohyah and Hahyah
deities.summary: The hybrid offspring of the Watchers and human women. They are described as giants — physical, enormous, and corrupted. They consume the produce of humanity, then humanity itself, before turning on each other. In 1 Enoch's account, when their physical bodies are killed in the Flood, their spirits do not die — they become the unclean spirits (demons) that afflict humanity in the present age. This is the Enochic theory of the origin of demons.

deities.earliest_attestation: Genesis 5:21–24 (canonical); 1 Enoch from c. 3rd c. BCE
primary_sources: Genesis 5:21–24 ('Enoch walked with God; and he was not, for God took him.')
1 Enoch (Book of the Watchers, Astronomical Book, Book of Parables, Animal Apocalypse, Epistle of Enoch)
3 Enoch / Sefer Hekhalot (Enoch's transformation into Metatron)
deities.etymology: Hebrew Ḥanok, possibly 'dedicated' or 'initiated'
iconography: (as Metatron:) 'the lesser YHWH' on a throne, with seventy names
deities.summary: Seventh from Adam. Did not die — was taken bodily to heaven. Intercedes for the bound Watchers (they ask him to plead for them; his plea is refused). Receives extensive cosmological revelations during his heavenly tour. In 3 Enoch (later, Merkavah mysticism) he is transformed into Metatron, the 'lesser YHWH,' second only to God himself — the highest-ranked angelic figure in some Jewish esoteric traditions. The pattern of the ascended human seer who becomes a divine intermediary.

deities.earliest_attestation: Ugaritic Baal Cycle, c. 1400–1200 BCE; older West Semitic roots
deities.etymology: Common Semitic *ʾil-, 'god' / 'the strong one'; cognate with Hebrew El, Akkadian ilu, Arabic Allāh
deities.summary: The high god of the Canaanite pantheon. Father of the gods, head of the divine council, dweller at the source of the two rivers. Bestows kingship and offspring. Withdrawn from active rule — Baal does the fighting — but the ultimate authority. He is the direct prototype of the older-layer Hebrew El / El Elyon.

deities.earliest_attestation: Ugaritic Baal Cycle; Hebrew Bible polemic against her cult
deities.etymology: Ugaritic Athirat, possibly 'she who treads upon the sea'
deities.summary: Consort of El, mother of seventy sons (the divine council). The Kuntillet Ajrud inscriptions ('YHWH and his Asherah') indicate she was worshipped alongside YHWH in popular Israelite religion before the Deuteronomistic reform suppressed her cult.

deities.earliest_attestation: Bronze Age West Semitic; Baal Cycle c. 1400–1200 BCE
deities.etymology: Baal = 'lord, master'; Hadad = the storm-god proper name
deities.summary: Storm-god, lord of fertility, slayer of Yam (sea-chaos) and dier-and-riser before Mot (death). The seasonal pattern of his cycle — death, descent, return — is the structural prototype for the Tammuz / Adonis / Osiris / Dionysus / Christ dying-and-rising-god type.

deities.etymology: Ugaritic mt, 'death', cognate with Hebrew mavet
deities.summary: Death personified. Devours Baal in the dry season; Anat in turn destroys Mot to bring back the rains. The cycle establishes the death-of-the-fertility-god pattern.

deities.etymology: Ugaritic ym, 'sea'
deities.summary: Sea-chaos, the primordial waters threatening to overwhelm cosmic order. Baal slays Yam to establish kingship. The combat structurally parallels Marduk vs Tiamat, Indra vs Vritra, Thor vs Jörmungandr, YHWH vs Leviathan.

deities.earliest_attestation: Ugaritic + Egyptian (Anat venerated in New Kingdom Egypt)
deities.etymology: Possibly 'providence' or 'sign'
iconography: wading in blood up to the thighs
deities.summary: Warrior virgin, sister-consort of Baal. Searches for Baal in the underworld and tears Mot apart to release him. Combines violent war and erotic-fertile aspects in one figure. Parallel to Inanna's descent.

deities.earliest_attestation: Ugaritic Rephaim Texts (KTU 1.20–1.22), c. 13th c. BCE
primary_sources: Hebrew Bible: Gen 14:5, Deut 2:11, Deut 3:11, Josh 12:4, Isa 14:9, Job 26:5
deities.etymology: Ugaritic rpʾ, 'to heal' — but in funerary context the rpum are the 'healed/restored ones,' i.e. the deified royal dead. Hebrew Rephaim carries both senses: living giant-warriors AND shades of the dead.
iconography: kings on chariots in the netherworld
associated with Bashan, Mt Hermon, the Golan
deities.summary: Ancestral warrior-shades, the deified royal dead of Ugarit, summoned in ritual feasts (marziḥu) to bless the living king. In the Hebrew Bible the term Rephaim sits across two registers: (1) the living giant-clans of Canaan — Og of Bashan is 'the last of the Rephaim' (Deut 3:11) — and (2) the shades in Sheol (Isa 14:9, Ps 88:10). Scholarly consensus is that the Ugaritic rpum and the biblical Rephaim share the same religious complex: ancestral warrior-dead centered on Bashan and Mt Hermon, the same territory the Bible assigns to King Og.

deities.earliest_attestation: Reconstructed from ethnonym Tuath Dé Danann attested in medieval Irish manuscripts (Lebor Gabála Érenn, 11th–12th c. CE); the name likely preserves much older PIE material
deities.etymology: From PIE *deh₂nu- 'river / flowing water' — same root as Vedic Dānu, Avestan dānu, and the river-names Danube, Don, Dnieper, Dniester, Donets, all of which preserve the original goddess-name as hydronyms
deities.summary: Ancestral mother of the Tuatha Dé Danann ('Peoples of the Goddess Danu'), the pre-Christian Irish divine race. Her independent attestation is thin — she survives mainly in the ethnonym and in place-names — but the linguistic descent from PIE *deh₂nu makes her the Celtic reflex of the same primordial water/mother goddess whose Vedic reflex is Dānu, mother of the Danavas.

deities.etymology: Old Irish 'good god' — good in the sense of 'skillful, mighty'
iconography: club that kills with one end and revives with the other
deities.summary: Father-figure of the Tuatha Dé. Wields the lorg mór (great club) and possesses the cauldron of abundance. The Irish parallel to the PIE sky-father / all-father archetype.

deities.earliest_attestation: Medieval Irish material; Gaulish god Lugus attested earlier
deities.etymology: Disputed; possibly from PIE *leuk- 'light' or *leug- 'oath / binding'
deities.summary: Polymath-hero of the Tuatha Dé. Slays his maternal grandfather Balor of the Evil Eye at the Second Battle of Mag Tuired. As many-skilled god he parallels Hermes / Odin / Krishna as a cross-tier polymath.

deities.earliest_attestation: Medieval Irish material; older Celtic war-goddess substrate
deities.etymology: Either 'Great Queen' (Old Irish mór + ríoghain) or 'Phantom Queen' (mor- 'terror')
deities.summary: Triple war-goddess and fate-weaver. Appears as raven over the battlefield. Parallels the Norse Valkyries, the Vedic Nirṛti, and the cross-cultural sovereignty-goddess who tests heroes.

deities.etymology: Disputed; possibly fo-mor 'under-sea / under-spectres' — chthonic / submarine origin
deities.summary: Chaos-powers and chthonic giants of Irish myth, defeated by the Tuatha Dé at the Second Battle of Mag Tuired. They occupy the structural slot of the Greek Titans, the Vedic Danavas / Asuras, and the Norse Jötnar — the older, defeated, monstrous race against which the gods of the cultivated cosmos define themselves.

deities.etymology: Possibly from a root meaning 'flashing' or 'deadly'
iconography: single great eye that destroys what it looks upon
deities.summary: King of the Fomorians, slain by his own grandson Lugh, who hurls a sling-stone through Balor's evil eye. The grandfather-slain-by-grandson motif parallels Kronos / Zeus and the dragon-slayer pattern.

deities.earliest_attestation: Rigveda (as one of the seven seers, Saptarṣi); fully developed in Brahmanas, epics, Puranas
deities.etymology: Sanskrit 'tortoise'; also linked to kaśya 'liquor' / 'wisdom'
deities.summary: Saptarṣi — one of the seven primordial seers — and prajāpati (progenitor) of nearly every class of being in the cosmos. Through his thirteen wives, the daughters of Daksha, he fathers the devas (through Aditi), the daityas (through Diti), the danavas (through Danu), the nāgas (through Kadru), Garuda and Aruna (through Vinata), and many other races. Structurally he sits in the slot occupied elsewhere by Adam or by El: the single male progenitor whose multiple consorts produce the contending divine lineages.

deities.earliest_attestation: Rigveda (multiple hymns; one of the oldest goddesses)
deities.etymology: Sanskrit a-diti, 'unbounded' / 'free from limits' — privative of diti, 'bound'
deities.summary: The unbounded, the infinite. Mother of the Ādityas — the solar/sky deities including Varuna, Mitra, Bhaga, Indra (in some lists), and in later layers Vishnu. Her name is the negative of Diti's: the unbound vs the bound. The structural pairing of Aditi and Diti is the Vedic version of the wider IE pattern in which the cosmos is born of two contending mothers.

deities.earliest_attestation: Late Rigveda / Brahmanas; fully developed in Puranas
deities.etymology: Sanskrit diti, 'bound / limited' — privative pair with aditi
deities.summary: Wife of Kashyapa, mother of the Daityas — the asura-clan opposed to the devas. After Indra kills her sons she does penance for a son who will defeat Indra; Indra preempts by entering her womb and splitting the fetus into the Maruts (storm-gods). She structurally occupies the slot of the 'mother of the defeated divine race' — the divine mother on the wrong side of cosmic order.

deities.earliest_attestation: Rigveda (RV 1.32, 2.11, etc. — esp. in Indra-Vṛtra hymns)
deities.etymology: From PIE *deh₂nu- 'flowing water / river' — directly cognate with Old Irish Danu, Avestan dānu, and the river-names Danube, Don, Dnieper, Dniester, Donets (all of which preserve the goddess-name as hydronyms). One of the most secure IE goddess-etymologies.
deities.summary: Vedic goddess of the primordial waters. Mother of Vṛtra (the cosmic serpent who withholds the waters until Indra slays him) and of the Dānavas (the broader asura-clan descended from her through Kashyapa). Rigveda 1.32 explicitly names her: 'The mother of Vṛtra, Dānu, lay like a cow with her calf.' She is the Vedic reflex of the same PIE water-mother whose Celtic reflex appears in Tuath Dé Danann.

deities.earliest_attestation: Rigveda (as an Aditya); developed in Brahmanas and Puranas
deities.etymology: Sanskrit dakṣa, 'capable / skillful'
iconography: father of consorts of the gods
deities.summary: Prajāpati (progenitor) whose thirteen daughters — Aditi, Diti, Danu, Kadru, Vinata, and others — are given as wives to Kashyapa. Through this single fatherhood, every class of cosmic being — devas, asuras, daityas, danavas, nāgas, garuḍas — shares Daksha as common grandfather. Famously beheaded by Shiva's wrath at the Daksha Yajña; revived with a goat's head.

deities.earliest_attestation: Vedic asura references; clan-specific naming in Brahmanas and Puranas
deities.etymology: Sanskrit daitya, 'descendant of Diti'
deities.summary: The asura-clan descended from Diti through Kashyapa. Their dynastic members include Hiranyakashipu, Hiranyaksha, Prahlada, Virochana, and Bali. Repeatedly defeated by the devas (often via Vishnu's avatars), they nevertheless contain moral exemplars — Prahlada and Bali are great devotees. They occupy the structural slot of the 'older, mighty, defeated divine race' — the same slot as the Greek Titans, the Norse Jötnar, the Irish Fomorians, the Mesopotamian older gods slain by Marduk, and arguably the biblical Anakim / Rephaim / Nephilim.

deities.earliest_attestation: Rigveda (collectively, as enemies of Indra); clan-specific naming later
deities.etymology: Sanskrit dānava, 'descendant of Dānu'
deities.summary: The asura-clan descended from Dānu through Kashyapa. Vṛtra is their archetypal member; in later literature the architect Maya Dānava is the master-craftsman of the asuras, surpassing even Vishvakarma. Rigveda calls them amānuṣa ('unhuman'). They occupy the same structural slot as the Greek Titans and the Irish Fomorians, with the additional feature — unique to the Vedic version — that the linguistic descent of the matriarch's name is shared with the Celtic Danu, giving a rare case of named IE goddess-continuity.

deities.earliest_attestation: Rigveda (RV 1.32 is the central Indra-Vṛtra hymn)
deities.etymology: Sanskrit vṛtra, 'the encloser / obstructor' — root vṛ- 'to cover, withhold'
deities.summary: The cosmic serpent who coils around the mountains and withholds the waters of the world, lying 'like a cow with her calf' beside his mother Dānu. Indra slays him with the vajra, releasing the waters. This is the IE dragon-slaying myth in its oldest preserved form — structurally identical to Thor vs Jörmungandr, Baal vs Yam, Marduk vs Tiamat, YHWH vs Leviathan, Apollo vs Python, St George vs the Dragon, Trito vs the three-headed serpent in PIE reconstruction.

deities.earliest_attestation: Egyptian Execration Texts, Middle Kingdom c. 19th–18th c. BCE (reference to 'Iy-ʿanaq' enemies of pharaoh in Canaan); Hebrew Bible: Numbers 13, Deut 1–3, 9, Joshua 11, 15
primary_sources: Papyrus Anastasi I (13th c. BCE, mentions Canaanite giants on travel routes)
1 Chronicles 20 (Goliath and his brothers as descendants)
deities.etymology: Hebrew ʿănāq / ʿănāqîm, possibly from a root meaning 'long-necked' or 'wearing a necklace/torque'. The Egyptian Iy-ʿanaq (with the ʿayin preserved) supports a real ethnonym, not just a Hebrew literary invention.
deities.summary: A giant warrior-clan of southern Canaan, attested independently in the Egyptian Execration Texts (where the three rulers of Iy-ʿanaq bear Semitic names) and in the biblical narrative. Numbers 13:33 explicitly identifies them as descendants of the Nephilim: 'And there we saw the Nephilim, the sons of Anak, who come from the Nephilim, and we were in our own sight as grasshoppers.' Deuteronomy 2:11 explicitly identifies them as a sub-group of the Rephaim. So in the biblical taxonomy: Nephilim (pre-flood) → Rephaim (post-flood giant complex) → Anakim (specific clan in southern Canaan) — with Og of Bashan as the last of the northern Rephaim and the Anakim/Goliath-line as the southern remnant. Caleb destroys the three sons of Anak — Sheshai, Ahiman, Talmai — at Hebron (Joshua 15:14). The Egyptian attestation as Iy-ʿanaq is the strongest piece of extra-biblical historical evidence for the giant-clan tradition.

primary_sources: Deuteronomy 3:11 ('his bedstead of iron, nine cubits long')
Talmud Niddah 61a (Og as son of Ahijah son of Shemhazai the fallen angel)
deities.etymology: Disputed; possibly related to Ugaritic ʿg, 'giant'
deities.summary: King of Bashan, slain by Israel under Moses. Described as the last of the Rephaim, with a bedstead nine cubits long (Deut 3:11 — approximately 14 feet by the standard cubit, the basis for tradition's claim of his giant stature). His kingdom in Bashan — the Golan, Mt Hermon region — overlaps exactly with the geography of the Ugaritic rpum cult. Rabbinic tradition (Talmud Niddah 61a) makes Og the grandson of Shemhazai the fallen Watcher, explicitly threading him into the Genesis 6 / Enochic lineage.

primary_sources: Genesis 15:20 (land promised to Abraham includes their territory)
deities.etymology: Hebrew rephāʾîm — dual sense: (1) 'giants / mighty ones,' deriving from a root rʾp suggesting strength/healing; (2) 'shades, the dead' in Sheol. The dual sense is inherited from Ugaritic rpum, which carries the same ambiguity.
deities.summary: Umbrella category for the giant-clans of Canaan, of whom Og is the last northern remnant and the Anakim the southern remnant. Each regional people had its own name: Moabites called them Emim, Ammonites called them Zamzummim, Israelites called them Rephaim. In poetic/wisdom literature the same word denotes the shades of the dead in Sheol — preserving the Ugaritic double sense in which the Rephaim are simultaneously giant warrior-ancestors AND the deified royal dead. Goliath and his brothers (1 Chr 20) are the last Philistine remnant of this complex.

alternate_names: the First Man and the Twin
primary_sources: Vedic: Manu and Yama (Yama as the first to die)
Norse: Mannus (in Tacitus's Germania) and Ymir (the giant whose body becomes the cosmos)
Iranian: Manu and Yima (Yima as first king and first mortal)
deities.etymology: *Manu = 'man' (cf. Skt Manu, Germanic Mannus, English 'man'); *Yemo = 'twin' (cf. Skt Yama, Avestan Yima, Norse Ymir, Latin geminus)
deities.summary: Reconstructed PIE cosmogonic twins. *Manu, the first priest, sacrifices *Yemo, the first king (his twin), and from Yemo's dismembered body the cosmos and its social orders are made. This is the source of the universal IE pattern of cosmos-from-sacrificed-primordial-being — surviving in Vedic Puruṣa-sūkta (RV 10.90), Norse Ymir, Chinese Pangu (via possible Tocharian transmission), and the Orphic Dionysus-Zagreus. The twin who is sacrificed becomes the first king of the dead (Yama / Yima).

deities.earliest_attestation: Rigveda 10.90 (Puruṣa Sūkta) — one of the latest Rigvedic hymns
deities.etymology: Sanskrit puruṣa, 'man / cosmic person / soul'
deities.summary: The cosmic Person whom the gods sacrifice at the beginning of creation; from his dismembered body the worlds, the four varnas, and all the elements arise. RV 10.90 is the Rigvedic version of the PIE Yemo-sacrifice cosmogony — the most explicit, datable instance of it. In later Sāṃkhya philosophy Puruṣa becomes the pure consciousness opposed to Prakṛti (nature/matter), shifting from cosmogonic figure to metaphysical principle.

deities.earliest_attestation: Rigveda (one of the most central concepts of Vedic religion)
deities.etymology: From PIE *h₂er- 'to fit / join properly' — cognate with Avestan aša, Greek arariskō, Latin ars/artus, and conceptually with the Egyptian ma'at
iconography: the lawful course of the cosmos
deities.summary: Not a deity in the strict sense but the cosmic order itself — the truth-fittingness that holds the universe together. Varuna is its primary guardian. This concept is shared with Zoroastrian aša and Egyptian ma'at — three independent attestations of the same PIE / Afro-Asiatic concept of impersonal cosmic order against which the gods themselves are measured.

alternate_names: the Father in the Milky Way
deities.earliest_attestation: Oral tradition; rock-art continuity dating to at least 4,000 years before present
primary_sources: Worrorra, Ngarinyin, and Wunambal custodian teachings
deities.etymology: Worrorra / Ngarinyin / Wunambal (specific morphological analysis not given in publicly available sources)
deities.summary: The first Wandjina, who dwells in the Milky Way and is father of all Wandjinas. Withdrawn from direct world-making; sent his son Wallungunder to create the earth. Structurally occupies a tier-1 / sky-father position.

deities.etymology: Worrorra / Ngarinyin / Wunambal
deities.summary: Son of Idjair. Created the earth and all life on it. Created the first human beings — the Gyorn Gyorn (Gwion Gwion) people. Returned to Idjair to bring back more Wandjinas to give the Gyorn Gyorn people the Law. The active world-creator.

primary_sources: Worrorra custodian teachings; Mowanjum Art and Culture Centre
deities.summary: Primary Wandjina of the Worrorra people. Rain-maker, fertility-bringer, lawgiver. Repainted ceremonially to ensure the continuation of life and the rains.
Primary Wandjina of the Ngarinyin people.
Primary Wandjina of the Wunambal people, who refer to Wandjina generally as Gulingi.

deities.earliest_attestation: Oral tradition; widely-attested Rainbow-Serpent complex across Aboriginal Australia
deities.summary: The Kimberley reflex of the broader pan-Aboriginal Rainbow Serpent creator-being, working in the same cosmological space as the Wandjina but with different ritual and territorial associations. Linked to deep water-holes (which are Wunggurr-places).

primary_sources: Paiute oral tradition; Winnemucca, Life Among the Piutes (1883)
deities.summary: The Great Spirit of Paiute cosmology. In one creation narrative, Their Great Spirit creates an earlier race of 'beautiful giants' between the Sierra Nevada and the Rockies; when the giants mistreat a disfigured child, the Great Spirit makes the land hot and desolate and allows enemies to conquer them. Two giants survive — Paiute and his wife — from whom the people descend.

alternate_names: the red-haired giants of Lovelock Cave
deities.earliest_attestation: Paiute oral tradition; first written attestation Winnemucca (1883)
primary_sources: Sarah Winnemucca Hopkins, Life Among the Piutes (1883)
Adrienne Mayor, Fossil Legends of the First Americans (2005)
deities.etymology: Northern Paiute Si-Te-Cah, 'tule-eaters' (after the fibrous water plant they ate while living on rafts)
deities.summary: A hostile people the Paiute warred against and ultimately destroyed at Lovelock Cave (modern Pershing County, Nevada). Earlier accounts (Winnemucca; her brother Natchez) describe them as 'ordinary human beings' who happened to be enemies; later versions describe them as red-haired giants and cannibals. The 1911 Lovelock Cave excavations recovered 10,000+ artifacts and mummified remains under bat guano; tourist-display entrepreneurs amplified the giant claim, but subsequent academic study (Loud & Harrington 1929; UN-Las Vegas anthropology 1970s) found skeletons within the normal human size range. The red hair is now generally attributed to post-mortem pigment degradation. The tradition is included in this database because it is part of the global giants complex; the giant-versus-ordinary-humans dispute is part of its history and is preserved in the scholarly_caveat field.

deities.earliest_attestation: 1 Enoch 6:3 (4th–3rd c. BCE); 4Q201; later Manichaean Book of Giants
deities.etymology: Hebrew/Aramaic 'my name has seen' or 'he sees my name'
deities.summary: Leader of the 200 Watchers who descended on Mount Hermon. Father (with a human wife) of the giants Ohyah and Hahyah per Book of Giants 4Q530. Taught humans enchantments and root-cuttings (1 Enoch 8:3). In rabbinic tradition (Talmud Niddah 61a) his grandson is Og of Bashan, explicitly tying him to the post-flood giant complex.

deities.earliest_attestation: 1 Enoch 8:1; Leviticus 16:8-10 (older Yom Kippur scapegoat tradition)
deities.etymology: Hebrew, possibly 'goat that departs' or 'fierce god'; in 1 Enoch 'God-made'
deities.summary: In 1 Enoch 8:1: 'And Azazel taught men to make swords, and knives, and shields, and breastplates, and made known to them the metals of the earth and the art of working them, and bracelets, and ornaments, and the use of antimony, and the beautifying of the eyelids.' He is the corrupter-by-technology, complement to Semyaza the corrupter-by-intercourse. In 1 Enoch 10:4-8 the angel Raphael binds him hand and foot in the desert place Dudael until the final judgment — the prototype of the cosmic-rebel-bound-in-pit pattern. Later Islamic tradition identifies him with Iblis.

deities.etymology: Hebrew/Aramaic, possibly 'earth of God'
deities.summary: Watcher chief; taught the signs of the earth (1 Enoch 8:3).

deities.etymology: Hebrew kokab + el, 'star of God'
deities.summary: Watcher chief; taught the signs of the stars (1 Enoch 8:3) — the prototype of forbidden astrology.

deities.etymology: Hebrew/Aramaic baraq + el, 'lightning of God'
deities.summary: Watcher chief; taught astrology / the signs of the lightnings. In the Book of Giants he is the father of the giant Mahaway (4Q530 frag 7); 'Baraqel, my father, was with me' (4Q203 frag 8).

deities.etymology: Hebrew/Aramaic, possibly 'cursed one' or 'arms of God'
deities.summary: Watcher chief; taught the resolving of enchantments / undoing of spells (1 Enoch 8:3).

deities.etymology: Hebrew sar + el, 'prince of God'
deities.summary: Watcher chief; taught the courses of the moon (1 Enoch 8:3).

deities.etymology: Hebrew shemesh + el, 'sun of God'
deities.summary: Watcher chief; taught the signs of the sun (1 Enoch 8:3).

deities.primary_name: The Other Watcher Chiefs (Râmêal, Dânal, Êzêqêal, Tâmîal, Râmîal, Batârêal, Anânêal, Zaqîêal, Satarêal, Tûrêal, Jômjâal)
deities.summary: The remaining named chiefs of tens from Semyaza's twenty. Each led ten subordinate Watchers (200 total in the descent). Specific named teachings are recorded for some (e.g. Ezekeel taught the knowledge of the clouds; Sariel taught the courses of the moon — see 1 Enoch 8:3); for others, the 1 Enoch text lists only their names and rank.

deities.earliest_attestation: Dead Sea Scrolls Book of Giants (4Q530, 4Q531, 4Q203), c. 2nd–1st c. BCE
primary_sources: Book of Giants 4Q530 frag 2 col ii (Ohyah's throne-room dream)
Book of Giants 4Q531 (Ohyah speaks to Gilgamesh)
deities.etymology: Aramaic; widely interpreted as ad hoc wordplay (possibly 'living one')
deities.summary: One of the two prominent giant brothers in the Book of Giants. Son of Shemhazai (Semyaza), the chief Watcher. Receives a throne-room dream (4Q530 col ii lines 15-20) that parallels Daniel 7 closely: 'The Ruler of the heavens came down to earth, and thrones were erected and the Great Holy One sat down.' Speaks with Gilgamesh in 4Q531. In the Manichaean version he is renamed Sām and fights the sea-monster Leviathan.

deities.earliest_attestation: Book of Giants 4Q530, c. 2nd–1st c. BCE
deities.etymology: Aramaic; like Ohyah, widely interpreted as ad hoc wordplay
deities.summary: Twin brother of Ohyah, son of Shemhazai. Receives the complementary dream in 4Q530 col ii lines 6-12: a large garden tended by gardeners, destroyed by fire and water, with one tree of three branches preserved — interpreted as the destruction of the giants and the preservation of Noah and his three sons.

deities.earliest_attestation: Book of Giants 4Q203, 4Q530, c. 2nd–1st c. BCE
deities.etymology: Disputed; possibly cognate with biblical Mehujael (Gen 4:18) or the Book of Moses Mahijah/Mahujah
deities.summary: Son of the Watcher Baraq'el (Baraqiel). The giants' messenger: after they cannot interpret the dreams of Ohyah and Hahyah, they send Mahaway to Enoch (who has been translated to heaven) for the interpretation. Mahaway flies on wings — the Manichaean version explicitly describes him as the winged envoy. Enoch gives him two tablets in response: one denouncing the giants, the second pronouncing judgment. Mahaway is structurally the only giant who can travel between earth and heaven — the messenger-figure who is also the only one with any chance of being heard.

deities.earliest_attestation: Book of Giants 4Q530, 4Q531, c. 2nd–1st c. BCE
primary_sources: Book of Giants 4Q530 col ii line 2; 4Q531 frag 22
deities.summary: The same Gilgamesh as the hero of the Babylonian epic — but appearing in the Book of Giants as one of the antediluvian giants opposed to Enoch and the loyal angels. This is 'the only mention of Gilgamesh outside the cuneiform literature' (J.T. Milik). The Book of Giants thus directly references and absorbs the Gilgamesh epic — including Humbaba, who appears as the giant Hobabish (Ḥobabish, from Humbaba). Concrete evidence of the cross-pollination between Mesopotamian heroic tradition and Second Temple Watcher tradition.

deities.etymology: Direct adaptation of Akkadian Humbaba, the cedar-forest monster slain by Gilgamesh and Enkidu
deities.summary: A named giant in the Book of Giants, whose name is the Aramaic adaptation of Humbaba — the cedar-forest guardian of the Gilgamesh epic. Milik first noted this identification. Confirms direct Mesopotamian influence on the Watcher-and-giants tradition.
A named giant in the Book of Giants; less narratively prominent than Ohyah, Hahyah, or Mahaway.

deities.earliest_attestation: 1 Samuel 17 (c. 10th c. BCE narrative, finalized later); 1 Chronicles 20
deities.etymology: Possibly Lydian or Anatolian; not Semitic
iconography: six cubits and a span tall (~9'9" by standard cubit)
deities.summary: The Philistine champion from Gath, slain by David's sling. 1 Chronicles 20:8 places him 'in the lineage of the giants' of Gath. The Septuagint and the Dead Sea Scrolls (4QSamᵃ) give his height as four cubits and a span (~6'9"), while the Masoretic Text gives six cubits and a span (~9'9"); this is one of the most-discussed manuscript variations in the Hebrew Bible. Goliath's brother Lahmi is named in 1 Chronicles 20:5 (also slain, by Elhanan).

alternate_names: the three sons of Anak at Hebron
deities.etymology: All three names show Hurrian rather than Semitic affinities (de Vaux 1948; Encyclopedia.com), complicating the question of whether the Anakim were ethnically distinct from the surrounding Semitic populations of Canaan.
deities.summary: The three named sons of Anak who inhabited Hebron. Driven out and killed by Caleb in Joshua 15:14. The fact that they are named (rather than counted as anonymous giants) gives them a specific historical-narrative weight; the Hurrian name-affinities are one of the most-discussed pieces of ethnographic detail in the Hebrew Bible.

deities.primary_name: Sippai, Saph, Lahmi, and the Six-Fingered Giant
alternate_names: the four giants of Gath slain by David's men
deities.summary: Four named Rephaim-descended Philistine warriors slain by David's men: Ishbi-benob (whose spear weighed 300 shekels of bronze; slain by Abishai), Saph/Sippai (slain by Sibbecai), Lahmi the brother of Goliath (slain by Elhanan), and the unnamed six-fingered, six-toed giant of Gath (slain by Jonathan). 1 Chronicles 20:8: 'These were descended from the Rephaim in Gath, and they fell by the hand of David and by the hand of his servants.' The last surviving Rephaim-line of the southern Levant after Joshua's earlier conquest.

alternate_names: the Mighty Hunter Before the Lord
primary_sources: Genesis 10:8-12 (the Table of Nations; founder of Babel, Erech, Akkad)
Rabbinic literature (extensive — as builder of the Tower of Babel, opponent of Abraham)
deities.etymology: Disputed; possibly from a root meaning 'rebel' (Hebrew mrd)
deities.summary: Great-grandson of Noah through Cush. Founder of Babel, Erech (Uruk), and Akkad — the Mesopotamian power-center. Identified in rabbinic tradition as the builder of the Tower of Babel and the king who threw Abraham into a fiery furnace. Some traditions identify him as a giant or as the first post-flood tyrant — the 'first mighty man on earth' (Gen 10:8). The structural parallel of the Tower-of-Babel narrative to the Quinametzin Aztec 'aspired to reach the gods' giants and to the Greek Titanomachy (Otus and Ephialtes piling Pelion on Ossa) makes him a key cross-tradition node.

alternate_names: the giants of the previous Sun
deities.earliest_attestation: Aztec / Nahua oral tradition; Vatican Codex A (Ríos Codex); Spanish chronicler accounts (16th c. CE)
primary_sources: Bernardino de Sahagún, Florentine Codex (esp. Book 12)
Diego Durán, Historia de las Indias
Fernando de Alva Ixtlilxochitl, Historia de la Nación Chichimeca
deities.etymology: Nahuatl quinametli + plural -tzin, 'giant people' (honorific plural)
deities.summary: Giants of the previous Sun-era (the sources disagree on which: Sun of Earth / Sun of Rain / Sun of Water). According to Aztec accounts they built the Great Pyramid of Cholula (by Xelhua, a 20-foot giant) and the city of Teotihuacan. They were destroyed by the gods through flood, earthquake, jaguars, hurricane, or collapse of the sky — sources vary — because of their sins, which include aspiring to reach the gods (the Tower-of-Babel motif), sins of the flesh, and drunkenness. Their bones, kept in Mictlan, are the material from which Ehecatl makes present-day humanity. Four giants — Cuauhtemoc, Izcoalt, Izcaqlli, Tenexuche — support the sky at the beginning of the Fifth (current) Sun. The Quinametzin parallel to the Nephilim is so close that Spanish chroniclers immediately identified them with the biblical giants and assimilated the two traditions.

deities.earliest_attestation: Aztec / Nahua oral tradition; Spanish chronicler accounts
deities.summary: Quinametzin giant credited with building the Great Pyramid of Cholula (the largest pyramid in the world by volume). Founder of seven pre-Columbian cities including Cuauhquechollan, Itzocan, Epatlán, Teopantlán, Tehuacán, Cuzcatlán, and Teotitlán. Survived the destruction of the previous Sun.

Historical Otomi warrior from Mexico-Tlatelolco who repelled Pedro de Alvarado's Spanish forces during the siege of Tenochtitlan by throwing rocks 'the size of watermelons.' Eyewitness accounts described him as over 10 feet tall. The rare case of a giant tradition with a near-contemporary, named, datable individual.

deities.etymology: Greek Titanes; etymology disputed (possibly 'lords' or 'strainers' from Hesiod's pun)
deities.summary: The twelve elder gods, children of Uranus (Sky) and Gaia (Earth): Oceanus, Coeus, Crius, Hyperion, Iapetus, Theia, Rhea, Themis, Mnemosyne, Phoebe, Tethys, and Kronos. They rule the world before being overthrown by the Olympians in the Titanomachy. The defeated Titans are imprisoned in Tartarus. Structural parallel to the Vedic asuras, the Irish Fomorians, the biblical Nephilim/Rephaim, the Aztec Quinametzin, and the Norse Jötnar.

deities.etymology: Greek; folk-etymology with Chronos ('time') developed later
deities.summary: Youngest Titan, son of Uranus and Gaia. Castrates his father with a sickle, becomes king of the Titans. Devours his own children to prevent the prophecy of his overthrow; tricked by Rhea who substitutes a stone for the infant Zeus. Eventually overthrown by Zeus. The grandfather-castrated-by-son-castrated-by-grandson motif is the Greek version of the IE succession-by-violence cosmogonic pattern.

deities.etymology: Possibly cognate with biblical Japheth (a longstanding speculation; the linguistic connection is debated but the parallelism of position is striking)
deities.summary: Titan, son of Uranus and Gaia. Father of Prometheus, Epimetheus, Atlas, and Menoetius. The Iapetus-Japheth parallel is one of the oldest comparative-mythology observations (going back to early Christian apologists); the linguistic relationship remains contested but the structural role of 'progenitor of a key human-related lineage' aligns.

deities.etymology: Greek a-tla, 'one who endures / bears'
iconography: holding the heavens on his shoulders
deities.summary: Titan, son of Iapetus, condemned by Zeus to hold up the sky for eternity after the Titanomachy. Structurally parallel to the four Quinametzin sky-bearers (Cuauhtemoc, Izcoalt, Izcaqlli, Tenexuche) of Aztec cosmology, and to the four directional Lokapalas of Hindu cosmology.

deities.etymology: Greek; possibly 'flowing one'
deities.summary: Titan, sister-consort of Kronos. Mother of the elder Olympians (Hestia, Demeter, Hera, Hades, Poseidon, Zeus). Saves the infant Zeus by substituting a stone for him. The mother of the divine-succession line.

deities.earliest_attestation: Old Norse poetic and prose Edda, 13th c. CE manuscripts preserving older oral material
deities.etymology: Old Norse jötunn, 'devourer / great eater'
deities.summary: The elder, larger race against which the Æsir define themselves. They predate the gods (Ymir, the primordial jötunn, is the first being). They live in Jötunheim. The Æsir are themselves descended from jötnar through Bestla (mother of Odin). At Ragnarök the jötnar, led by Surtr, destroy the world. Structural parallel to the Titans, Fomorians, Quinametzin, Nephilim/Rephaim, Daityas, Danavas.

deities.earliest_attestation: Eddic material; closely cognate with PIE *Yemo
deities.etymology: Old Norse Ymir, cognate with PIE *Yemo 'twin' (Vedic Yama, Avestan Yima)
deities.summary: First being, born from the meeting of fire and ice in Ginnungagap. Killed by Odin, Vili, and Vé; from his body the world is made (flesh = earth, blood = sea, skull = sky, brains = clouds, eyebrows = Midgard). The Norse direct reflex of PIE *Yemo, the sacrificed twin from whose body the cosmos is constructed. The Vedic Puruṣa Sūkta (RV 10.90) is the closest mythological parallel.

deities.etymology: Old Norse 'the black one' or 'the swarthy one'
deities.summary: Lord of Muspelheim (the fire-realm). At Ragnarök he leads the jötnar across Bifrost (which breaks under their weight) and burns the world with his flaming sword. The cosmic-fire-ender. Structural parallel to Kalki (Vedic eschatological horseman who ends the Kali Yuga) and Christ-at-second-coming.

Jötunn-mother of Odin, Vili, and Vé. Her presence means the Æsir themselves are half-jötunn by maternal line — the Norse equivalent of the Bnei-Elohim / Daughters-of-Men mixing of divine and primordial bloodlines.
Jötunn-king who steals Thor's hammer Mjölnir and demands Freyja as bride; tricked by Thor (disguised as Freyja) and killed.
Jötunn-woman who married into the Æsir (briefly, to Njörðr). Demonstrates the porous boundary between jötnar and gods.
The primordial Buddha — the self-originated, beginningless awareness from which all manifestation arises. Not a creator but the ground of all awakening.
deities.etymology: Sanskrit ādi 'primordial' + buddha 'awakened'

deities.summary: The Buddhist adversary — lord of the desire-realm and tempter of the Buddha. Sent armies and daughters to prevent the Buddha's enlightenment.
Bodhisattva of compassion who hears all cries of suffering. Voluntarily delays complete nirvana. In East Asia becomes the feminine Guanyin.
The future Buddha — currently dwelling in Tushita heaven, will descend at the end of the present dharmic decline to re-establish the dharma.
deities.etymology: Sanskrit maitrī 'loving-kindness'

deities.summary: Buddhist female savior — born of Avalokitesvara's tears. Twenty-one manifestations. Mother of all Buddhas. The feminine principle of swift compassionate action.
Boundless Time — the impersonal principle above both Ahura Mazda and Angra Mainyu in the Zurvanite heresy. Father of the twin spirits.
primary_sources: Eznik of Kolb's account of Zurvanism
deities.etymology: Avestan zruvan 'time'

deities.summary: The supreme Wise Lord — uncreated creator of all good, light, and truth. Source of the six Amesha Spentas. Locked in cosmic dualism with Angra Mainyu.
deities.etymology: Avestan ahura 'lord' + mazdā 'wise'

deities.summary: The Destructive Spirit — cosmic adversary of Ahura Mazda, source of all evil, darkness, death, and the Lie. Will be defeated at the Frashegird.
deities.etymology: Avestan angra 'destructive' + mainyu 'spirit'

deities.summary: The future world-savior — born of a virgin from Zarathustra's preserved seed. Will defeat Angra Mainyu, resurrect the dead, and complete the Frashegird (cosmic renovation).
deities.etymology: Avestan 'one who brings benefit'

deities.summary: Sovereign of the celestial bureaucracy — rules the cosmos like an emperor with ministers and secretaries. Below the Three Pure Ones but highest personal divine authority.
alternate_names: Yu Huang Dadi 玉皇大帝

deities.earliest_attestation: Traditionally 6th c. BCE; text c. 4th c. BCE
deities.summary: Traditional author of the Tao Te Ching; deified as Daode Tianzun, one of the Three Pure Ones — a recurring descent of cosmic principle into human form.
The Chinese creator goddess who fashioned humanity from yellow earth and repaired the broken sky with five-colored stones.
The supreme being of Yoruba theology — transcendent, unknowable, beyond direct prayer or sacrifice. Delegates creation and governance entirely to the orishas.
primary_sources: Samuel Johnson's History of the Yorubas (1921)
deities.etymology: Yoruba: 'owner of the source of unending greatness'

deities.summary: Senior orisha — delegated by Olodumare to shape human bodies from clay. When he drinks palm wine and works drunk, he creates bodies with disabilities, who become his most sacred devotees.
The divine trickster and messenger — guardian of crossroads and gateways. Must be propitiated first in any ceremony. Controls access to all other orishas.
Orisha of fresh water, love, beauty, fertility. Saved the world when the other orishas forgot to include her — rivers dried, women became infertile — until Olodumare intervened.
Supreme creator who emerged from Lake Titicaca, made the sun, moon, and stars, shaped humanity, walked the earth as a wandering teacher, then departed across the Pacific.
The living earth mother of Andean cosmology — not a deity above the earth but the earth itself as a living sacred being. Still actively venerated.
God of the sea and (in Samoan tradition) supreme creator. In Maori tradition, ruler of the ocean and father of fish, in perpetual conflict with Tane.
The great trickster demigod — fished up islands, slowed the sun, stole fire, attempted to win immortality for humanity by entering the body of the goddess of death, dying in the attempt.
The Hawaiian goddess of volcanoes — creates new land through lava flows and destroys old land through the same force. Creation and destruction are one act.
The supreme god of the Slavic pantheon — god of thunder, oak trees, and the sky. Battles the chthonic serpent Veles in an eternal cosmic conflict. Direct reflex of PIE *Perkʷunos.
deities.etymology: Direct reflex of PIE *Perkʷunos

deities.summary: The Slavic god of the underworld, cattle, magic, and wealth — the great serpentine adversary of Perun. Also patron of cattle, commerce, music, and poetry.
The only female deity explicitly mentioned in the Primary Chronicle. Goddess of fertility, weaving, fate, and moisture. Spins the thread of fate at night.
primary_sources: Rybakov's Paganism of the Ancient Slavs (1981)
deities.etymology: Proto-Slavic *mok- (wet, moist)

deities.summary: The supreme solar goddess of Shinto — born from Izanagi's left eye. Withdrew into the Heavenly Rock Cave plunging the world into darkness; lured out by the gods' laughter. Ancestor of the imperial dynasty.
The storm god born from Izanagi's nose — wild, weeping, destructive, then heroic. Banished from heaven, slew the eight-headed serpent Yamata no Orochi by getting it drunk.
Female creator-kami who with Izanagi created the Japanese islands. Died birthing the fire-kami; became queen of Yomi (the underworld). Vows to kill a thousand people per day.

deities.earliest_attestation: oral; written c. 1100 CE (Lebor Gabála Érenn)
deities.summary: Chief god of the Irish Tuatha Dé Danann — a colossal father-god with a magic cauldron that never empties and a club that kills with one end and resurrects with the other.
deities.etymology: Irish: 'the good god' (good at everything)

deities.earliest_attestation: oral; Gaulish inscriptions c. 1st c. BCE
deities.summary: The pan-Celtic sun god of skill — master of all arts and crafts. Led the Tuatha Dé Danann to victory. The harvest festival Lughnasadh is named for him.
The great Irish goddess of war, fate, and sovereignty — appears as a crow on the battlefield, prophesies death, tests heroes. Triple goddess embodying the full spectrum of female divine power.

The antlered god of animals, fertility, the underworld, and liminal spaces. Sits cross-legged between worlds holding a torc and a serpent. Most widespread Gaulish deity whose mythology is almost entirely lost.
First king of Egypt, civilizer, murdered by Set, dismembered and scattered, reassembled by Isis. Became lord of the Duat and judge of the dead. The archetypal dying-and-rising-king.
Sister-wife of Osiris, mother of Horus. Recovered and reassembled Osiris. Queen of Heaven. Savior-goddess whose Roman-era cult spread across the Mediterranean.

God of writing, wisdom, the moon, and magic. Scribe of the gods. Reckoner of time. His Greek interpretatio is Hermes; the Hermetica are attributed to Hermes Trismegistus — Thoth-Hermes Thrice-Greatest.
Personification of cosmic order, truth, justice, and right-ordering. The heart of the deceased is weighed against her feather at judgment. Cognate with Vedic rta and Iranian asha.
Thunder-god. Wields Mjölnir. Protector of Asgard and Midgard. At Ragnarök will fight Jörmungandr — they will kill each other. Son of Odin and Jörd (Earth). Germanic preservation of the PIE thunderer.
Daughter of Loki. Queen of Hel — the realm of those who die of illness or old age (not in battle). Half-alive, half-dead. The Norse underworld-queen.
Cosmic world-serpent, son of Loki. Cast into the ocean encircling Midgard. At Ragnarök, he and Thor kill each other.

The one God of Islam — utterly transcendent, without partners or offspring. Possesses 99 beautiful names (al-Asma al-Husna) each describing a divine attribute. Neither born nor begets (Quran 112). Creator of all from nothing (ex nihilo). Speaks through the Quran via the angel Jibreel to Muhammad.
deities.etymology: Arabic: possibly from al-ilāh "the god"

deities.summary: The Islamic adversary. Once a jinn (fire-being) or angel of high station who refused to prostrate before Adam when commanded by Allah — arguing that fire is superior to clay. Cast out of paradise, given respite until the Day of Judgment to lead humanity astray. Not a co-equal adversary (no Islamic dualism) but a rebellious created being.
primary_sources: Hadith on Shaytan and his waswasa (whispering)
deities.etymology: Arabic: possibly from Greek diabolos or from Arabic ablasa "to despair"

deities.summary: The expected guided one — a messianic figure who will appear before the Day of Judgment to restore justice and equity to a world filled with injustice and oppression. Sunni tradition: a future figure from the family of the Prophet. Shia tradition: the Hidden Imam who has been in occultation since 874 CE and will return.
primary_sources: Various hadith (Sunan Abu Dawud, Sunan Ibn Majah)

deities.summary: The mysterious green/immortal guide who appears to Moses in Quran 18. Performs three acts that appear immoral but are revealed to have divine wisdom. Identified in hadith as al-Khidr (the Green One) — a figure granted immortality who wanders the earth as a guide to prophets and mystics. In Sufi tradition, the supreme initiating master who teaches outside normal human chains.
deities.etymology: Arabic: possibly "the green one" — from khudra (greenness)

deities.summary: The Perfect Man — Ibn Arabi's concept of the fully realized human being as the mirror of all divine attributes. The cosmos was created for the sake of the Perfect Man. Muhammad is the paradigmatic Perfect Man. In Sufi cosmology, the Perfect Man is the isthmus (barzakh) between the divine and the created — the cosmic axis.

Jesus in Islamic theology — born of a virgin (Maryam), performed miracles, the "Word of God" (Kalimat Allah) and "Spirit from Him" (Ruh minhu). Not crucified (God raised him up) — a substitute was crucified. Will return before the Day of Judgment to break the cross and kill Dajjal (the Antichrist). A great prophet but not divine.
primary_sources: Hadith on the return of Jesus
deities.etymology: Arabic Isa from Syriac Isho from Hebrew Yeshua

deities.earliest_attestation: Hadith (major signs of the Last Hour)
deities.summary: The Islamic Antichrist — the False Messiah who will appear before the Day of Judgment, performing miracles and leading masses astray. One-eyed, the word kafir (unbeliever) written on his forehead. Will be killed by Jesus at his return.
primary_sources: Ibn Kathir, al-Nihaya fi al-Fitan wa al-Malahim

deities.summary: The Breath of the Compassionate — Ibn Arabi's concept of the divine exhalation through which all creation comes into being. God's sigh of longing to be known generates the cosmos as the unfolding of His own attributes.

The Virgin Mary as Theotokos (God-bearer) — Mother of God in Catholic and Orthodox theology. Immaculately conceived (Catholic dogma 1854), bodily assumed into heaven (Catholic dogma 1950). Co-redemptrix in some Catholic theology. Queen of Heaven. Mediatrix of all graces. Her iconography directly continues Isis-Queen-of-Heaven and possibly Astarte/Asherah.

The highest angel in Hekhalot mysticism — the transformed Enoch who sits on a lesser throne beside God's throne, serves as the heavenly scribe, Prince of the Divine Presence, and the "lesser YHWH" (3 Enoch 12). His transformation from human to divine being is the most developed apotheosis narrative in Jewish tradition.

deities.earliest_attestation: c. 360 CE (Council of Constantinople); Solovyov/Bulgakov 19th-20th c.
deities.summary: Divine Wisdom (Sophia) as a hypostasis in Eastern Orthodox theology. The Hagia Sophia (Holy Wisdom) of Constantinople is named for her. Russian Orthodox Sophiology (Solovyov, Bulgakov, Florensky) developed Sophia as the divine feminine principle, the world-soul, and the bridge between Creator and creation.
primary_sources: Vladimir Solovyov, Lectures on Divine Humanity
Sergei Bulgakov, The Wisdom of God

deities.summary: The God of Latter-day Saint theology — an exalted, embodied, corporeal being (flesh and bones as tangible as man's). Was once as humans are; humans may become as He is (Lorenzo Snow couplet). Father of all human spirits in a pre-mortal existence. Has a Heavenly Mother as eternal consort.
primary_sources: Pearl of Great Price (King Follett Discourse 1844)

deities.earliest_attestation: Eliza R. Snow hymn 1845; formal doctrine underdeveloped
deities.summary: The divine feminine in LDS theology — Heavenly Father has an eternal consort, the Mother of all human spirits. Deliberately kept secret by God to protect her from blasphemy. One of the most theologically underdeveloped yet structurally significant divine feminine figures in modern religion.
primary_sources: Teachings of the Prophet Joseph Smith
The Family: A Proclamation to the World (1995) (implied)

deities.summary: Meister Eckhart's distinction between "God" (Gott — personal, relational, creating) and "the Godhead" (Gottheit — the silent abyss beyond all attributes, beyond being itself). The Godhead is the desert beyond God, the silence before the Word. The soul in its ground (Seelenfünklein — the spark of the soul) is identical with the Godhead.

The 24th and final Tirthankara (ford-maker) of the present cosmic age. Attained kevala (omniscience) and moksha. Jain theology has no creator god — the universe is eternal and self-regulating. Liberated souls (Siddhas) occupy the apex of the cosmos but do not intervene in worldly affairs.

The liberated souls at the apex of the Jain cosmos (Siddhashila — the abode of the liberated). Infinite, omniscient, formless, and perfectly still — they have no further interaction with the world. The Jain equivalent of a divine realm — but one populated entirely by formerly human souls.

The Sikh name for God — the Wondrous Enlightener. Formless (Nirankar), without attributes in one sense, yet personal and relational. Ik Onkar ("One Creator") is the foundational theological statement. Waheguru is simultaneously the transcendent absolute and immanent in all creation through the concept of Hukam (divine will/order).
primary_sources: Adi Granth / Guru Granth Sahib (entire text)
deities.etymology: Punjabi: Wahe (wondrous/exclamation of awe) + Guru (enlightener)

deities.summary: The eternal living Guru of the Sikhs — not a book but the living Word of God.
