Guru Gobind Singh conferred the Guruship permanently on the scripture in 1708 CE, ending the line of human Gurus. Treated with full divine honors: installed on a throne, fanned with a chauri, put to rest each night.
primary_sources: Bhai Nand Lal on the transfer of Guruship

deities.summary: Sky father and head of the Sumerian pantheon — the primordial sky whose name means "heaven/sky." Father of the Anunnaki. Increasingly withdrawn in later mythology as Enlil and then Marduk take active leadership. The Anunna (heavenly ones) are his family. Symbol: the horned crown.
primary_sources: Anu's descent from An to Anu in Akkadian period
deities.etymology: Sumerian An = sky/heaven

deities.summary: Lord of Wind and Atmosphere — the active sovereign of the Mesopotamian pantheon through most of the third and second millennia. Rules from Nippur, city of the divine assembly. Holder of the Tablets of Destiny. Sends the Flood because human noise disturbs him (Atrahasis). Eventually displaced by Marduk.
deities.etymology: Sumerian En (lord) + Lil (wind/air/breath)

deities.summary: Lord of the abzu (freshwater deep), wisdom, magic, and civilization. The great friend of humanity in the Mesopotamian pantheon. Warns Utnapishtim of the Flood (subverting Enlil's decree). Gives humanity the me (divine civilizational powers). Father of Marduk. Creator of humans with Ninhursag.
deities.etymology: Sumerian En (lord) + Ki (earth) or Ku (sacred)

deities.summary: The great earth mother of Mesopotamian mythology. Lady of the Sacred Mountain (Ninhursag) or Lady Great (Ninmah). With Enki, fashions humanity. Mother of the gods. One of the four primary Sumerian deities (An, Enlil, Enki, Ninhursag).

Queen of the Great Below — ruler of the Mesopotamian underworld (Kur/Irkalla). Sister of Inanna who descended to challenge her and was killed and hung on a peg. Seized by Nergal who became her consort. Her grief fills the underworld with wailing.
primary_sources: Descent of Ishtar to the Underworld
iconography: the peg on which she hung Inanna

deities.summary: God of the underworld, plague, and war. Descended to the underworld, overcame Ereshkigal, and became her co-ruler. Associated with the summer sun (the killing heat), war, pestilence, and destruction.

Sun god of justice and truth. His light reveals what is hidden. The divine judge — laws are given under his authority (Hammurabi's Code was given by Shamash). Travels through the underworld each night, giving light to the dead.
primary_sources: Code of Hammurabi stele (the king receives laws from Shamash)
iconography: foot emerging from mountain at dawn

deities.summary: The primordial salt-water ocean — first mother of all the gods. With Apsu (fresh water), generates the first divine generation. After Apsu is slain by Ea/Enki, she raises an army of monsters (11 monster-beings led by Kingu). Slain by Marduk, whose cosmos is built from her body.
iconography: split body becoming sky and earth

deities.summary: The primordial fresh water — consort of Tiamat. From their mingling all the gods arise. Slain by Ea/Enki when he plots to destroy the noisy younger gods.

2/3 divine, 1/3 human king of Uruk. Seeks immortality after witnessing the death of his friend Enkidu. Reaches the edge of the world but fails to obtain the plant of immortality. The first great hero narrative in recorded literature — the quest for immortality as humanity's defining project.
primary_sources: Standard Babylonian Epic of Gilgamesh (12 tablets)
Book of Giants 4Q531 (appears as a giant)

deities.summary: The supreme solar deity — creator, king of the gods, and sustainer of the cosmos. His solar barque travels across the sky each day and through the underworld (Duat) each night, where he confronts Apophis. Merged with Amun as Amun-Ra in the New Kingdom to become the supreme state deity.
deities.etymology: Egyptian rꜥ — possibly meaning "sun" or related to "mouth"

deities.summary: The creator god of Memphis — created the cosmos by thought in his heart and speech on his tongue. The first documented theology of creation by the Word (logos). Patron of craftsmen, architects, and artists. In the Memphite theology (c. 700 BCE compilation), Ptah's heart = Atum, his tongue = Horus — all gods are aspects of Ptah's creative thought and speech.
primary_sources: Shabaka Stone (Memphite Theology, c. 700 BCE, from earlier source)

deities.summary: The falcon sky god — son of Osiris and Isis, avenger of his father, rightful king of Egypt. Each Pharaoh was the living Horus; each dead Pharaoh became Osiris. Multiple Horus forms: Horus the Elder (predynastic sky god), Horus the Child/Harpocrates (infant with finger to lips), Horus of Edfu (great winged sun disc).

The lioness goddess of war, plague, and the destructive power of the sun. Eye of Ra sent to destroy disobedient humanity — nearly exterminates them. Pacified by beer dyed red (mistaken for blood), transformed into the gentler Hathor.
primary_sources: medical texts (Sekhmet as plague and cure)

deities.summary: Goddess of love, beauty, music, motherhood, and joy. The pacified form of Sekhmet. Lady of the West (receives the dead). Lady of the sycamore (feeds the dead). In her Dendera form (Hathor of Dendera), one of the most important cult centers of ancient Egypt.

The sky goddess — her star-spangled body arches over the earth (Geb) as the visible sky. Swallows Ra (the sun) each evening and gives birth to him at dawn. Mother of Osiris, Isis, Set, Nephthys, and Horus the Elder. The inside of coffin lids shows Nut arching over the deceased.
The earth god — brother-husband of Nut. The earth is his body; earthquakes are his laughter. Father of Osiris, Isis, Set, and Nephthys. His goose is the cosmic goose that laid the World Egg.

God of chaos, storms, the desert, and foreigners. Murderer of Osiris. Paradoxically, protector of Ra on the solar barque — only Set has the strength to fight Apophis nightly. The most theologically complex Egyptian deity — genuinely necessary evil.

Supreme creator deity and lord of the heavens, day, and night. Inventor of writing and books. His name may mean "Iguana House" — the cosmos as the body of a crocodilian. Father of the Bacabs (the four world-bearers). Husband of Ix Chel.

deities.earliest_attestation: Popol Vuh c. 1550 CE (from Classic antecedents)
deities.summary: The Hero Twins of the Mayan Popol Vuh — the sons of Hun Hunahpu (the Corn God). They descend to Xibalba (the underworld), defeat the Lords of Death through cunning, resurrect their father, and ascend to become the sun and moon. The central narrative of Mayan cosmology.
primary_sources: Popol Vuh (K'iche' Maya text, c. 1550 CE)
iconography: Classic vase depictions of ball game

deities.summary: The Maize God — father of the Hero Twins, killed by the Lords of Xibalba. His severed head placed in a gourd tree impregnates Xquic (Blood Moon) with the Hero Twins. Resurrected by his sons after their victory. The dying-and-rising corn god of Mesoamerica.

The rain deity — brings the life-giving rains essential to Mayan agriculture. Four aspects corresponding to the four cardinal directions. His tears are rain; his lightning axe cleaves the clouds. One of the most-represented deities in Mayan art.
Moon goddess, goddess of medicine, weaving, and childbirth. Wife of Itzamna. In her aged form, she pours water from a jar (floods). In her young form, she is the moon, weaving, and healing.

The 12 Lords of Death who rule the Mayan underworld — One Death (Hun Came) and Seven Death (Vucub Came) as their leaders, plus ten named subordinates governing specific torments. Defeated by the Hero Twins through cunning.

The most important figure in Navajo theology — the earth as a living being who changes through the seasons. Created from an abalone shell and turquoise by First Man and First Woman. Mother of the Hero Twins (Monster Slayer and Born-for-Water). Embodies the feminine principle of growth, renewal, and the seasons.
primary_sources: Navajo oral tradition (recorded by Washington Matthews, 1897)

deities.summary: The sacred figure who brought the Sacred Pipe to the Lakota people. Appeared as a beautiful woman, gave the pipe ceremony and the seven sacred rites, then transformed into a white buffalo calf and departed. She will return at the end of an age.

The Great Mystery — not a personal god but the totality of the sacred. All things contain Wakan Tanka; Wakan Tanka contains all things. Not anthropomorphic, not localized — the sacred power that pervades all existence.
primary_sources: Walker, Lakota Belief and Ritual (1914-1917 field notes)
deities.etymology: Lakota: Wakan = sacred/mysterious + Tanka = great

deities.summary: The great trickster-creator of the Northwest Coast peoples (Haida, Tlingit, Tsimshian). Stole the sun from the box in which it was kept, releasing light into the world. Stole fire, fresh water, and salmon. Transformed the world through cunning and appetite. Simultaneously creator, transformer, and insatiable trickster.

Fell (or was pushed) from the Sky World through a hole left by an uprooted tree. Fell onto the back of a great turtle, where animals dove to bring mud from the sea bottom. She danced the world into being on Turtle's back — Turtle Island (North America). Mother of the Good Twin (Sapling/Teharonhiawako) and the Evil Twin (Flint/Tawiskaron).
primary_sources: Haudenosaunee oral tradition recorded by various

deities.summary: The Inuit goddess of the sea and marine animals. Thrown from a kayak by her father (or husband), her fingers cut off as she clung to the gunwale — each finger joint became a different species of sea creature. She rules the undersea world and controls the availability of marine animals to hunters.

The Great Hare — trickster, culture hero, and transformer of the Anishinaabe people. Grandson of Nokomis (the Earth). Steals fire, teaches survival skills, recreates the world after the flood (earth-diver tradition).

The Supreme Being of the Akan people — the sky god who sends rain and sustains life. Name elements: Onyame (the one who satisfies), Onyankopon (the great one). Together with Asase Yaa (earth goddess), forms the Akan creator pair. Often seen as withdrawing after creation, with lesser spirits (obosom) as intermediaries.
primary_sources: Rattray, Religion and Art in Ashanti (1927)
deities.etymology: Akan: possibly "to satisfy" or "shining"

deities.summary: The spider trickster — keeper of all stories. Originally, the Sky God (Nyame) owned all stories. Anansi purchased them with three impossible tasks, and now all stories belong to Anansi. A trickster who wins through intelligence, not strength. Spread through the African diaspora to become the most widely known West African trickster.

The supreme creator of Dogon cosmology. Created the universe from an egg containing the seeds of all life. The first creation (Ogo/Nommo) went wrong — Ogo rebelled and became the pale fox (Vulpes pallida), source of disorder. Nommo (the divine twins, fish-human beings) were sacrificed and resurrected to restore order.
primary_sources: Griaule and Dieterlen, The Pale Fox (1965)

deities.summary: The divine twin fish-ancestors of Dogon cosmology — eight Nommo created by Amma, the most important being Nommo Titiyayne. One Nommo (Ogo's twin) was sacrificed, his body cut up and scattered to purify the earth, then resurrected. They are the models of perfect creation — dual, water-associated, civilizing.

The dual supreme creator of Fon cosmology — Mawu (feminine, moon, night, west, wisdom) and Lisa (masculine, sun, day, east, strength) are twins or a couple, two aspects of a single supreme being. Their union creates the cosmos. Together they are Da Aido Hwedo's controller.
alternate_names: Nana Buluku (their parent in some versions)

deities.summary: The first man and creator of the Zulu — literally "the greatest of the great." Emerged from reeds (or from Uhlanga, the source). Created people, cattle, and all things. In some accounts, a cultural ancestor rather than a sky god.
primary_sources: Callaway, The Religious System of the Amazulu (1870)
deities.etymology: Zulu: unkulu = great; doubled for superlative

deities.earliest_attestation: oral tradition; among oldest living traditions
deities.summary: The divine trickster-creator of the San (Bushmen) people. Takes the form of the praying mantis. Created the moon from an ostrich feather. A trickster who suffers, dies, and returns — one of the oldest documented trickster-creator figures.
primary_sources: Bleek and Lloyd, Specimens of Bushman Folklore (1911)

deities.earliest_attestation: Samguk Yusa, c. 1281 CE (older oral tradition)
deities.summary: The legendary founder of Gojoseon, the first Korean kingdom. Born of Hwanung (son of the heavenly god Hwanin) and a bear-woman (Ung-nyeo) who transformed into human form after 100 days in a cave eating garlic and mugwort. A divine founder who combines heavenly descent, earth-transformation, and human civilization.
deities.etymology: Dangun = "altar" + "lord"; Wanggeom = "lord of the people"

deities.summary: Son of Hwanin (lord of heaven), Hwanung descended to the summit of Mount Taebaek to rule. Brought 3,000 followers, three divine seals (wind, rain, cloud), and 360 areas of governance. Married the transformed bear-woman and fathered Dangun.
deities.etymology: Korean: possibly "heavenly master"

deities.earliest_attestation: oral tradition; texts c. 13th c. CE
deities.summary: The Eternal Blue Sky — supreme deity of the Mongols, Turks, and related Central Asian peoples. Not anthropomorphic — Tengri IS the sky. His will moves through everything. Genghis Khan received his mandate from Tengri. The oldest continuously attested sky-deity name in human record (Turkish inscriptions c. 550 CE).
primary_sources: Secret History of the Mongols (c. 1240 CE)
deities.etymology: Turkic-Mongolian: sky/heaven/god — possibly cognate with Chinese Tian (sky)

deities.summary: Lord of the underworld and adversary of Tengri — rules the lower world (Tamag) and the dead. Sends evil spirits to afflict the living. In some versions, first created alongside the shaman-hero figure but became ruler of darkness. The most developed sky/underworld dyad in Central Asian religion.

deities.earliest_attestation: oral tradition; reconstructed from 19th c. folklore
deities.summary: The Baltic sky god — the most linguistically pure preservation of the PIE sky-father *Dyeus. Dievas (Lithuanian) and Dievs (Latvian) = "sky/god" = cognate of Sanskrit Dyaus, Greek Zeus, Latin Deus. An old, wise farmer-king of heaven who works in the fields and tends horses.
deities.etymology: Baltic Dievas = sky/god = PIE *deywos (celestial, shining)

deities.summary: The Lithuanian thunder god — direct PIE *Perkwunos reflex, cognate of Slavic Perun, Vedic Parjanya. Battles the cosmic serpent Velnias (= Slavic Veles) who hides cattle under the earth. The best-preserved PIE storm-god vs. serpent dyad — the storm-god chases the serpent through forest, field, and water.
deities.etymology: Baltic Perkunas = PIE *perkwunos (the striker/oak-tree god)

deities.summary: The Latvian and Lithuanian fate goddess — she determines the fate of each human at birth. Her name is the word for "happiness/fate." She decrees whether a life will be fortunate or difficult. The cuckoo is her sacred bird (its call counts the years of one's life).
deities.etymology: Baltic: laima = happiness, fate

deities.summary: The Baltic sun goddess — feminine sun deity (rare in world religion). She weaves the fabric of cosmic time, rides a chariot across the sky, and mourns her daughters who are stolen by the Moon. The sun as feminine, weaving, and maternal is a distinctive Baltic contribution.
alternate_names: Saules meita (daughter of the sun — Latvian)
deities.etymology: Baltic: Saule = sun (cognate of Latin Sol)

deities.summary: The primordial shaman-singer and culture hero of the Kalevala. Old from the beginning — he was in the womb for 30 years before birth. Creates the world by singing. Forges the Sampo (the world-mill of abundance). Defeats death, retrieves the Sampo, and ultimately sails away in a copper boat to the edge of the world.

The Mistress of the North — ruler of Pohjola (Northland), the cold underworld-like realm in the Kalevala. Hoards the Sampo, steals the sun and moon. The great antagonist of the Kalevala heroes — powerful, cunning, and terrible. A genuine anti-divine figure in a tradition that otherwise lacks clear good/evil dualism.

The Finnish thunder god — "old man" (ukko = old man). Ruler of the sky, clouds, and rain. Strikes his fire-sword to create lightning. Direct cognate of Estonian Uku and possible distant cognate of PIE thunderer family.
deities.etymology: Finnish ukko = old man; also = sky/thunder god

deities.summary: The supreme deity of Basque mythology — the mountain goddess who lives in a cave on Mt. Anboto (and rotates through other peaks). She controls weather and storms, punishes liars, and rewards honesty. Married to Sugaar (the serpent). Pre-Indo-European Iberian survival — possibly the oldest preserved European goddess tradition.
iconography: woman wrapped in fire or tree

deities.summary: The serpentine consort of Mari — a masculine serpent deity who joins with Mari periodically to produce storms. Their meetings in mountain caves create hailstorms and tempests. A sky/earth — masculine serpent/feminine mountain divine couple that is pre-IE.

The Hurrian-Hittite storm god — supreme deity of the Hurrian pantheon, adopted into Hittite religion. Fights and defeats the cosmic serpent Illuyanka. His victory in the Kumarbi Cycle parallels Marduk vs. Tiamat and Zeus vs. Typhon — the Hittite link in the ancient Near Eastern storm-god tradition.

The displaced father-god who swallowed the sky-god Anu's genitals and conceived the storm-god Teshub and the monster Ullikummi. The Kumarbi Cycle is the direct literary ancestor of Hesiod's Theogony (Uranus → Cronus → Zeus). The most important text for tracing the Mesopotamian-to-Greek transmission of cosmogony.
primary_sources: Kumarbi Cycle (Song of Kumarbi, Song of Ullikummi)

deities.summary: The patron god of Tyre — whose name means "king of the city." Died each year and was resurrected through a sacred fire ceremony (egersis — awakening). The Phoenician dying-and-rising god. Identified with Herakles by the Greeks — Herakles' labors are partly Melqart's mythology.
deities.etymology: Phoenician: mlk qrt = king of the city

deities.summary: Chief goddess of Carthage — the face of Tanit (triangle with circle and horizontal bar) is the most recognizable symbol of Punic religion. Consort of Baal Hammon. Addressed as "Face of Baal." Associated with the moon, fertility, and the fires of Tophet (sacred precinct).
primary_sources: Greek and Roman sources on Punic religion

deities.summary: The absolute first principle of Neoplatonic philosophy — utterly simple, beyond being, beyond thought, beyond goodness. All existence emanates from the One through Nous (Intellect) and Psyche (Soul). The One does not know itself (knowing requires duality). The most philosophically rigorous articulation of the Absolute in Western tradition.

The Roman god of beginnings, doorways, transitions, time, and duality. Two-faced — one looking to the past, one to the future. January is named for him. Uniquely Roman — no Greek equivalent, no mythology imported. He is the moment of beginning itself.
deities.etymology: Latin: possibly from ire (to go) or ianua (door)

deities.summary: The Unconquered Sun — the imperial solar theology of late Rome. Introduced by Emperor Aurelian in 274 CE as a unifying solar monotheism. December 25th was Sol Invictus' birth day — later the date for Christmas. The direct theological predecessor of Constantine's Christianity.

The Orphic Dionysus — born as Zagreus from Zeus and Persephone, torn apart and eaten by the Titans, his heart rescued by Athena (or Rhea), reborn as the Olympian Dionysus. Humanity is born from the ashes of the Titans mixed with divine Zagreus substance — we contain within us both the Titanic (earthly, impure) and the Dionysiac (divine, pure). The Orphic theology of the soul as prisoner awaiting liberation.
The primordial Mahavidya — time, death, and liberation. Tongue out, girdle of severed arms, drinks blood from a skull. She IS time consuming all things.
The fierce blue goddess who saves from the ocean of worldly existence. Compassionate destroyer. Her roar destroys demons.
The most beautiful in the three worlds — the supreme goddess of Sri Vidya tradition. Governs the Sri Yantra, the geometric map of the cosmos. Union of all beauty, all knowledge, all bliss.
Lady of the Universe — her body IS the cosmos. Holds all worlds within her form. The spatial dimension of divine reality.
alternate_names: She Whose Body Is the World

deities.summary: The fierce one — the power that destroys ignorance, ego, and limitation. Terrifying and liberating simultaneously.

The self-decapitated goddess — she cuts off her own head with a sword, feeds her two attendants with the two streams of blood, and herself drinks the third stream. The paradox of self-sacrifice and self-nourishment.
The smoky widow — the goddess of poverty, misfortune, inauspiciousness, and the void. She represents what remains after destruction. Uniquely: she has no consort.
The goddess who paralyzes — holds the tongue of her enemy (symbolizing speech/thought) and stills all movement. The power of silence and stillness against all aggression.
The outcaste goddess — associated with pollution, waste, and the margins of society. She rules speech, music, and knowledge from the position of the excluded.

The lotus goddess in her tantric form — beauty, abundance, and prosperity arising from the cosmic waters. Identical with Lakshmi but placed last in the Mahavidyas as the most accessible, gentle aspect.

The supreme warrior goddess — the concentrated power (shakti) of all the gods when they were individually unable to defeat the buffalo-demon Mahishasura. She is not a consort but the autonomous power of the divine feminine. Ten arms, rides a lion.
iconography: ten arms each holding a weapon

deities.summary: The elephant-headed son of Shiva and Parvati — remover of obstacles, lord of beginnings, patron of wisdom and scribes. Invoked first before all undertakings. His large ears hear all prayers; his trunk removes all obstacles or creates them.

Shiva as the Lord of the Cosmic Dance — his dance (Ananda Tandava) simultaneously creates, sustains, and destroys the universe. Each gesture and posture encodes a complete cosmology. One of the most theologically dense images in world religion.
iconography: right foot on dwarf of ignorance

deities.summary: The illuminator — white, center, wheel symbol. Transforms ignorance into all-encompassing wisdom.
The immovable — dark blue, east, vajra symbol. Transforms hatred into mirror-like wisdom.
The jewel-born — yellow, south, jewel symbol. Transforms pride into equalizing wisdom.
The infinite light — red, west, lotus symbol. Transforms desire into discriminating wisdom. Lord of the Pure Land.
The infallible accomplisher — green, north, sword symbol. Transforms envy into all-accomplishing wisdom.
Bodhisattva of wisdom — wields the flaming sword that cuts through ignorance. The dharma of discriminating prajna (wisdom) personified. His sword destroys conceptual fabrications, not beings.

The bodhisattva who vowed not to achieve Buddhahood until all hell realms are empty. He descends to the hell realms to teach and liberate the suffering. In East Asia (Di Zang Pusa), the most beloved of the bodhisattvas in popular devotion.

Mother of the orishas and of the Yoruba people. Goddess of the Ogun River and of the sea in diaspora. Her name means "mother whose children are like fish." Nurturing, protective, and fierce when her children are threatened.
primary_sources: Diasporic traditions (Yemanja in Brazil, Yemaya in Cuba)

deities.summary: God of thunder, lightning, and royal power. The fourth Alaafin (king) of Oyo, deified after his death. His axe is the oshe, his color is red and white.
God of iron, war, hunting, and all who use iron tools. First to clear the path. The ambivalent power of civilization through destruction.
Goddess of storms, wind, death transitions, and the Niger River. Warrior goddess. She escorts the dead to the other world and controls the marketplace of life and death.
Orisha of disease, healing, and the earth. Causes and cures smallpox, AIDS in modern devotion. Both feared and revered — he punishes those who mock the sick.

The orisha of wisdom, knowledge, and Ifa divination. Witnessed creation and knows every person's destiny. Through the Ifa oracle (the most sophisticated African divination system), he reveals divine will.
primary_sources: Odu Ifa (256 Odu — the entire corpus is his wisdom)

deities.summary: The arch-orisha who shapes human bodies from clay. Lord of white cloth, purity, and all that is white. Father-figure among orishas. Arbitrates disputes.

The Queen Mother of the West — one of the most ancient and powerful figures in Chinese religion. She rules the Kunlun Mountains, presides over female immortals, and guards the peaches of immortality that ripen every 3,000 years. In the oldest texts she is terrifying and shamanistic; in later texts she is the gracious host of the divine banquet.
primary_sources: Classic of Mountains and Seas (Shanhai Jing)

deities.earliest_attestation: Historical person c. 160-220 CE; deified progressively
deities.summary: A historical general of the Three Kingdoms period — deified as God of War, righteousness, brotherhood, and loyalty. Worshipped across Chinese, Taoist, Buddhist, and folk traditions. His deification process is the most documented apotheosis in Chinese history.
primary_sources: Official Ming/Qing dynasty decrees of deification

deities.summary: Goddess of love, fertility, war, and death. Chief of the Vanir. Receives half of the battle-slain in Folkvangar (Odin takes the other half in Valhalla). Taught seidr (shamanic magic) to Odin. Cries tears of gold. Rides a chariot drawn by cats.

The most beautiful and beloved of the gods — whose death triggers the beginning of Ragnarok. Killed by the mistletoe dart guided by Loki and thrown by Hod. Every thing in the world swore not to harm him except the mistletoe. He will return after Ragnarok.
Queen of the Aesir, wife of Odin. Goddess of marriage, motherhood, and foreknowledge. She knows the fates of all beings but does not speak of them. Friday is named for her (Frigg's day).
God of the sea, earthquakes, and horses. Brother of Zeus, ruler of his own domain. Temperamental and powerful — his earthquakes reshape the world. His trident splits rocks and raises islands.

Goddess of the hunt, the moon, and the protection of young girls. Twin of Apollo. Virgin huntress in the wilderness. Lady of the beasts. Her anger is fierce — she sent the Calydonian Boar and punished Actaeon.
God of war in its bloodthirsty, brutal aspect. Distinguished from Athena (strategic war) — Ares is war as violence and terror. Disliked even by the gods. Lover of Aphrodite.

The divine craftsman — lame god of the forge, fire, and craftsmanship. Builds the palaces of the gods, the armor of heroes, and the chains of Prometheus. Thrown from Olympus (by Hera or Zeus in different versions).

One of the oldest and most important Mesoamerican deities — god of rain, earthly water, and agricultural fertility. His paradise (Tlalocan) is the destination of those who drown, die in rain, or are struck by lightning. Both giver of life through rain and taker of life through drowning.

The earth mother — "she of the serpent skirt." Mother of Huitzilopochtli (who she conceived from a ball of feathers). Wears a skirt of writhing serpents and a necklace of skulls, hands, and hearts. The devouring earth that both births and destroys.

Our Lord the Flayed One — god of agriculture, seasons, disease, and renewal. Priests wore the skins of sacrificed victims to symbolize the earth's new skin of vegetation. The dying-and-rising god of Aztec theology — he must be killed to generate life.
deities.etymology: Nahuatl: "our lord the flayed one"
iconography: flayed skin worn over the priest

deities.summary: The Sky Father — clasped in eternal embrace with Papatuanuku (Earth Mother). Their children, trapped between them in darkness, forced them apart to let in light. His tears (dew) fall on Papatuanuku still.
The Earth Mother — the great body on which all life rests. Separated from Ranginui by their children. Her sighs are the wind; her movements are earthquakes.

God of forests and birds — separated Ranginui and Papatuanuku by standing on his head and pushing them apart with his legs. Creator of the first human woman (Hineahuone) from red earth. Father of humanity.
God of the sea and fish — brother of Tane, ruler of the ocean domain. All sea creatures are his children. Major deity across all of Polynesia.

Goddess of volcanoes, fire, and creation through destruction. She creates new land through her eruptions. Fierce, passionate, and territorial — she will destroy what is in her path to create what must be.

The goddess of death and the underworld — originally Hine-titama (Dawn Maiden), she fled to the underworld when she discovered Tane was her father, becoming ruler of the dead. Maui was crushed by her obsidian teeth when he tried to gain immortality.
Supreme creator deity of Hawaii — god of sunlight, fresh water, and life. The most benevolent of the four great Hawaiian gods. Created the first man from red earth and his own image.
God of war, strength, and masculine power. One of the four great Hawaiian gods. Ku demands the most significant ritual attention — including human sacrifice in the heiau (temple) tradition.
God of agriculture, fertility, rainfall, and peace. One of the four great Hawaiian gods. Captain Cook was initially identified as Lono returning — a mistaken identification with tragic consequences.

The All-Father sky creator of southeastern Aboriginal peoples — Wiradjuri, Kamilaroi, and others. An active creator who fashioned the world and taught humans to hunt, gather, and live. He withdrew to the sky but maintains connection through the Milky Way.
primary_sources: Howitt, Native Tribes of SE Australia

deities.summary: The Wedge-tailed Eagle creator of Kulin peoples — he created the land, plants, animals, and humanity. He and his wives (two black swans) and son Binbeal (the rainbow) inhabit the sky.
primary_sources: Howitt, Native Tribes of SE Australia

deities.summary: The brother and two sisters who paddled from the spirit-land (Baralku) at the beginning. They planted sacred poles (rangga) as they walked the land, creating waterholes, plants, and the first human beings from their own bodies. The foundational creation narrative of Yolngu people.

The sky god of the Arrernte people — he created the world and then withdrew completely, having no further involvement. The paradigmatic deus otiosus (god who ceased). The Dreaming (Altyerre) is his creation but he no longer intervenes.
primary_sources: Spencer and Gillen, Native Tribes of Central Australia (1899)

deities.summary: The spider-web creator of Banks Islands (Vanuatu) — made people from wood, gave them life by dancing and drumming at them. Created night by trading with Night for his product. A true trickster-creator.
The Sun God of the Inca — supreme state deity. Father of the Inca royal line. The Coricancha (golden enclosure) in Cusco was his temple, lined entirely in gold. His wife is Mama Quilla (Moon Mother).
primary_sources: Garcilaso de la Vega, Royal Commentaries of the Incas
Sarmiento de Gamboa, History of the Incas

deities.summary: The Moon Mother — wife of Inti, mother of the Inca royal line alongside her husband. Governs the monthly calendar, women's cycles, and marriage. Her silver temple faced the golden temple of Inti.

Lord of the underworld (Uku Pacha — the world below). Originally the lord of the dead and underground — became associated with the Christian Devil after conquest but originally a neutral ruler of the dead.

Our Father — the supreme creator of the Guarani, who created the world by the power of his primordial wisdom (mbaraete) and word. The cosmos is sustained by his chant — when he stops chanting, the world ends.
alternate_names: Tupa (Portuguese colonial name for Guarani God)

deities.summary: The all-seeing sky sovereign of the Rigveda — his thousand eyes are the stars. He knows every human thought and deed. Commands the cosmic law (Rita). He and Mitra together uphold Rita as the Adityas.

The fire god — the most hymned deity in the Rigveda. Messenger between humans and gods, carrying sacrificial offerings upward through the smoke. He is in every fire — domestic hearth, funeral pyre, and sacrificial altar.
deities.etymology: Sanskrit: agni = fire (cognate of Latin ignis)

deities.summary: The storm king and dragon-slayer of the Rigveda — slays the cosmic dragon Vritra with his vajra (thunderbolt), releasing the cosmic waters. The most hymned deity in the Rigveda after Agni. Later dethroned by Vishnu and Shiva.

The divine plant and its pressed juice — a deity and a sacrament simultaneously. The drink of immortality (amrita) that gives the gods their power. One entire book of the Rigveda (Book 9) is devoted to Soma.

The male creator deity — with Izanami, stirred the primordial ocean with the Jeweled Spear of Heaven to create the islands of Japan. After Izanami died in childbirth and descended to Yomi, Izanagi followed to retrieve her, found her rotting, fled in horror, and performed ritual purification — from which Amaterasu, Tsukuyomi, and Susanoo were born.

The moon god — born from Izanagi's right eye during purification. He killed the food goddess Ukemochi when she produced food from her body in a way he found disgusting. Amaterasu was so horrified she refused to look at him — creating the separation of day and night.

deities.earliest_attestation: first shrine records c. 711 CE; deity much older
deities.summary: The most widely worshipped kami in Japan — guardian of rice, agriculture, industry, worldly success, and foxes. Over 30,000 shrines (more than any other kami). Depicted as male, female, or androgynous — as an old man, young woman, or fox.

The storm god — born from Izanagi's nose. Expelled from heaven for crying to see his mother Izanami. In exile, defeats the eight-headed serpent Yamata no Orochi by making it drunk, recovers the sword Kusanagi (later one of Japan's three imperial treasures), and becomes a benevolent culture hero.

deities.earliest_attestation: Song dynasty (c. 960-1279 CE) formal canonization; older folk roots
deities.summary: The supreme ruler of heaven and the celestial bureaucracy — oversees all gods, humans, and demons. He governs by cosmic law and delegates to a vast hierarchy of functionary deities. The Jade Emperor is the divine mirror of the Chinese imperial system.
primary_sources: Journey to the West (Wu Cheng'en, 16th c.)
Investiture of the Gods (Feng Shen Yanyi)
alternate_names: Jade August Supreme Emperor of Heaven

deities.summary: The Goddess of Compassion — the feminized Chinese transformation of Avalokitesvara. The most widely worshipped deity in East Asia. She hears the cries of the suffering world and responds with infinite compassion. Thousand-armed form encompasses all who call to her.
Creator goddess who fashioned humanity from yellow clay, repaired the sky after it collapsed, and established marriage. Half-human, half-serpent. The paradigmatic female creator in Chinese religion.

deities.earliest_attestation: Historical origin c. 960 CE; deified progressively
deities.summary: Goddess of the sea and protector of sailors — born Lin Moniang, a girl who could enter trance to guide ships to safety. Deified after death. Now worshipped by over 200 million people primarily around the Taiwan Strait and southeast coast.
primary_sources: Historical records from Song dynasty onward

deities.summary: The six divine emanations of Ahura Mazda — Vohu Manah (Good Mind), Asha Vahishta (Best Truth), Khshathra Vairya (Desirable Dominion), Spenta Armaiti (Holy Devotion), Haurvatat (Wholeness), and Ameretat (Immortality). Each governs a domain of creation and a moral quality.
deities.etymology: Avestan: amesha (immortal) + spenta (holy/bountiful)

deities.summary: Boundless Time — the heterodox Zoroastrian deity who preceded both Ahura Mazda and Angra Mainyu. In Zurvanism, Zurvan is the ultimate first principle from whom both good and evil emerge. Zurvan offered sacrifices wishing for a son (Ahura Mazda) but doubt entered his mind — from doubt was born Angra Mainyu, from the sacrifice Ahura Mazda.
Lord of Annwn — the Welsh Otherworld. Exchanges places with Pwyll for a year. His realm is a land of feasting and abundance, not punishment. Hunts with the Cwn Annwn (hounds of the underworld).
primary_sources: Mabinogion, First Branch (Pwyll Prince of Dyfed)

deities.summary: The sovereignty goddess of Wales — rides an enchanted horse no one can catch. Unjustly accused of killing her infant son. Bears her punishment with supernatural patience. Marries Pwyll and later Manawydan. Connected to horses and birds.

God of the sea and the Irish Otherworld (Tir na nOg). Guides the dead to the Isles of the Blessed. His cloak of invisibility creates the mists between worlds. Shakes his cloak between Ireland and the gods to separate the two realms after the Tuatha De Danann are defeated.

deities.earliest_attestation: early Irish texts; Feast of Imbolc
deities.summary: Triple goddess of poetry/inspiration, smithcraft, and healing. Daughter of the Dagda. Her eternal flame was tended at Kildare (later by St Brigid's nuns — the same fire, different theology). One of the most pan-Celtic figures, attested in Britain, Ireland, and Gaul.
primary_sources: St Brigid records (flame continued to 1220 CE)

deities.earliest_attestation: oral tradition; among the oldest figures in British mythology
deities.summary: The Old Woman of Winter — the divine hag who shapes the landscape by dropping boulders, creating mountains and lochs. She rules winter and fights against spring (Brigid). Possibly one of the oldest continuous deity figures in the British Isles.

The Gaulish horse goddess — the only Celtic deity to have had an official Roman cult (December 18 feast). Protector of horses, riders, and cavalry. Depicted seated on a horse or flanked by horses, holding fruit or a cornucopia.
primary_sources: Roman imperial inscriptions (300+ from Britain to Romania)

deities.summary: The Horned God — antlered, seated in the lotus position, surrounded by animals. Lord of nature, wild animals, and the liminal space between human and wild. He holds a torque (symbol of divine power) and a serpent.

deities.earliest_attestation: South Slavic oral tradition; recorded 19th c.
deities.summary: The god of spring, vegetation, and fertility — born and killed annually. He is the divine son of Perun who is stolen at birth and raised in the underworld by Veles. Returns in spring, marries his twin sister Morana/Marzanna, then is killed at midsummer. His death brings harvest.

The supreme sky deity of the Turkic-Altaic shamanic tradition — a benevolent creator who lives in the highest heaven. Opposed by Erlik in the underworld. The shamanic cosmos is a vertical axis between Ulgen's heaven and Erlik's underworld.

The cosmic axis — the tree, pole, or mountain that connects the underworld, middle world, and upper world. The shaman climbs it in trance. Present across Siberian, Norse (Yggdrasil), Mayan (World Tree), and many other traditions.

deities.earliest_attestation: Thai folk tradition; Hindu Brahma adapted
deities.summary: Thai version of the Hindu Brahma — the four-faced creator deity. In Thai folk religion, he is worshipped for worldly success and protection. The Erawan Shrine's Phra Phrom is among the most visited sacred sites in Bangkok.

Tu Bat Tu — the Four Immortals of Vietnamese tradition: the Immortal of the Mountain, the Immortal of Children, the Immortal of Heaven, and the Immortal of Cultivation. Each represents a domain of Vietnamese sacred life.

The God-King concept of Angkor — the Khmer king as divine incarnation of Shiva (or Vishnu), legitimized by a sacred lingam on Phnom Kulen. Angkor Wat is the mountain of the gods (Mount Meru) made architecture.

The lwa who controls the crossroads — the gateway between the human world and the spirit world (Ginen). All other lwa must pass through Legba. He is the first and last to be invoked. An old man with a cane and dog.

The lwa of the dead — baron of the cemetery, lord of death, healer of the dying. Cross-hat, cigar, rum. He can heal any illness except when he has dug the grave. Crude, sexual, irreverent — death as comedy.

The Maasai sky god — dual in nature: Engai Narok (Black God, benevolent, associated with rain and cattle) and Engai Nanyokie (Red God, vengeful, associated with lightning). One of the clearest divine dyads in African religion.

The Fullness — the divine realm of 30 Aeons arranged in syzygies (divine pairs). The highest pair: Bythos (Depth) and Sige (Silence). From their overflow, the crisis that creates the material world: Sophia's errant desire reaches beyond her and generates the Demiurge.

The Demiurge — born from Sophia's errant desire reaching beyond the Pleroma. He creates the material world thinking himself the only God: "I am a jealous God; there is no other God beside me." This boast reveals his ignorance — he does not know the higher divine realm. The Gnostic interpretation of Exodus 20:5.
primary_sources: On the Origin of the World

deities.summary: The first emanation from the Father — the divine mother, the first thought, the Forethought (Pronoia). She is the divine feminine principle of the highest realm, the three-fold perfect power. She pleads for the redemption of the divine sparks trapped in matter.

deities.earliest_attestation: Sefer ha-Bahir c. 1175 CE; Zohar c. 1280 CE
deities.summary: The Infinite — the absolute divine reality prior to any self-revelation or attribute. Not the God who speaks or acts but the groundless ground from which all divine activity emerges. The Ein Sof does not create directly — creation occurs through the 10 Sefirot (divine attributes/emanations).
deities.etymology: Hebrew: ein (not) + sof (end) = the endless

deities.earliest_attestation: Sefer Yetzirah c. 300 CE; developed in Zohar
deities.summary: The ten divine attributes through which Ein Sof creates and sustains all existence: Keter (Crown), Chokhmah (Wisdom), Binah (Understanding), Chesed (Lovingkindness), Gevurah (Strength/Judgment), Tiferet (Beauty/Harmony), Netzach (Victory/Eternity), Hod (Splendor), Yesod (Foundation), Malkhut (Kingdom). They form the Tree of Life.

The Primordial Human — the first configuration of divine light after the primordial contraction (Tzimtzum). He is not the historical Adam but the cosmic human blueprint from whom all creation descends. His body IS the structure of the Sefirot.
primary_sources: Chaim Vital, Etz Chaim (recording Luria's teachings)
iconography: the entire Sefirot structure as a human body

deities.summary: The spider god of Kiribati — Te Nareau the Old (Nareau the Elder) and Te Nareau the Young. The Old created the sky, sea, and earth from his thoughts. He asked the Young to complete creation by having other beings split the primordial joined sky-earth.

The Supreme Being of the Maori — known only to the priestly class (tohunga). Io-matua-kore (Io the parentless), Io-matua (Io the parent), and many other epithets. Io preceded all creation and created through words.
primary_sources: Maori esoteric tradition; Best, Maori Religion (1924)

deities.summary: The Feathered Serpent — god of wind, learning, and the morning star. Historical priest-king of Tula who became mythologized. He departed eastward on a raft of serpents, promising to return. The Aztec calendar predicted his return-year: 1519 CE, when Cortes arrived.

Lord of the dead and Mictlan (the underworld). The skeleton deity who receives the dead after their four-year journey through the nine levels of Mictlan. Quetzalcoatl descended to Mictlan to retrieve the bones of the dead to create the current human race.

The Sami Sun God — his warmth brings reindeer and life back to the arctic tundra each spring. His arrival is celebrated with butter sacrifices smeared on doorposts (the butter melting in sunlight = sacrifice accepted).

The Sami mother goddess — one of the four Sami mother goddesses (Madderakkha, Sarahkka, Juksakkha, Uksakkha). Madderakkha is the primary mother who receives the soul-stuff for each new human from God and gives it to her daughters to complete.
The Estonian god of music and song — it was his singing that taught all living beings their voices. Birds, animals, and humans all sing because Vanemuine sang first.
The Anglo-Saxon Odin — god of wisdom, war, death, poetry, and the wild hunt. Father of the English royal lines (Ethelbald of Mercia, etc., trace descent to Woden). Wednesday = Woden's day.
deities.etymology: OE Woden = ON Odin = PIE *Wod-anaz (the inspired one)

deities.earliest_attestation: Bede, De Temporum Ratione c. 725 CE
deities.summary: The Anglo-Saxon spring goddess — her month (Eosturmonath = April) gives Easter its English name. Possibly associated with the dawn (cognate of Greek Eos, Sanskrit Ushas). Bede is our only source.
deities.etymology: OE Eostre = PIE *h2ewsos (dawn)

deities.summary: The Germanic earth mother — Tacitus describes an annual procession of her sacred wagon across the land, during which peace is kept and weapons are laid down. She then bathes in a sacred lake, and her attendants are drowned to maintain the secret. The most complete description of Germanic earth-goddess worship.

The three Germanic mother goddesses — attested in over 800 inscriptions from the Rhine region. They appear as a triad of seated women, often holding fruit, bread, and infant. Named Aufaniae, Gabiae, Vacallinehae, and many local names. The most widely attested pre-Christian religious practice in Germanic-Roman territory.

The supreme Etruscan sky god — held three thunderbolts (warning, punishing, destroying). He rules with Uni (Juno) and Menrva (Minerva) as the Etruscan Capitoline triad. The direct predecessor of Jupiter in Roman religion.
primary_sources: Liver of Piacenza (bronze liver cosmological map)

deities.summary: The Etruscan psychopomp/death demon — not the Greek Charon but a terrifying hammer-wielding demon who escorts (or drives) souls to the underworld. His face is demonic; he wields a massive hammer.

The primordial Earth — first to emerge from Chaos. She bears Ouranos (Sky) and from their union generates the Titans, Cyclopes, and Hecatonchires. The very body of the earth, the mother of all life, and the source of the oldest oracles (Delphi was originally Gaia's).

Goddess of the crossroads, night, magic, and the moon. Triple-form (Hecate Triformis). She holds torches and stands at the crossroads between worlds. Patron of witches and magicians. She was present at Persephone's abduction and helped search for her.

The divine messenger, psychopomp, trickster, and patron of crossroads, commerce, thieves, and travelers. Steals Apollo's cattle on the day of his birth. Guides souls to the underworld. Invents the lyre from a tortoise shell.

Goddess of grain, agriculture, and the sacred law. Her grief at Persephone's abduction starves the world — her joy at the return restores it. Founder of the Eleusinian Mysteries, which promised initiates a blessed afterlife.

King of the Roman gods — the sky, thunder, law, and justice. Husband of Juno. The Capitoline triad (Jupiter, Juno, Minerva) was the state religion of Rome. He receives the eagle as his sacred bird and the oak as his tree.
deities.etymology: Latin: Iuppiter = Iovis (genitive) = PIE *Dyeus Phater

deities.summary: God of war and agriculture — in early Rome, Mars was primarily an agricultural protector before becoming the war god. Father of Romulus, the founder of Rome. March is named for him.

Goddess of love, beauty, desire, and fertility. Mother of Aeneas (through whom Rome traced divine ancestry). Julius Caesar and Augustus claimed descent from Venus through Aeneas. She is more political in Rome than Aphrodite in Greece.
primary_sources: Lucretius, De Rerum Natura (hymn to Venus)

deities.summary: Goddess of the hunt, the moon, and women in childbirth. Her great temple at Aricia (Diana Nemorensis) was one of the oldest in Italy. In Frazer's Golden Bough, her priest-king (the Rex Nemorensis) who kills his predecessor is the paradigmatic dying-king.
Goddess of the hearth and sacred fire — the eternal flame in the Forum Romanum was her body. The Vestal Virgins tended it for 1,000 years. If the flame went out, Rome would fall.

God of the sun (in later tradition), prophecy, music, poetry, healing, and plague. Twin of Artemis. His oracle at Delphi was the most important in the ancient world. He shoots arrows of plague and healing alike.
Goddess of love, desire, beauty, and pleasure. Born from the sea foam around Ouranos's severed genitals. Her power is irresistible — even Zeus fears it. Her son Eros is her instrument.

Goddess of wisdom, strategic war, craft, and justice. Born fully armored from Zeus's head. Patron of Athens. She mediates between the old (Erinyes) and new (Olympian) divine orders in the Oresteia. Both divine wisdom and practical craft.

King of the Olympian gods — sky, thunder, law, justice, and cosmic order. Overthrew the Titans. His three realms (sky, sea, underworld) divided with Poseidon and Hades. The Fate (Moira) even Zeus cannot override.
deities.etymology: Greek Zeus = PIE *Dyeus (shining sky)

deities.earliest_attestation: Bhagavata Purana c. 900 CE; Mahabharata c. 400 BCE
deities.summary: The eighth avatar of Vishnu — divine lover, warrior, philosopher, and cosmic trickster. The Bhagavad Gita reveals his cosmic form (Vishvarupa). He plays the flute as the cosmic song that draws all souls to him.

The destroyer and transformer — his destruction enables renewal. Lord of yoga, meditation, and the ascetic path. His Tandava dance is Nataraja. He is simultaneously the ascetic (the still) and the erotic (Linga). Mahadeva — the Great God.

The Buddha of Infinite Light — through 48 vows, he created the Pure Land (Sukhavati) as a realm where all who call his name with sincere faith are reborn. In East Asian Buddhism, Pure Land theology is the most widely practiced path.

The arch-orisha who shapes human bodies — Olodumare sent him to create the physical world, but he got drunk on palm wine and Oduduwa completed the task. He is still the orisha of purity, white cloth, and the creator of human bodies, including those born with disabilities (who are sacred to him).

Orisha of fresh water, love, fertility, beauty, and healing. The youngest and most beloved of the orishas. When the male orishas excluded her from their council, she withheld her powers — the world dried up and the council was forced to include her.

deities.earliest_attestation: Investiture of the Gods novel c. 1600 CE; older roots
deities.summary: The divine child warrior — born in a lotus ball after three years and six months in the womb. Kills the Dragon King's son. To spare his parents from divine retribution, he cuts off his own flesh and returns it to his parents, then is reborn from lotus flowers. The most beloved divine child in Chinese folk religion.
primary_sources: Investiture of the Gods (Feng Shen Yanyi)

deities.summary: Supreme deity of the Taino — the great earth goddess and mother of water. Her son Yucahu (lord of cassava and the sea) is the male divine principle.
Lord of cassava and the sea — the Taino male creator deity, son of Atabey. He and his mother are the supreme divine pair.

Smoking Mirror — the dark omniscient god who sees all through his obsidian mirror. Rival of Quetzalcoatl, he defeated him and drove him from Tula. Creator and destroyer of cosmic ages. Patron of warriors, sorcerers, and nighttime.
The Olmec divine child — half-human, half-jaguar, crying mouth. The most iconic Olmec sacred image, possibly representing the rain deity or a shamanic transformation between human and jaguar.
The Scythian supreme deity — goddess of fire and the hearth. Herodotus identifies her as the Scythian equivalent of Hestia. The most sacred deity, invoked in the most binding oaths.

deities.earliest_attestation: c. 800 BCE (Phrygian); Roman cult from 204 BCE
deities.summary: The Great Mother of the gods — her dying-rising consort Attis annually dies and is resurrected. Her priests (Galli) were self-castrated. Her cult was so powerful the Romans imported it officially during the Punic Wars.
The dying-rising consort of Cybele — castrated himself (or was killed by a boar), died, and was resurrected. The annual rites of Attis (15-27 March) culminated in resurrection on the Day of Joy.

The Mystery god of the soldier cult — Mithras slays the cosmic bull (tauroctony), from whose blood springs the grain and from whose tail the vine. Initiation through seven grades. Born December 25 from a rock.

deities.earliest_attestation: New Kingdom (c. 1550 BCE) as Amun-Ra; Ptolemaic expansion
deities.summary: The supreme god of the New Kingdom — the merger of the hidden god Amun (The Hidden One) with Ra (the solar principle). He contains both the transcendent (Amun) and the immanent (Ra). Serapis is the Ptolemaic development.

The Hellenistic synthesis deity — Osiris + Apis bull + Zeus/Pluto. Created by Ptolemy I to unite Greek and Egyptian subjects. His cult spread across the Mediterranean and was the most serious competitor to early Christianity.
The Hopi creator deity — she thought the world into being and is the source of all creative wisdom. She guides the Hero Twins (Poqanghoya and Palongawhoya) and all humans who need guidance.
The Oromo sky god — the supreme deity of the Oromo people of Ethiopia. Eternal, single, and the source of ayyaana (divine spirit) that inhabits everything.

The Ainu divine system — Kamuy Mosir (the world of the gods) mirrors the human world. Kamuy are divine beings inhabiting all aspects of nature. The bear (Kim-un Kamuy) is the most important kamuy — the mountain god who visits in the form of a bear.

The supreme teacher of the Bon tradition — the Tibetan pre-Buddhist spiritual tradition. He revealed the Bon teachings 18,000 years ago (traditional dating). He mirrors the Buddha's role in Buddhism but in the indigenous Tibetan tradition.

Co-creator of Japan with Izanagi, her husband. She died giving birth to the fire god Kagutsuchi. She descended to Yomi (the underworld) and when Izanagi followed and saw her rotting form, she became queen of the dead and threatened to kill 1,000 people per day.

The deified Guan Yu at his highest apotheosis — Emperor Guan, God of War and Righteousness, patron of the military, police, and business. His canonization as an emperor-god is the highest apotheosis in Chinese history.

The supreme Shinto deity and solar goddess — born from Izanagi's left eye. When Susanoo's violence drove her into the cave (Ama-no-Iwato), the world fell into darkness. The other gods danced and laughed until she emerged, restoring light.
Great Spirit of the Cherokee — the supreme creator. Pervades all things. Beyond gender and form. The earth itself was made from mud brought up by Water Beetle from beneath the primordial waters.

Corn Mother of the Cherokee — produced corn from her own body. When her sons discovered the secret and were horrified, she instructed them to kill her and drag her body in seven circles — corn grew from the bloodied earth. The Cherokee dying-and-rising vegetation goddess.
The Hunter — Cherokee culture hero. Husband of Selu. Father of the Twin Thunders. Knew all animals before they roamed free; his sons released them out of curiosity, creating the hunting world.
The Apache Life-Giver — supreme deity who is the source of all life and breath. Not actively interventional but the underlying source of all existence. Withdrawn but always present.
primary_sources: Goodwin, The Social Organization of the Western Apache

deities.summary: The Mountain Spirits — supernatural beings who taught humanity how to live. They dance in the Crown Dance (Gaan Dance) and protect Apache people. They are the source of healing power.

The elder of the Navajo Hero Twins — son of Changing Woman and the Sun. With his brother Born-for-Water, slays the monsters that plagued the Earth, making it safe for the Diné. Their journey is recounted in the Blessingway.
The younger Navajo Hero Twin — son of Changing Woman. Born for Water complements his brother Monster Slayer through cunning and spiritual practice rather than violence.

deities.earliest_attestation: oral tradition; Waters, Book of the Hopi (1963)
deities.summary: The Hopi supreme being — the Creator. In the beginning was Taiowa alone, then he created Sotuknang to make the universe. Taiowa is the source from which all emerges.
The nephew (or first creation) of Taiowa — the one who shaped the universe according to Taiowa's plan. He created the Spider Grandmother and the first humans.

The Spirit of Death and Earth — guardian of this Fourth World (the current age). He greeted the emerging Hopi when they came up from the Third World and taught them how to live in this world. He alone can step between worlds.

The ancestral spirits and nature-spirits of the Hopi — hundreds of named kachinas. They visit the villages from the San Francisco Peaks for half the year. Each represents an aspect of creation: rain, corn, animals, ancestors. Children receive kachina dolls (tihu) as both toys and teaching.

The Spider — Lakota trickster and culture hero who created the world but is also the source of foolishness and trouble. His tricks teach as often as they harm. Inventor of language and the first to deceive humans.
The supernatural thunder beings — they bring rain and lightning. Their wings make thunder; their flashing eyes are lightning. They protect humans but are too powerful to look upon directly.
The Great Spirit of the Anishinaabe peoples — supreme creator and source of all spiritual power. Not a personal god but the cosmic spiritual force.

The cannibal ice spirit — embodies the destructive force of greed and starvation. A human who eats human flesh becomes a Windigo. Stands as a moral warning against selfishness in a survival-marginal climate.
The trickster-hero-creator of the Anishinaabe — the Great Hare. Brought fire, taught medicines, recreated the world after the great flood. Same figure as Nanabozho in different transliteration.

The supreme being of the Mapuche people — the lord/maker of people. Quadripartite: old man, old woman, young man, young woman — the cosmic four-fold unity. The most articulated quadripartite supreme deity in South America.
Ancestral spirits and volcanic-thunder beings of the Mapuche — they inhabit volcanoes and govern weather. The thunderous voices of the ancestors.
The Muisca Great Mother — emerged from Lake Iguaque with a three-year-old boy. When he grew up they married and populated the world. After teaching the people, both returned to the lake as serpents.
primary_sources: Spanish colonial accounts of Muisca religion

deities.summary: The Muisca supreme creator — pure light contained within himself. He released the light from his interior, creating black birds that flew over the world bringing dawn.

The Akkadian goddess of love, war, sex, and political power — successor to Sumerian Inanna with distinct Akkadian emphases. Her descent to the underworld in the Akkadian version differs from Inanna's. Patron of Nineveh.
primary_sources: Descent of Ishtar to the Underworld (Akkadian)

deities.earliest_attestation: c. 1900 BCE (city); supreme deity from c. 1300 BCE
deities.summary: The Assyrian state god — supreme deity of the Assyrian Empire. Originally a city deity of Ashur (the city), elevated to imperial chief deity. Identified with Anshar (heaven), and given Marduk's attributes during Assyrian conquest of Babylon.
God of writing, wisdom, scribes, and the planet Mercury. Son of Marduk. His cult flourished in Babylon and especially Borsippa. Akkadian Thoth/Hermes — the divine scribe.

deities.earliest_attestation: c. 2000 BCE (Sumerian lilītu); developed in Talmud, Zohar
deities.summary: The night-demon — wind-demoness in Sumerian, the disobedient first wife of Adam in later Jewish tradition (refusing to lie beneath him), queen of demons in the Zohar. She steals infants and seduces sleeping men. The most theologically developed female demon in Western tradition.
primary_sources: Alphabet of Ben Sira c. 700-1000 CE

deities.summary: Isis in her late Egyptian/Hellenistic form — became the universal mother goddess of the Mediterranean. Her temples extended from Britain to Mesopotamia. She is the Queen of Heaven, Star of the Sea, healer, magician supreme. The Aretalogies (self-revelations) list her as containing all other goddesses.
alternate_names: Mistress of the Words of Power

deities.summary: The wrathful protective goddess — the only female deity among the eight Dharmapala (Dharma protectors). Rides a mule across a sea of blood. Holds a skull cup. Her wrath protects the Dharma.
The first patriarch of Chan/Zen Buddhism — the Indian monk who brought meditation tradition to China. Reportedly sat facing a wall for 9 years at Shaolin. Founder of the lineage that became Zen.
The Sixth Patriarch of Chan — illiterate woodcutter who became enlightened upon hearing the Diamond Sutra. Author of the Platform Sutra. Founded the Southern School of sudden enlightenment.

deities.earliest_attestation: c. 1500 BCE (Zhou dynasty); central in Confucian thought
deities.summary: Heaven — not a personal god but the cosmic moral order. Tian Ming (Mandate of Heaven) gives rulers legitimacy and removes it when they fail. The impersonal supreme principle of Chinese imperial cosmology.

Confucius as the supreme sage of Chinese civilization — progressively elevated to divine status, with state cult and temple offerings. The most successful philosopher-to-deity transformation in human history.

The Supreme Being of Caodaism — combines Buddha, Lao Tzu, Confucius, Jesus, and Muhammad with Victor Hugo, Sun Yat-sen, and Joan of Arc as saints. The most explicit modern syncretism of world religions into one tradition.
alternate_names: Đại Đạo Tam Kỳ Phổ Độ

deities.summary: The Birth Grandmother — Korean shamanic deity of childbirth, infants, and child-rearing. Three goddesses or one triple goddess. Mothers pray to her for safe birth and healthy children.
The Seven Stars — Korean shamanic deities of the Big Dipper. They govern longevity, fortune, and human destiny. Their cult predates Buddhism in Korea and was absorbed into Korean Buddhism.
The ancestor spirits of Mongolian shamanism — spirits of deceased shamans and significant ancestors that protect and guide the living. Embodied in small images kept in the home (also called ongon).

The supreme deity of the Buryat — the bald-headed father god. Rules the 99 Tenger (sky beings): 55 western (good) and 44 eastern (evil). The cosmic dualism of Buryat shamanic theology runs through the entire pantheon.
The Great Mother of Buryat shamanism — feminine cosmic source. Various local manifestations across the Buryat region.

deities.earliest_attestation: pre-Christian Magyar; survived in Christian Hungarian
deities.summary: The supreme Magyar deity — Old Hungarian name for God, surviving the conversion to Christianity. Possibly cognate with Turkic Tengri family. Rules the Sky and the celestial Tree of Life.

The Ryukyuan creation goddess — descended from heaven to create the islands. Mother of all life in Okinawa. The Ryukyuan kami system is distinct from mainland Shinto, with strong matriarchal elements.

deities.earliest_attestation: c. 1800 CE (Cuban transformation of Yoruba Shango)
deities.summary: The Santeria transformation of Yoruba Shango — fully fused with the Catholic saint Santa Bárbara. The red and white colors, thunder, and royal power persist. The most fully-realized syncretism in the Afro-Caribbean tradition.

The rebellious first creation of Amma — impatient, he tore himself from the cosmic egg before time, taking only part of his placenta (which became the earth in fragmented form). His sin contaminated creation. He became the Pale Fox (Vulpes pallida), wandering the earth as the source of disorder and divination.
primary_sources: Griaule and Dieterlen, The Pale Fox

deities.summary: The Two Men — ancestor beings of the Western Desert peoples who traveled across the desert in the Dreamtime, creating waterholes, plants, and sacred sites. They are the most widespread Western Desert ancestor figures.

The most iconic figure of Minoan religion — bare-breasted woman holding snakes, sometimes with a cat on her head. Her exact identity and theology are unknown (Linear A is undeciphered), but the iconography suggests a powerful goddess of life, death, and rebirth.

The supreme Armenian deity — derived from Persian Ahura Mazda but assimilated with Greek Zeus during the Hellenistic period. Father of the gods, creator of heaven and earth. His chief temple was at Ani-Kamakh.

Armenian goddess of fertility, healing, and wisdom — descended from Iranian Anahita. Mother and protector goddess. Her major shrine at Eriza had a famous golden statue (destroyed by Antony, restored by Tiridates III).

deities.earliest_attestation: pre-Christian; preserved in Georgian Christian period
deities.summary: The supreme Georgian deity — pre-Christian sky god whose name became the Georgian word for God after Christianization. Lives in the highest heaven, removed from direct intervention. Lower deities and mountain spirits serve as mediators.
primary_sources: Charachidze, Le système religieux de la Géorgie païenne

deities.summary: The supreme deity of the Luba — beyond comprehension, beyond image. Worshipped indirectly through ancestor spirits and the sacred kingship. The Luba sacred kingship is one of the most institutionally articulated divine kingships in Africa.
primary_sources: Luba oral tradition; Reefe, The Rainbow and the Kings

deities.summary: The female ancestral medium of the Luba kingdom — when the king died, his sacred essence was transferred to a female medium (Mwadi) who held the kingship's spiritual power. A rare example of explicit female mediation of male royal divinity.
primary_sources: Reefe; de Heusch, The Drunken King

deities.summary: The Good Twin (Teharonhiawako) — son of Sky Woman's daughter. Created beneficial things: human beings, edible plants, gentle animals, the sun. Defeated his evil twin in cosmic combat. The Iroquois Christ-figure.

The Evil Twin (Tawiskaron) — created harmful things: thorns, predators, disease, monsters, winter. Born by tearing through his mother's side (killing her). Defeated by Sapling but his creations remain.

The hummingbird god of war and sun — patron of the Mexica/Aztec. Born from Coatlicue when a ball of feathers fell into her clothes. Killed his sister Coyolxauhqui (the moon) the moment he was born, defending his mother. The Aztec sun who needs human hearts to continue rising.

The Moon — daughter of Coatlicue, sister of Huitzilopochtli. She led her 400 siblings (the stars) to kill their pregnant mother but was decapitated and dismembered by the newborn Huitzilopochtli, who threw her body down Coatepec mountain. The famous Coyolxauhqui Stone shows her dismembered form.

Obsidian Butterfly — the fierce skeletal deity with jaguar claws and butterfly wings of obsidian knives. A tzitzimitl (star-demon) of the Cihuateteo (warrior-women who died in childbirth). Patron of fertility and warfare in their inverse forms.

The Moche/Chimu moon deity — the most powerful celestial body in coastal Andean religion (more important than the sun, contrary to the highland Inca tradition). Si governed fishing, crops, and storms.

The Olmec proto-deity — a composite creature combining crocodile, serpent, and bird features. The earliest documented divine image in Mesoamerica. Possibly the prototype of all later Mesoamerican feathered serpents.
primary_sources: Olmec stone sculpture (San Lorenzo, La Venta)

deities.summary: Fish avatar — saved Manu and the Vedas from the cosmic flood. One of the ten avatars (Dasavatara) of Vishnu.
Tortoise avatar — supported Mount Mandara during the churning of the cosmic ocean. One of the ten avatars (Dasavatara) of Vishnu.
Boar avatar — lifted the earth from the cosmic ocean where it had been dragged by the demon Hiranyaksha. One of the ten avatars (Dasavatara) of Vishnu.

Man-lion avatar — killed the demon Hiranyakashipu to protect the devotee Prahlada. Neither man nor animal, neither inside nor outside, neither day nor night. One of the ten avatars (Dasavatara) of Vishnu.
Dwarf avatar — defeated the demon-king Bali by asking for three paces of land then expanding to cosmic size. One of the ten avatars (Dasavatara) of Vishnu.
Axe-wielding warrior — destroyed corrupt Kshatriya rulers 21 times. The wrathful Brahmin avatar. One of the ten avatars (Dasavatara) of Vishnu.
The hero of the Ramayana — virtuous king of Ayodhya, perfect husband, restorer of dharma. Killed the demon-king Ravana. One of the ten avatars (Dasavatara) of Vishnu.
In Vaishnava tradition, the Buddha is the 9th avatar — sent to lead unworthy beings away from the Vedas. The Hindu absorption of the Buddha. One of the ten avatars (Dasavatara) of Vishnu.

The future avatar — will appear on a white horse at the end of the Kali Yuga to destroy the wicked and inaugurate a new age. Hindu eschatological savior. One of the ten avatars (Dasavatara) of Vishnu.
Goddess of fortune, wealth, beauty, and abundance. Consort of Vishnu. Born from the churning of the cosmic ocean. She blesses whomever she chooses; she is fickle but never absent from the divine.
Goddess of wisdom, music, arts, and rivers. Consort of Brahma. The Saraswati river in Vedic times was sacred; she is identified with its life-giving flow.

The supreme goddess in her gentle, married form — daughter of the mountain (Himavat), wife of Shiva, mother of Ganesha and Kartikeya. She is the same divine feminine as Kali and Durga but in domesticated form. Parvati = Kali = Durga as Tridevi.

God of war, victory, and youthful divine power. Son of Shiva and Parvati. Major Tamil deity (Murugan). Leads the divine army against demons. His six faces correspond to the six attributes of perfection.
The monkey god — devoted servant of Rama, the embodiment of bhakti (devotion). Son of the wind god Vayu. Possesses immense strength and yogic powers. The model of perfect service to the divine.

The first mortal — therefore the lord of death. King of the dead. Judges the dead and assigns them to heavens or hells. His sister Yami (the river Yamuna) was his twin. The Indo-Iranian first man / first king of the dead.
deities.etymology: Sanskrit yama = twin (PIE *yemo)

deities.summary: God of the wind, breath, life-force. Father of Hanuman and Bhima. The breath in living beings is his presence. Cognate with Avestan Vayu.
The cosmic feminine energy — the active principle without which Shiva is inert. "Shiva without Shakti is shava (corpse)." All goddesses are manifestations of Shakti.

The preserver — second of the Trimurti. Reclines on the cosmic serpent Shesha on the cosmic ocean. From his navel grows the lotus from which Brahma emerges. Takes ten avatars to preserve cosmic order.

The creator — first of the Trimurti. Has four faces looking in all directions. Born from the lotus growing from Vishnu's navel. Rarely worshipped despite being the creator — only two temples in all of India.

The historical Buddha — Siddhartha Gautama, prince of the Shakya clan who renounced his kingdom, achieved enlightenment under the Bodhi tree, and taught the Dharma for 45 years before his parinirvana. The founder of Buddhism.
The Diamond Being — embodiment of the purification of all karmic obscurations. His 100-syllable mantra is the most important Vajrayana purification practice.
The Destroyer of Death — the wrathful form of Manjushri. Buffalo-headed, with multiple faces and arms, surrounded by skulls and flames. He defeated Yama (death) to liberate beings from samsara.
The female bodhisattva of compassion and longevity. Seven eyes (one on forehead, palms, soles of feet) see suffering in all directions. Particularly invoked for healing and long life.
The Great Black One — protector of the Dharma, fierce defender of practitioners. Originally a Hindu form of Shiva, adopted into Vajrayana as a protector. Multiple forms (2-arm, 4-arm, 6-arm).
The Buddha in tantric union with his consort Nairatmya. Eight heads, 16 arms. Central deity of the Hevajra Tantra — one of the most important Anuttarayoga Tantras. His name means "Joyful Vajra."

Queen of the Norse underworld (also called Hel). Daughter of Loki. Half-living, half-corpse-blue. Receives those who die of sickness and old age (those who die in battle go to Valhalla or Folkvangar).

God of the sea, wealth, and seafaring. Chief of the Vanir before peace with the Aesir. Father of Freyr and Freya. His marriage to the mountain-giantess Skadi failed because she hated the sea and he hated the mountains.

The original Germanic war god — likely the original sky-father (PIE *Tiwaz, cognate with Zeus). His role diminished as Odin rose. Lost his hand to Fenrir to bind the wolf, becoming the god of oaths and lawful combat.
deities.etymology: PG *Tiwaz = PIE *Dyeus

deities.summary: Keeper of the golden apples that maintain the gods' youth. When Loki delivered her to the giant Thiazi, the gods began to age — proving that even gods need her gift.

Three female beings who weave the fate of all beings, including the gods: Urd (Was/Fate), Verdandi (Becoming), Skuld (Shall-Be). They tend Yggdrasil with water from the Well of Urd. Their decrees cannot be undone, not even by Odin.

Jackal-headed god of mummification and the journey of the dead. Conducts the Weighing of the Heart against the feather of Ma'at. The earliest Egyptian death deity, later displaced by Osiris but retaining the judgment role.
Lord of the dead, dying-and-rising god. Murdered and dismembered by Set, gathered and revived by Isis enough to conceive Horus. Then ruled the underworld. Every dead Pharaoh became Osiris.

The cosmic serpent of chaos — every night attacks Ra's solar barque trying to prevent the sun's rebirth. He is the absolute adversary, the principle of non-being. Defeated by Set (and Ra's magic) each night, but eternal in his enmity.

Goddess and concept of cosmic order, truth, justice, and balance. The feather of Ma'at is weighed against the heart of the dead. She is both a goddess and the cosmic principle she embodies — the most important abstract concept in Egyptian theology.

Great goddess of magic, motherhood, healing, and the throne. Sister-wife of Osiris. Mother of Horus. Tricked Ra into revealing his secret name (gaining power equal to his). The most magically powerful Egyptian deity.
Queen of the Olympian gods — wife and sister of Zeus. Goddess of marriage, women, and family. Her jealousy is famous; she persecutes Zeus's mortal lovers and their children (especially Heracles).
Lord of the underworld and the dead. Brother of Zeus and Poseidon. Drew the underworld in the lots-drawing among the three brothers. Not evil — just the impersonal lord of an irreversible domain.
God of wine, ecstasy, theatre, ritual madness, and resurrection. Born twice (from Semele and Zeus's thigh). His maenads tear apart whoever opposes him. The god who dissolves boundaries of self.
Already entered — lame craftsman god. Adding additional facet.

The legendary singer-shaman whose music could move stones and tame beasts. Descended to the underworld to retrieve his wife Eurydice — almost succeeded but lost her by looking back. Founder of the Orphic mystery tradition.
The Three Fates — Clotho spins the thread of life, Lachesis measures it, Atropos cuts it. Even Zeus is subject to Fate. The Greek parallel of the Norns, Norse Norns, and Mokosh.

The divine champion — son of Zeus and Alcmene. Performed the 12 Labors. Eventually ascended to Olympus, deified after death. The paradigmatic divine hero — half-mortal, suffering, achieving godhood through trial.

The primordial cosmic being — emerged from the cosmic egg. As he grew (10 feet per day for 18,000 years), his body separated yin and yang, sky and earth. When he died, his body became the world: eyes = sun and moon, breath = wind, hair = stars, flesh = earth.
iconography: gigantic figure between sky and earth

deities.summary: Mother of the ten suns — she gives birth to suns and bathes them. The original Chinese cosmology had ten suns rising in rotation until Houyi shot down nine of them.
The divine archer who shot down nine of the ten suns when their simultaneous appearance scorched the earth. His wife Chang'e took his elixir of immortality and fled to the moon.

Wife of Houyi — drank his elixir of immortality and ascended to the moon, where she lives with a jade rabbit. The mid-Autumn festival celebrates her. The most beloved Chinese mythological figure for ordinary people.

deities.earliest_attestation: Yuan dynasty (1271-1368 CE); older folk origins
deities.summary: The eight legendary immortals of Taoist tradition: Lu Dongbin, He Xiangu, Zhang Guolao, Han Xiangzi, Tieguai Li, Cao Guojiu, Lan Caihe, Han Zhongli. Each represents a path to immortality and carries a unique magical implement.

The Nine-Tailed Fox — both auspicious omen (in Tang dynasty) and shapeshifting seductress demon (in popular tradition). Becomes more powerful with each century of life. In some traditions, a benevolent goddess; in others, a man-eating demon.

deities.earliest_attestation: Journey to the West (Wu Cheng'en, 16th c.); older folk origin
deities.summary: The Monkey King — born from a stone egg, learned the 72 transformations, defeated the gods of heaven, was imprisoned under a mountain by Buddha for 500 years, then accompanied Tang Sanzang to retrieve the Buddhist sutras from India. Bridge between Hindu Hanuman and East Asian trickster traditions.

The legendary controller of the floods — tamed the rivers of China by digging channels rather than building dams. Founded the Xia dynasty. The cultural hero whose taming of nature founded Chinese civilization.
Vedic dawn goddess — the most beautiful in the Rigveda. Her appearance is celebrated with 20+ hymns. The most direct surviving PIE *h₂éwsōs.
deities.etymology: Sanskrit Ushas = PIE *h₂éwsōs = Greek Eos, Latin Aurora, Lithuanian Auszra, English Eostre

deities.summary: The Vedic god of contracts and friendship — partner of Varuna as the Adityas. While Varuna oversees the cosmic order, Mitra oversees the human bonds (treaties, alliances) that maintain it.
The first man — survived the flood by Matsya's warning. Father of humanity. Author of the Manusmriti (Laws of Manu). The Vedic equivalent of Adam — the first human and the first patriarch.
deities.etymology: Sanskrit Manu = PIE *manus (man) = English man, German Mann

deities.summary: Already entered as the Wanderer. Adding facets for completeness.
The Good God — chief of the Tuatha De Danann. His cauldron feeds all; his club kills and revives; his harp controls the seasons.
Greatest Irish hero, son of Lugh. His battle-frenzy (riastrad) transforms him into a monstrous figure. Defended Ulster alone against Connacht.
First king of the Tuatha De Danann — lost his arm in battle, replaced with a silver prosthetic by Dian Cecht. King must be physically perfect; he abdicated and was restored.
