Physician of the gods — healed the wounded Tuatha De by throwing them in the Well of Slaine. Crafted Nuada's silver arm. Killed his own son Miach when Miach's healing surpassed his.
The master magician of the Welsh gods — shapeshifter, trickster, creator of the flower-bride Blodeuwedd. Uncle and guardian of Lleu Llaw Gyffes.

Witch-goddess and keeper of the Cauldron of Inspiration (Awen). Brewed the potion for a year; it was accidentally tasted by the servant Gwion, who fled through shapeshifting transformations before being swallowed and reborn as the bard Taliesin.
God of fertility, sunshine, and rain — chief of the Vanir alongside his sister Freya. Gave away his sword to win Gerd; it fights giants on its own. At Ragnarok, dies fighting Surt without his sword.
Trickster shapeshifter — companion and nemesis of Thor and Odin. Mother of Sleipnir (while in mare form), father of Fenrir, Jormungandr, and Hel. Engineered Baldur's death. Bound until Ragnarok.
The watchman of the gods — stands at Bifrost to guard Asgard. His senses are so acute he can hear grass grow. Will sound the Gjallarhorn at Ragnarok.

The World Tree — the immense ash tree connecting the nine worlds. At its roots: the Well of Urd (Norns), Mimir's Well of Wisdom (Odin's eye price), and Niflheim. Its branches reach to Asgard. Nidhogg gnaws its roots; the eagle perches at its crown.
The three weavers of fate — Urd (Was), Verdandi (Becoming), Skuld (Shall-Be). They tend Yggdrasil and weave the fate of gods and men. Their decree cannot be undone.
The Midgard Serpent — child of Loki, encircles all the earth and bites its own tail. Thor's cosmic enemy. At Ragnarok, Thor kills Jormungandr but dies from its venom.

The great wolf — child of Loki. Was bound by the magical ribbon Gleipnir (made from impossible things). Tyr sacrificed his hand as pledge of good faith when Fenrir submitted to be bound. Will break free at Ragnarok and kill Odin.
The first Imam in Shia Islam — cousin and son-in-law of Muhammad. His authority was divinely ordained at Ghadir Khumm. Embodies divine knowledge (ilm) and is the gate of the Prophet's knowledge.

The martyred Imam — grandson of Muhammad, killed at Karbala on Ashura. His martyrdom is the foundational tragedy of Shia Islam — the cosmic sacrifice that illuminates the nature of justice and injustice.
The daughter of Muhammad and wife of Imam Ali — "the radiant." In Shia theology she holds a unique cosmic status; her intercession is sought. The Infallible Lady.

In Nizari Ismaili Islam, the Imam is always present — a living, walking divine guide descended from Ali. Currently His Highness the Aga Khan IV. The Imam is the speaking Quran (natiq) to the written Quran's silence.
The cosmic Christ in Revelation — slain yet standing, with seven eyes and seven horns, worthy to open the seven seals. The sacrificial lamb who becomes the sovereign of the cosmos.

The great harlot — rides the scarlet beast, drunk on the blood of saints. In Protestant tradition identified with Rome; in critical scholarship, likely refers to Rome itself. The anti-divine feminine of Revelation's cosmic drama.
The Bride of Christ descending from heaven — the new creation, 12,000 stadia on each side, walls of jasper, streets of gold. The eschatological city-paradise.

Amida as the object of nembutsu practice — calling the name with sincerity guarantees rebirth in the Pure Land. Shinran (1173-1263) made this even more radical: the call itself is Amida's gift, not human effort.
Founder of Jodo Shinshu — the most radical Pure Land school: salvation is entirely Amida's work. The nembutsu is not a practice to earn merit but a response of gratitude to already-received grace.

The butterfly-dreamer — wrote the most profound of Taoist philosophical texts. A cook cuts a bull by following the grain of nature (Tao). Death is transformation, not loss. The sage flows with Tao like water.
Chaos — the ruler of the Center. Emperors of North and South drilled seven holes in him (to give him senses), and he died. The Taoist parable: intervention destroys what it intends to improve.

Supreme deity of the Mother Tantra — in union with his consort Vajravarahi. Subdued Shiva's 24 power-sites. Blue, with four faces and twelve arms. At the center of the Kagyu and Gelug meditation practices.

The supreme female deity of Vajrayana — naked, red, holding a skull cup of blood and a curved knife. The embodiment of bliss-emptiness. Her practice is considered the highest and most secret in Tibetan Buddhism.
The Secret Assembly — one of the oldest Vajrayana tantras. Three-faced, six-armed, in union with his consort Sparshavajra. Lord of body, speech, and mind. The foundational tantra of Gelug practice.

The supreme being of the Igbo — "the great chi." Omnipresent, all-powerful, unknowable directly. The sun (Anyanwu) is his messenger. He is approached through chi (personal spirit) and lesser deities (alusi).
Earth goddess of the Igbo — the most important deity in daily life. Governs morality, fertility, and the dead (who rest in her womb). Her laws (nso ala) are the foundation of Igbo ethics.
God of thunder and lightning — speaks through thunder, brings justice through lightning strike. In Igbo tradition, a lightning death is a divine judgment.
God of yams and agriculture — the Igbo staple crop deity. The new yam festival (Iwa Ji) is the most important Igbo ceremony.
The Earth Mother of the Akan — the consort of Nyame. Thursday is her day (farmers do not work the earth on Thursday). She receives the dead into her body. More approached than Nyame in daily life.
primary_sources: Rattray, Religion and Art in Ashanti

deities.summary: The great Ashanti priest-magician — called the Golden Stool from the sky, legitimizing Osei Tutu's kingdom. The Stool (Sika Dwa Kofi) became the sacred repository of Ashanti collective soul.
The Zapotec rain and lightning god — the Zapotec parallel of Tlaloc/Chaac. His glyph is a jaguar combined with a serpent-lightning bolt. He controlled rain, agriculture, and time.
The Zapotec corn god — lord of the sustaining food, parallel to the Maya Corn God.

The historical Quetzalcoatl Ce Acatl Topiltzin — the Toltec priest-king of Tula who embodied the god. He was driven out by Tezcatlipoca's trickery and fled east, promising to return. His return was awaited in 1519.
The Maker Above — supreme creator of the Crow people. He created the first people from mud. Benevolent and relatively distant; the Crow approach him through vision quest and the Medicine Wheel.
primary_sources: Frey, The World of the Crow Indians

deities.summary: The Crow trickster-transformer — the one who remade the world from a duck's dive into the primordial waters. Simultaneously creator, teacher, and fool.
The supreme orixa of Candomble — Brazilian transformation of Yoruba Obatala. He creates human bodies at the moment of birth. Wears white, associated with Jesus Christ (syncretically).
primary_sources: Bastide, The African Religions of Brazil

deities.summary: Brazilian sea goddess — transformation of Yoruba Yemoja. Queen of the sea, mother of the orixas. Her festival (February 2) on Brazilian beaches is the most widely attended religious ceremony in Brazil.

Brazilian goddess of fresh water, fertility, love, and wealth — transformation of Yoruba Oshun. Gold, honey, mirrors, and the river. Syncretically identified with Our Lady of the Immaculate Conception.

The Khakass sky god — Father Sky (Cher-Ezi) paired with Mother Earth (Cher-Ezi). The shamanic cosmos of the Yenisei Turks has 16 heavens above and 16 underworld realms below, with our world in between.
The Yakut supreme deity — the White Creator Lord who dwells in the highest eastern heaven. The most benevolent deity; all good things descend from him.

The Real — Ibn Arabi's central divine name. Reality itself is the divine self-disclosure. All existence is Al-Haqq making itself known through its own attributes. The cosmos is the dream of God knowing itself.

The First Intellect emanating from the One — Al-Farabi's Islamic Neoplatonism maps Plotinus's Nous onto the Islamic divine order. The intellect of the First Intellect generates the ten intellects of the planetary spheres.
Founder of Shugendo — the Japanese mountain asceticism that combines Shinto, Buddhist, and Taoist elements. Said to command demons and fly through the air.
The Avatar Buddhas of the mountains — in shinbutsu-shugo (Shinto-Buddhist syncretism), Japanese mountain kami are avatars (gongen) of specific Buddhas. Zao Gongen is the most famous.
The Mordvin/Erzya supreme deity — the great god (Ine-Pas = Great God). Creator of the world. The Mordvin pantheon includes Sky God, Earth Mother, Water Mother, and a host of lesser pas (gods).

The lwa of love, luxury, and feminine power — the Haitian Venus. She loves beautiful things, weeps for humanity's suffering and her own unfulfillment, and wears three wedding rings (for three husbands: Damballa, Ogou, Agwe).

The great snake lwa — the oldest and most venerable. He is the cosmic serpent, the rainbow serpent, the creator who lives in trees and springs. White, pure, ancient beyond words. He and his wife Ayida Wedo are the serpent couple that supports the sky.
The warrior lwa of iron and revolution — the Haitian transformation of Yoruba Ogun. He carries a machete and wears red. Patron of soldiers and revolution; the spirit of Haitian independence.
The cosmic anaconda of Shipibo-Conibo cosmology — the great serpent whose body generates the geometric patterns (kene) that structure reality. Shamanic (ayahuasca) contact reveals her patterns.
The spirit of the Jurema tree (Mimosa hostilis) — the sacred plant deity of the Brazilian Jurema religion. The tree contains the divine drink that allows shamanic journeys.
The Santeria transformation of Yoruba Orunmila — master of Ifa divination. He knows every person's destiny because he witnessed creation. Syncretically identified with Saint Francis of Assisi.
The Santeria Obatala — creator of human bodies, lord of white and purity. Syncretically identified with Our Lady of Mercy (Las Mercedes). The supreme creative principle in Lucumi theology.

The Eye of Ra sent to destroy humanity — almost exterminated them until she was pacified by beer disguised as blood. Her priests were the physicians of ancient Egypt; she both sends plague and heals it.
Sister of Isis and Osiris — the shadow twin of Isis. While Isis represents the visible, Nephthys represents the hidden. She mourns with Isis at Osiris's death. Lady of the House.
The crocodile god — lord of the Nile, fertility, and military power. Associated with the pharaoh's might. His major temple at Kom Ombo shares space with Horus the Elder.
The ram-headed potter god — shapes human bodies and their ka (spiritual double) on his potter's wheel. His temple at Elephantine, at the source of the Nile.
Falcon-headed war god of Thebes — the original patron of the pharaoh's military might before Amun rose to prominence. His fierce power was the epitome of royal military valor.
Moon goddess — the moon itself, driving her chariot across the night sky. She fell in love with Endymion and put him in eternal sleep so she could kiss him each night.
The sun itself — drives his chariot across the sky each day. His son Phaethon borrowed it, lost control, and Zeus had to destroy him with a thunderbolt to save the world.
Goddess of victory — stands beside Zeus, carries a palm branch and wreath. Her winged form was one of the most reproduced images in the ancient world.

God of medicine and healing — son of Apollo, killed by Zeus for raising the dead. His snake-staff (Rod of Asclepius) is still the symbol of medicine. His temples (Asclepia) combined healing, dream-incubation, and religious practice.
The goddess of dawn — the rosy-fingered dawn who opens the gates of heaven for the sun. The most directly surviving PIE *h2ewsos in Greek. She loved mortal men (Tithonus) who aged while she did not.
Necessity — the primordial goddess of inevitability, compulsion, and fate. Even the Moirai are instruments of Ananke. Plato's Spindle of Ananke holds the eight spheres of the cosmos.
Queen of the Roman gods — guardian of the state, women, and marriage. Her anger at Troy persisted through the Aeneid. The Capitoline Triad: Jupiter, Juno, Minerva.
Goddess of wisdom, crafts, and strategic warfare — uniquely Roman; born from Jupiter's head. Patron of teachers, artisans, and doctors. Part of the Capitoline Triad.

deities.earliest_attestation: Republican period; mystery cult from c. 200 BCE
deities.summary: The Roman Dionysus — god of wine and ecstatic release. His mystery cult (Bacchanalia) was suppressed by the Senate in 186 BCE for alleged immorality, then rehabilitated. His mysteries promised afterlife.
The Roman lord of the underworld — Hades in his wealth aspect (Pluto = the wealthy one, for underground riches). His cult was more positive than the Greek Hades — he was a generous patron.
God of the sea and fresh water — not exactly Poseidon's Roman equivalent; he had domain over all water sources, not just the sea. Horse deity as well (Equestrian Neptune).
God of merchants, travelers, communication, and the underworld — Roman Hermes. The most syncretically absorbed deity in the Roman Empire (identified with Celtic Lugus, Germanic Wotan, etc.).
Roman goddess of grain and harvest — her name gives us cereal. Mother of Proserpina. The Aventine triad (Ceres, Liber, Libera) was the plebeian parallel to the Capitoline triad.

The Gorgon whose gaze turns to stone — once a beautiful priestess of Athena, transformed as punishment. Killed by Perseus. Her blood generated Pegasus and the origin of snakes in Libya. Her severed head became Athena's shield.
The identity of the individual soul (Atman) with the cosmic soul (Brahman) — "Tat tvam asi" (That art thou). The central insight of Vedanta.
The emptiness of inherent existence — all phenomena lack a fixed, independent self. Nagarjuna's Madhyamaka philosophy made this the foundation of Mahayana.
The teaching, the truth, the cosmic law — in Buddhism, the second of the Three Jewels. The Buddha's teaching as a living cosmic principle.
The Spirit of God that hovers over the primordial waters before creation — the divine breath/wind as cosmic principle.
The great prophet who was taken to heaven in a fiery chariot without dying — expected to return before the Day of the LORD.
The household gods of Rome — Lares are ancestral protective spirits, Penates are guardians of the pantry. Every Roman household maintained their domestic cult.

The ten-headed demon-king who abducted Sita — the great antagonist of the Ramayana. Also the greatest devotee of Shiva, whose hymn (Shiva Tandava Stotra) he composed. The most theologically complex adversary in Indian religion.
The Buddhist tempter — tempted Siddhartha the night of his enlightenment with desire (daughters), fear (army), and doubt. Lord of the realm of samsara that keeps beings trapped.
The PIE cosmic truth/order — Avestan Asha (opposed by Druj/Lie), Sanskrit Rita (cosmic order). The moral order built into the structure of reality.
Goddess of sin and purification — she eats filth (sexual transgression) from dying confessors. Both causes and cleanses erotic sin.
Seven Serpent — Aztec goddess of corn and sustenance. Seven represents harvest abundance.
She of the Jade Skirt — goddess of rivers and storms, wife of Tlaloc. Destroyed the fourth world with a great flood.
Snake Woman — earth mother and war goddess. She appears carrying an empty cradle, wailing — an omen of war and sacrifice.
Cloud Serpent — god of the hunt and the Milky Way. Father of Quetzalcoatl in some Toltec traditions.
The corn god — the male divine corn, paired with Chicomecóatl. Son of Tlazolteotl in some accounts.
God of death and ruler of Metnal (the lowest underworld) — depicted as a skeletal figure wearing bells. The most feared Mayan deity.
The younger Hero Twin — becomes the moon after defeating the lords of Xibalba alongside his brother Hunahpu.
The elder Hero Twin — becomes the sun after victory in the underworld. Father of the Maize God.
The Earthquake Maker — son of Seven Macaw, boasted he could shake mountains. Defeated by the Hero Twins.
The aged moon goddess — carries a water jar, governs medicine, weaving, and floods. Wife of Itzamna.
Thor's wife — her golden hair was cut off by Loki, then replaced by the dwarves with magical gold. Possibly an earth goddess whose hair is the grain.
The Silent God — son of Odin, second strongest of all gods. Will survive Ragnarok and avenge Odin by killing Fenrir.
Born for revenge — Odin begot him specifically to avenge Baldur's death. Killed Hod the day after his birth. Survives Ragnarok.
God of poetry and eloquence — the skald of the gods, husband of Idun. He who greets the slain at Valhalla.
Son of Baldur — god of justice and reconciliation. The best judge among gods and men, settles all disputes.
God of skiing, hunting, and single combat — invoked on oaths alongside Odin. One of the most ancient Norse deities.
Guardian of the Well of Wisdom at Yggdrasil's root — Odin sacrificed his eye to drink from it. After Mimir's death, Odin preserved his head and consulted it for counsel.
The wisest being ever — created from the saliva of all gods. Killed by dwarves who brewed the Mead of Poetry from his blood.
Sea goddess who collects the drowned in her net — wife of Aegir, mother of the nine wave-daughters. To drown is to be taken by Ran.
Lord of the sea — brews ale for the gods in an enormous cauldron. His nine daughters are the waves.
The goddess of righteous retribution — punishes hubris and undeserved good fortune. The cosmic balancer of excess.
Goddess of fortune and luck — spins the wheel of fate, her gifts are unpredictable. Roman equivalent: Fortuna.
God of the wild, shepherds, and rustic music — half-man, half-goat, son of Hermes. His sudden appearance caused panic.
Goddess of strife — threw the Apple of Discord that caused the Trojan War. In Hesiod, she has two forms: the evil strife (war) and the good strife (competition that drives excellence).
Goddess of the rainbow — divine messenger before Hermes took that role, connecting heaven and earth with her rainbow bridge.
God of dreams — takes human forms within dreams. Son of Hypnos, brother of Thanatos. He who shapes the dream.
Personification of gentle death — twin of Hypnos (Sleep), son of Nyx. The process of dying as divine presence.
God of sleep — twin of Thanatos, lives in the underworld where sun never shines. Even Zeus feared Hypnos.
The shape-shifting sea god who knows all things — must be caught and held through all his transformations to force him to tell the truth.
Necessity — the primordial compulsion. Plato's Spindle of Ananke holds the cosmos. Even Zeus bows to Ananke.
First-born of Kronos and Rhea — the eldest Olympian who tends the eternal hearth flame. She gave up her throne for Dionysus to avoid conflict.
God of medicine killed by Zeus for raising the dead — his temples (Asclepia) practiced healing through dream-incubation. His rod with snake is the symbol of medicine.
The deified Romulus — third member of the archaic Roman triad (Jupiter, Mars, Quirinus). The founder of Rome made divine.
Goddess of fruit trees and orchards — uniquely Roman with no Greek equivalent. Won by Vertumnus after his many disguises.
God of seasons, change, and gardens — shapeshifted through many forms to win Pomona's love.
Goddess of flowers and spring — her festival Floralia (April 28) was a raucous celebration of spring's return.
Roman lord of the underworld and underground wealth — equated with Pluto/Hades. Connected to the Gauls through Caesar.
God of fertility and the eastern desert — depicted ithyphallically, associated with lettuce (considered aphrodisiac). One of the oldest Egyptian deities.
Cat goddess — originally lioness, later domesticated into the gentle cat. Goddess of home, cats, music, and dancing. Her annual festival involved enormous numbers of pilgrims.
Lord of the Memphite necropolis — god of the funerary realm specifically at Memphis. Later merged with Osiris and Ptah as Ptah-Sokar-Osiris.
She Who Loves Silence — the cobra goddess of the Valley of the Kings, protecting the royal tombs.
Cobra goddess of harvest and nourishment — she decided each person's ren (name and fate) at birth. Lady of the granary.
Ram-headed potter god — shapes each human body and its ka (spiritual double) on his divine potter's wheel at conception.
Wife of the Moon God Nanna — mother of Inanna and Utu/Shamash. She pleaded with the divine assembly to spare Ur from destruction; they refused.
primary_sources: Lament for the Destruction of Ur

deities.summary: Goddess of grain, writing, and scribal arts — the original Sumerian divine scribe, patron of all scribes before Nabu took the role.
God of war and agriculture — slays the cosmic serpent Asag and the Anzu bird. The archetype of the champion-hero dragon-slayer in Mesopotamia.
King of the wind-demons — terrifying in appearance but used apotropaically against Lamashtu. His image on amulets warded off the child-killing demon.
Sumerian storm god — the Sumerian parallel of Akkadian Adad. Lord of rain and thunder, son of Enlil.
Great Mother and Lady of the Sacred Mountains — with Enki, fashions humanity from clay. One of the four primary Sumerian deities alongside Anu, Enlil, and Enki.
The divine horsemen twins — physicians of the gods, they rescue the drowning, restore youth, bring light at dawn. The Vedic parallel of the Dioscuri.
The storm troop of Indra — 27 or 49 shining warriors who ride with Indra in battle, wielding lightning. Sons of Rudra.
The howling storm god — dangerous, wild, both the source of disease and its healer. Father of the Maruts. Shiva's direct predecessor in the Vedic tradition.
Lord of wealth and king of the Yakshas — guardian of the north quarter and keeper of all divine treasure. Lives on Mount Kailash near Shiva.
God of erotic love — shoots five flower-tipped arrows that cause specific types of longing. Burned to ash by Shiva's third eye; revived through Parvati's love.
Goddess of the night — sister of Ushas (Dawn). One of the most beautiful hymns in the Rigveda addresses her: she illuminates the night with stars.
The divine craftsman of the gods — fashioned the vajra for Indra, made the first soma cup. The divine smith who shapes the implements of cosmic order.
The divine architect — built Lanka for Ravana, the city of Dwaraka for Krishna, and the vehicles and weapons of all the devas.
The Immovable King — wrathful protector of practice, cannot be moved from truth. Blue, with sword and lasso. Most important of the Five Wisdom Kings in Japanese Shingon Buddhism.
The enlightened layman — the bodhisattva in ordinary life. His thunderous silence in response to Manjushri's question about non-duality is the most celebrated moment in Mahayana literature.
The swift-acting Tara — her right foot is extended, ready to spring into action. Faster to answer prayers than any other deity. Specifically for protection from eight fears.
The People of the Goddess Danu — the divine race of Ireland who defeated the Fomorians, were then defeated by the Milesians, and retreated into the sidhe (fairy mounds).
The Silver Wheel — daughter of Don, mother of the twins Lleu and Dylan (the sons she tried to deny). Her name means "silver wheel" — a moon and fate goddess.
Physician of the Tuatha De Danann — healed the wounded gods in the Well of Slaine. Crafted Nuada's silver arm. Killed his own son Miach out of professional jealousy.
The patient craftsman — Welsh equivalent of Manannán Mac Lir. After losing his kingdom, mastered three crafts to sustain his companions.
The Slavic sky-smith — the divine fire of heaven, smith of the gods. Father of Dazbog (the sun) and Svarozhich (the hearth fire).
The Slavic sun god — giver of wealth and prosperity, son of Svarog. The Slavic solar deity.
The midsummer deity — bonfires, water, ferns, and fertility. His festival (Ivan Kupala) is one of the most widely preserved Slavic pre-Christian celebrations.
Goddess of winter and death — her effigy is drowned or burned at the end of winter to welcome spring and the green god Jarilo.
Gaulish thunder god — the wheel is his sacred symbol. Lucan lists him among the three Gaulish gods who received human sacrifice.
Tribal deity — "god of the tribe/people." Equated by Romans with Mars or Mercury. Required sacrifice by drowning in a cauldron.
The Good Striker — mallet god of the Gauls. His mallet could both kill and revive. Associated with forests and Celtic underworld.
God of war and humanity — the red fierce deity who refused to hide from the primal parents' children. Humans are his food.
God of cultivated food and peace — Rongo and Tu are the twin poles of Maori theology: peace and war.
The moon goddess of Polynesia — weaves tapa cloth on the moon, governs lunar cycles, associated with women's crafts.
The good creator of Vanuatu mythology — opposed by the evil Suqe. Together they made the imperfect world.
The Samoan supreme being — Tagaloa created the sky, then the earth, then sent birds to see if the earth was ready.
The great mountain kami — father of Konohanasakuya-hime (the ancestor of emperors). Lord of all mountains.
Princess of Blossoming Trees — wife of Ninigi (Amaterasu's grandson), divine ancestor of the Japanese imperial line. She proved her fidelity by giving birth in a burning hut.
God of thunder and lightning — depicted as a fearsome figure beating drums made of clouds. Twin of Fujin.
God of wind — carries a large bag of winds. Twin of Raijin. Together they guard the gates of shrines.
Dragon King of the Sea — rules the ocean from his undersea palace Ryugu-jo, controls the tides with magical tide jewels.
Izanami in her death-form — after dying in childbirth, she became queen of Yomi (the underworld), generating 1500 deaths per day to match Izanagi's 1500 births.
The divine ghost-catcher — a scholar who failed the imperial exams and killed himself; appointed King of the Ghosts by the Jade Emperor to compensate.
The Earth God — the most local of all deities. Every neighborhood, field, and building has its own Tu Di Gong who reports annually to the Jade Emperor.
The Kitchen God — watches the family all year and reports to the Jade Emperor before the New Year. His picture is burned to send him to heaven.
Duke of Thunder — strikes those who commit hidden crimes that escape earthly justice. His weapon exposes evil.
Mother of Lightning — the female counterpart of Lei Gong, uses mirrors to create the flashes of lightning that illuminate evil.
The Two Door Gods — divine generals who guard the doors of homes and temples against evil spirits. Based on generals of Tang Emperor Taizong.
Judge Bao — the historical incorruptible Song dynasty judge deified as a divine arbiter, accessible to common people seeking justice.

The Korean Maitreya — the future Buddha who will establish a perfect world. In a primordial contest, Mireuk lost the present world to Shakyamuni through Shakyamuni's trickery, explaining the world's imperfection.
The Birth Grandmother — triple goddess who presides over childbirth, child-rearing, and early childhood. Approached with prayer and rice offerings for health and fertility.
The lwa of the sea — admiral and master of the ocean, husband of Erzulie Freda. He accepts offerings of tiny boats sent out to sea.
Lwa of fresh water, crossroads, and magic — patron of sorcerers and diviners. He lives at the boundary of fresh and salt water.
The first mambo (Vodou priestess) — Ayizan initiates all Vodou priests and priestesses, presides over the beginnings of sacred ceremony.
Orisha of storms, change, the Niger River, and death — she escorts the dead. She IS change: nothing stays the same after Oya passes.
Orisha of disease and healing — expelled by Shango, wandered the world in rags spreading smallpox, now propitiated with gratitude for being spared.
The great trickster of the American Southwest — steals fire, disrupts creation, teaches through chaos and failure. Simultaneously creator and destroyer.
primary_sources: Southwest oral traditions (Navajo, Apache, Paiute, Shoshone)
alternate_names: Old Man Coyote (separate figure in Crow)

deities.summary: Thought Woman (Kokyangwuti) — she thinks things and they come into being. The Hopi first creator who thought the world into existence and helps those in trouble.
The divine mother of corn — the sustainer of life across the Eastern Woodlands. She sacrificed herself so the people could eat.
Mother Earth — Maka is the earth herself. All life comes from her and returns to her womb at death.
The Sun — Grandfather Wi, source of all light, warmth, and life. His strength and endurance are the divine qualities the Lakota admire.
Chief of the Thunderbirds — commands the storms that battle the water serpents (Horned Serpents) lurking in the deep. The cosmic conflict between sky and water.
The prophet-apostle of light — claimed to be the Paraclete promised by Jesus and the successor of Zoroaster, Buddha, and Christ. Executed by the Zoroastrian establishment.
The divine being who descended to fight Darkness — partially defeated, his scattered light-elements became the particles of divine light trapped in matter that constitute human souls.
The Lord of the World in Theosophical teaching — an Ancient of Days who has guided Earth's spiritual evolution for 18.5 million years from his retreat at Shambhala.
primary_sources: Alice Bailey, A Treatise on Cosmic Fire

deities.summary: The great Kushan mother goddess — a synthesis of Anahita, Mesopotamian Nana, and local Central Asian goddesses, widely worshipped from Bactria to Tang China on Silk Road routes.

The deliberately syncretic god — Ptolemy I combined Osiris, Apis, Zeus, Hades, and Asclepius into a single deity for Greek and Egyptian worship. The most deliberately engineered syncretic deity in antiquity.
God of forests and uncultivated land — the liminal deity of the boundary between civilized and wild. Worshipped by slaves and freedmen.

The Great Mother officially adopted by Rome in 204 BCE to win the Second Punic War. Her ecstatic cult with castrated priests (Galli) was the most foreign element ever officially incorporated into Roman religion.
Goddess of Night — mother of Sleep, Death, the Fates, Strife. Even Zeus feared her.
The Earth itself — first to emerge from Chaos. Mother of Uranos, the Titans, the Giants. The ultimate source.
Titan who ruled the cosmos after castrating Uranos — swallowed his children, was overthrown by Zeus.
The primordial sky — castrated by Kronos on Gaia's instruction. Aphrodite born from sea foam around his severed genitals.
God of the sea, earthquakes, and horses — brother of Zeus. His trident strikes cause earthquakes.
Lame god of the forge — made the armor of Achilles, the palaces of the gods, the automata. Thrown from Olympus twice.
Queen of the dead for half the year — her return each spring creates spring. Presides over Eleusinian Mysteries.
Queen of Olympus — goddess of marriage and women, jealous defender of sacred union.
Lord of the dead — not evil, simply absolute. Pluto (the wealthy) for underground riches.
God of wine, ecstasy, theatre — born twice, died and rose in the Orphic version.
King of the Roman gods — sky, thunder, justice. His eagle is Rome's symbol.
God of war and agriculture — father of Romulus. Second most important Roman deity.
Queen of Roman gods — guardian of state, marriage, women.
Goddess of love, beauty, and Rome's divine ancestry — mother of Aeneas.
Goddess of wisdom and craft — Capitoline Triad member with Jupiter and Juno.
God of fire and the forge — the Roman Hephaestus, divine blacksmith.
Creator who spoke the world into being — patron of craftsmen, Lord of Truth.
Lioness of war and plague — sent by Ra to destroy humanity, pacified with beer.
Goddess of love, music, beauty, and joy — the feminine divine pleasure principle.
The sky goddess — her body arches over the earth, stars are her adornments, she swallows the sun each evening and gives birth to it each morning.
The earth god — husband of Nut. His laughter causes earthquakes.
Goddess of love, war, magic, death — receives half the slain. Practitioner of seidr.
Odin's wife — knows all fates but speaks none. She failed to protect Baldur.
The beloved — killed by mistletoe (Loki's mischief). His death begins Ragnarok. Will return after.
One-handed god of oaths — sacrificed his hand binding Fenrir.

deities.earliest_attestation: c. 2700 BCE historical; epic c. 2100 BCE
deities.summary: The great king — 2/3 divine, 1/3 human. His quest for immortality after Enkidu's death is world literature's first great journey.
Queen of the Great Below — her roar is the underworld itself. She stripped Inanna of power at each gate.
Lord of Wind and lord of the divine assembly — sent the flood to destroy humanity.
Sun god of justice — sees all deeds. Gave Hammurabi the law code. Judge of gods and men.
Lord of beginnings, remover of obstacles — elephant-headed son of Shiva and Parvati. Called first in all ceremonies.
Divine lover, teacher of the Gita, cosmic player — the eighth avatar of Vishnu and the supreme deity of Vaishnavism.
The ideal king — seventh avatar of Vishnu. His rescue of Sita from Ravana is the Hindu epic of dharma.
King of the gods in the Vedas — slayer of Vritra, drinker of soma, lord of storms. His prominence later diminished.
Bodhisattva of wisdom — wields the flaming sword that cuts through ignorance.
He who vowed not to enter nirvana until all hells are empty — goes into the worst hell realms to liberate beings.
The most widely worshipped deity in China — goddess of mercy, hears all cries. Originally male Avalokitesvara.
The supreme ruler of heaven — celestial bureaucrat, presides over all gods and the fate of all humans.
Supreme solar goddess — withdrew to cave (solar eclipse), lured out by laughter. Ancestor of all emperors.
Storm god and hero — expelled from heaven, slew the eight-headed dragon Yamata-no-Orochi, found the sacred sword.
Witness to creation — knows every person's destiny. Through Ifa divination, his knowledge is accessed.
Creator of human bodies — Olodumare's deputy who molds each body from clay. Lord of purity, white, and the head.
Mother of Waters — source of all rivers, mother of most orishas. The cosmic feminine water principle.
War-sun god who needs human heart-sacrifice to continue rising — patron of the Mexica.
Rain god with goggle eyes — both life-giver and demander of child sacrifice. His paradise (Tlalocan) receives those drowned.
The Feathered Serpent — civilizing deity, creator of humans from bone and blood, god of wind and Venus.
Smoking Mirror — the omniscient adversary who sees all through his obsidian mirror, rival of Quetzalcoatl.
The Horned One — lord of animals, wild nature, and the boundary between worlds.
Triple goddess of poetry, smithcraft, and healing. Her eternal flame at Kildare was kept by 19 nuns.
The Irish sea god — his four children were transformed into swans for 900 years by their jealous stepmother.
primary_sources: The Fate of the Children of Lir

deities.summary: Slavic thunder god — wielder of the stone axe, eternal foe of Veles. His battle with Veles IS the weather.
Slavic god of the underworld, magic, cattle, and wealth — the cosmic serpent who eternally battles Perun.
Slavic earth and fate goddess — weaves fate, protects women and weaving. The only female deity named in the Primary Chronicle.
The divine horse queen — wrongly accused of killing her son, punished with carrying visitors on her back like a horse.
Prince of Dyfed who traded places with Arawn, lord of the Otherworld for a year. The mortal who becomes divine through otherworldly encounter.
The Inca sun god — father of the ruling dynasty, supreme state deity. His golden disk in Coricancha was the most sacred object in the empire.
The creator and wandering teacher — made the world, destroyed it with flood, remade it, then walked into the sea.
The Earth Mother — she who shakes (earthquakes as her stretching). The most persistently worshipped deity from Inca times to today.
The Akan supreme being — Nyame the sky god, creator and sustainer. His symbol is the Adinkra Gye Nyame (Except God).
The Fon trickster-messenger — the youngest of the vodun, who speaks all languages and opens all roads.
The dual creator — Mawu (moon/female) and Lisa (sun/male) together make all things. The supreme dual being of Fon theology.
The Finnish sky-father and thunder god — his rumbling is thunder, his sparks are lightning.
The eternal sage-shaman — born of the primordial waters, the first to exist. His songs create reality.
The Bounteous/Holy Spirit — the divine emanation through which Ahura Mazda acts in the world. The spirit of good.
Good Mind — the first and greatest Amesha Spenta. He received the Gathas from Zarathustra.
Infinite Light — the three negatives of Kabbalistic theology: Ain (Nothing), Ain Soph (Infinite Nothing), Ain Soph Aur (Infinite Light).
The Lesser YHWH — the highest angel, possibly the transformed Enoch, scribe of heaven, guardian of the Throne.
Earth Mother — locked in embrace with Ranginui (Sky Father) until their children forced them apart. The earth itself.
Sky Father — his tears of grief for Papatuanuku are rain. Their separation is the original act of creation.
God of agriculture, fertility, and peace — his season (Makahiki) was four months of peace and games.
The cosmic breath/consciousness — Sila is the air, the weather, and the intelligence permeating all things.
primary_sources: Inuit oral tradition (Rasmussen, Intellectual Culture of the Iglulik Eskimos)

deities.summary: The Latvian fate goddess — she decides each person's fate at birth. The cuckoo is her sacred bird; her call counts remaining years.
Lithuanian thunder god — slayer of the cosmic serpent, the clearest surviving PIE thunder-god form.
Goddess of Night and mother of primordial forces.
Goddess of the rosy-fingered dawn.
The moon herself, driving her chariot across the sky.
The Titan sun — sees all, cannot be deceived.
Necessity — the inescapable cosmic compulsion.
Goddess of victory.
The third being from Chaos — cosmic attraction that enables creation.
The final monster who challenged Zeus for the cosmos.
The Gorgon whose gaze turns to stone.
Divine smith, lame craftsman of the gods.
Two-faced god of doorways, beginnings, and time.
God of boundaries and property limits — even Jupiter must yield to him.
God of the sea and all waters.
God of fire and forge.
The Roman Kronos — god of the Golden Age.
Goddess of abundance and harvest — wife of Saturn.
Goddess of prophecy and childbirth.
Goddess of doorposts and hinges — Janus gave her power to repel evil from thresholds.
Divine scribe, keeper of the cosmic record.
The living pharaoh as hawk-god, son of Osiris and Isis.
God of chaos, storms, the desert — slayer of Osiris, defender of Ra.
Shadow twin of Isis — the hidden, mourning aspect of the divine feminine.
The self-created — the first to emerge from Nun.
The supreme solar king who journeys through the underworld each night.
The scarab who pushes the morning sun.
The crocodile god of the Nile — lethal fertility.
Falcon-headed war god of Thebes.
Lord of the divine assembly who sent the flood.
Sky father — remote supreme of the Mesopotamian gods.
God of the underworld and plague — married Ereshkigal.
Divine scribe, god of writing and wisdom.
Akkadian storm god — the Akkadian Ishkur.
Grain god of the Philistines and Canaanites — father of Baal in some traditions.
Ferocious warrior goddess — waded through blood to her waist.
Death — Baal's great enemy. They struggle eternally.
The Sea — Baal's first great enemy, ruler of chaos waters.
The storm king who slays Vritra and drinks soma.
The sacred fire who carries offerings to the gods.
The preserver who descends as avatars.
The destroyer-transformer-ascetic supreme deity of Shaivism.
The invincible warrior goddess who defeated Mahishasura.
The fierce liberator — tongue out, standing on Shiva.
Lord of beginnings — elephant head, remover of obstacles.
War god of youthful divine power, commander of the divine army.
Bodhisattva of wisdom who wields the flaming sword.
Lord of death in Buddhist iconography — the judge of karma.
Solar goddess whose sacred mirror is the imperial regalia.
The moon god who killed the food goddess, creating day and night.
The male creator who purified himself after fleeing Yomi.
God of forests and birds who separated sky and earth.
God of the sea and father of all fish.
The trickster demigod who fished up islands and slowed the sun.
Goddess of volcanoes and the creator of new land.
The thunder king — deified fourth ruler of Oyo.
God of iron, war, and labor who opened the paths.
The trickster who speaks all languages and must be propitiated first.
Orisha of storms and death who escorts souls.
The great trickster who disrupts creation to teach.
She who gave the sacred pipe and seven rites.
The Great Mystery — the sacred power pervading all things.
Virgin keeper of the sacred hearth — first-born, last to receive offerings.
The spring maiden who became queen of the dead — the cycle of seasons is her story.
Goddess of strife — threw the Apple of Discord that launched the Trojan War.
God of dreams who takes human form within the dream.
Goddess of grain whose grief for Persephone created winter.
Triple goddess of magic, crossroads, and the moon.

facets.core_claim: The Monad is beyond name, form, gender, and category. It does not create directly; it self-contemplates, and from that contemplation the Aeons emerge. It is the silence before the first word.
Yaldabaoth, hidden away in a cloud by Sophia, fashions the material cosmos in imitation of the Pleroma he has never seen. He populates it with seven Archons and rules as if he were alone.
Declares 'I am God and there is no other.' Rebuked from above: 'You are wrong, Samael — blind god.' This is the figure mainstream religion has mistaken for the Most High.

facets.facet_name: Creator of the heavens and the earth
facets.core_claim: In Genesis 1, Yahweh-Elohim fashions the cosmos from formless void. In orthodox reading, this is the ultimate God creating from nothing. In Gnostic reading, it is the Demiurge ordering pre-existing chaos in ignorance of the Pleroma.
'I am the Lord thy God; thou shalt have no other gods before me. For I the Lord thy God am a jealous God.' (Exodus 20). Demands exclusive worship, blood sacrifice, and unflinching obedience.
Yahweh enters into covenant with Abraham, Isaac, Jacob; gives Torah to Moses; binds himself to Israel as their God.

El Elyon presides over the divine council and apportions the nations among the sons of God (Deut 32:8-9 in the older textual tradition). He is the true Most High in the Gnostic and older-Canaanite reading.
Each night Set stands at the prow of Ra's barque and spears Apophis, the chaos-serpent. He is necessary chaos in service of cosmic order.

facets.facet_name: Murderer of Osiris, usurper of the throne
facets.core_claim: Set murders his brother Osiris out of jealousy, dismembers him, claims the throne. Embodies disorder and the violation of ma'at.

facets.facet_name: Lord of storms and the red desert
facets.core_claim: Set rules the red lands beyond the cultivated Nile valley — the desert, foreign nations, the wild places.
Marduk slays Tiamat, the primordial chaos-mother, splits her body to form heaven and earth, and is exalted by the gods with fifty names.
Marduk takes up arms when no other god will face Tiamat. Armed with thunderbolt and the four winds, he defeats her.
Inanna is the bride of Dumuzi, the principle of erotic and fertile love that drives the renewal of the world.
Inanna rides into battle on lions; her hymns celebrate her as the destroyer of cities and the breaker of armies.

Inanna descends through seven gates of the underworld, stripped at each gate of a divine attribute, until she stands naked before Ereshkigal. Killed and hung on a hook; revived by Enki's intervention; returns transformed.
Inanna is the Queen of Heaven, ruler over the morning and evening star, present at the divine council.
Zeus is the bright sky personified — the Indo-European Dyaus, the shining father. He presides over the council of the gods on Olympus.
In Aeschylus's Prometheus Bound, Zeus is depicted as a recent tyrant who seized the throne from Kronos and punishes Prometheus for giving fire to humans. This is the Demiurge-aspect of Zeus.
Zeus enforces oaths. Those who swear falsely by Zeus are struck by his thunderbolt or pursued by the Furies.
Zeus wields the thunderbolt forged by the Cyclopes. Storm and rain are his domain.
Prometheus steals fire from the gods in a fennel-stalk and gives it to humanity. For this he is chained to a rock in the Caucasus, where an eagle eats his liver each day — and it regrows each night.
Odin is All-Father, head of the Aesir, ruler from Hliðskjálf where he sees all the worlds.
Odin walks the worlds in disguise — wide-brimmed hat, blue cloak, single eye — testing kings, learning wisdom, gathering knowledge.
Odin sacrifices an eye at Mimir's well to drink and gain wisdom. He learns the nine songs and seizes the mead of poetry.

facets.facet_name: The Self-Sacrificed (Hung on the World Tree)
facets.core_claim: 'I know that I hung on a windswept tree nine long nights, wounded by spear, given to Odin, myself to myself.' (Hávamál 138). He seizes the runes.
Odin gathers half the slain warriors to Valhalla, where they feast and fight and prepare for Ragnarök.
Loki repeatedly extracts the Aesir from disasters of their own making — and sometimes creates them. He brings them Mjölnir, Sleipnir, Gungnir, and other treasures, often through cunning theft.
Loki contrives Baldr's death by tricking blind Höðr into throwing the mistletoe-dart. For this he is bound beneath a venom-dripping serpent.
Bound beneath a serpent whose venom drips on his face. At Ragnarök he breaks free and leads the forces of chaos against the gods.
Shiva dissolves the manifest universe at the end of each cosmic cycle. Destruction is the precondition for renewal.
Shiva sits in eternal meditation on Mount Kailash, ash-smeared, withdrawn from the world, the prototype of all yogis.
Shiva as Dakshinamurti faces south, sits beneath a banyan tree, and transmits the highest knowledge through silence to aged sages.
Shiva as Linga is the generative principle of the cosmos. As Nataraja, he dances the universe into being and out of being simultaneously.
Shiva in his terrible aspect, haunting cremation grounds, attended by dogs, embodying the fearsome necessity of dissolution.
On the battlefield of Kurukshetra, Krishna reveals to Arjuna that he is the supreme Self, the source of all gods and worlds. He teaches the paths of action, devotion, and knowledge.
Krishna reveals to Arjuna his Vishvarupa — the cosmic form containing all beings, all worlds, all time. 'I am become Time, the destroyer of worlds.'
Krishna of Vrindavan plays his flute and the gopis come; Radha is his eternal beloved. The lila of divine love is the deepest theology.
Baby Krishna steals butter, mounts the hood of Kaliya the serpent, plays pranks on his mother. The trickster aspect of the supreme.
Quetzalcoatl descended to give humans corn, calendar, writing, the arts. He opposed blood sacrifice and was exiled by Tezcatlipoca.
Quetzalcoatl as Ehecatl is the wind that precedes the rains, the breath of the divine in the world.
Quetzalcoatl descends to Mictlan to retrieve the bones of past humanity and restores them to life with his own blood.
Sailing east on his raft of serpents, Quetzalcoatl prophesied his return in a One Reed year.
Tezcatlipoca rules the night sky and the stars. From this vantage he sees all and judges kings.
Through his smoking obsidian mirror, Tezcatlipoca sees the inner thoughts of all beings and the unfolding of fate.
Tezcatlipoca exiled Quetzalcoatl from Tula through trickery — sending him away with a mirror in which Quetzalcoatl saw his own face and was shamed.
Tezcatlipoca grants and revokes the right to rule. Tezcatlipoca-priests examined and tested new kings.

facets.facet_name: The Fallen Aeon (Mother of the Demiurge)
facets.core_claim: Sophia, last of the Aeons, attempts to create without her consort. Her error produces Yaldabaoth. She falls into the lower regions and must be recovered.
Sophia descends to recover the divine sparks trapped in humanity. With the Christ-Aeon, she effects the return of the lost light to the Pleroma.

Persephone is abducted by Hades and taken into the underworld. She eats the pomegranate seeds and is bound thereafter to return for part of each year. Her descent and return is the seasonal cycle made theological — and the central mystery of Eleusis.
Persephone reigns as queen beside Hades. The maiden becomes queen; the abducted becomes sovereign. The pattern of integration of the feared by way of becoming it.

The eternal Logos — the divine Word through whom all things were made — takes on human form to teach, heal, and reveal the Father. John 1:1–14. The descended-teacher archetype in its central Western form.

Christ is crucified — sacrificed on the wood of the cross, pierced by the spear of Longinus, descending into death. In Trinitarian theology this is 'God offering God'; in Pauline reading, the atonement; in mystical reading, the ego dying so the true Self can rise. The supreme Western instance of the sacrificed-on-tree archetype.

On the third day Christ rises. In esoteric tradition, the Harrowing of Hell: Christ descends into the underworld to free the just souls before returning. The rising-from-death motif that links Christ to every dying-rising god in the comparative cluster.

Colossians 1:15–17: 'He is the image of the invisible God, the firstborn of all creation; in him all things hold together.' The Cosmic Christ encompasses all that is — structurally identical to the role Krishna assigns himself in Bhagavad Gita 11.

Christ will return at the end of the age — Parousia — to judge the living and the dead. The Western form of the returning-savior archetype shared with Kalki, Maitreya, the Mahdi, Saoshyant, the returning Quetzalcoatl, and the once-and-future Arthur.

Hermes Trismegistus reveals the path of gnosis through cosmic understanding. The Emerald Tablet's 'as above, so below' is the core operating principle of the Western esoteric tradition. The synthesis of Greek Hermes and Egyptian Thoth produced the single most influential figure of Renaissance and post-Renaissance occultism.

Hermes (via the Greek inheritance) is the guide of souls between worlds, the messenger who crosses every threshold. In Hermetic literature, the same function elevated: guide of the soul on its ascent through the spheres back to its source.

*Dyḗus Ph₂tḗr is the bright daytime sky personified, addressed as father. He presides over the divine council. Every Indo-European sky-father — Zeus, Jupiter, Tyr/Týr, Dyaus, Tiwaz — is a linguistically continuous descendant of this single root deity.

*Perkʷunos wields the thunder-weapon (stone axe in earlier reconstruction, hammer/thunderbolt in daughter traditions). The root from which Thor, Indra, Zeus-as-storm, Jupiter Tonans, Perun, Perkūnas, and Pērkons all derive.

The PIE storm-god strikes down the serpent who has dammed up the cosmic waters, releasing the rivers and restoring cosmic order. This narrative is preserved nearly verbatim across daughter traditions: Indra vs. Vritra, Thor vs. Jörmungandr, Zeus vs. Typhon, Apollo vs. Python, Perun vs. Veles, Tarhunna vs. Illuyanka, Thraētaona vs. Aži Dahāka.

*H₂éwsōs is the dawn personified — daughter of the sky-father, eternally young, drawn across the sky by horses. The root from which Uṣas, Ēōs, Aurōra, Aušrinė, and Ēostre all descend. The Christian feast of Easter preserves her name.

Trito, the Third, slays the three-headed serpent who has stolen the primordial cattle and recovers the herd. This narrative — warrior recovers stolen wealth from the monster — is the founding myth of the Indo-European warrior class. Heracles vs. Geryon, Heracles vs. Cacus, Thor vs. Hrungnir, Trita vs. Viśvarūpa, Thraētaona vs. Aži Dahāka all preserve it.

facets.facet_name: Fire as Messenger Between Worlds (PIE root)
facets.core_claim: The sacrificial fire is itself divine — the visible messenger that carries the offering from human altar to heavenly recipients. Preserved most fully in Vedic Agni; the linguistic cognates (Latin ignis, Slavic ogon', Hittite Agniš) testify to the PIE root.
Vishnu sustains the cosmos across each yuga cycle. While Brahma creates and Shiva dissolves, Vishnu maintains. The principle of preservation in the Hindu trimurti.

Whenever dharma declines, Vishnu descends as an avatar. The Bhagavad Gita 4.7–8: 'Whenever there is a decline of dharma and a rise of adharma, then I send forth myself.' Ten classical avatars: Matsya, Kurma, Varaha, Narasimha, Vamana, Parashurama, Rama, Krishna, Buddha, Kalki.

facets.facet_name: Narayana — Sleeping on the Cosmic Ocean
facets.core_claim: Vishnu reclines on Shesha the cosmic serpent atop the ocean of milk, between cosmic cycles. From his navel grows the lotus from which Brahma emerges to create the next universe. In this aspect, Vishnu is the unmanifest Source from which manifestation springs.
Indra wields the vajra (thunderbolt) and rides the elephant Airavata. King of Svarga, the heavenly realm. The most-praised deity of the Rigveda; storm-warrior of the Indo-European root.

Vritra the serpent has dammed the cosmic waters and withheld them from the world. Indra, fortified by soma, strikes Vritra with the vajra and releases the waters. The Vedic preservation of the PIE storm-god-slays-water-serpent myth.

Indra is empowered to kill Vritra only after drinking enormous quantities of soma — the sacred plant-juice of the Vedic cult, cognate with Iranian haoma. The deity who needs the ritual substance to perform his cosmic function.

In the Puranic period, Indra is repeatedly humbled — by Krishna (Govardhana incident), by sages who curse him for adultery (Ahalya), by demons who occasionally seize his throne. He retains his title but loses his pre-eminence. The Vedic high-god demoted to a jealous, fallible chief-of-the-lesser-rulers — a structural parallel to the Gnostic reading of Yahweh/Yaldabaoth.
Varuna sees every oath sworn and binds with his noose anyone who swears falsely. The dread enforcer of sworn agreements — the Vedic parallel to Zeus Horkios, Yahweh-as-covenantal, and Mitra.
Varuna is the sovereign of rta — the cosmic order, truth, the way things rightly are. Cognate concept with Egyptian ma'at, Iranian asha, Chinese tao, and Greek logos/dike.
Varuna sees the flight of every bird, every wink of every eye. Originally the all-encompassing sky itself. The Vedic precursor to the omniscient sovereign of later monotheisms.

Mitra is the sworn agreement made divine. He guards alliances and punishes their breach. Paired with Varuna: Mitra is the friendly side of contract; Varuna the dread side. Invoked in the 1380 BCE Mitanni-Hittite treaty as oath-witness — one of the oldest Indo-European deity-names in any document.

In the Iranian development, Mithra becomes mediator between Ahura Mazda and humanity, associated with the sun. In the late Roman Mithraic mysteries, Mithras the bull-slayer is the savior of initiates. The Indo-Iranian contract-deity transformed into a salvific figure.

facets.facet_name: Lord of the Dead, Judge of Souls
facets.core_claim: Yama receives every soul at death, weighs its deeds against the ledger kept by his scribe Chitragupta, and assigns rebirth or hell-realm accordingly. King of the southern direction.

Yama is the first being who chose to die, and by dying opened the path that every subsequent being would walk. His Avestan cognate Yima (Jamshid) is preserved instead as the first king of the golden age — a fork in the Indo-Iranian myth.

Agni is the divine priest who carries every offering from the human altar to the recipient god. The Rigveda opens with 'Agni I praise, the priest set forward, the divine ministrant of the sacrifice.' Fire is the mediator that makes all other Vedic rituals possible.

Agni is two-headed: the household hearth that warms and cooks, and the sacrificial altar-fire that carries the offering up. Both faces are one deity. The same root divinity that becomes Vesta/Hestia in the Greco-Roman world.

Kali destroys what must end. She drinks the blood of Raktabija before it touches the ground (where each drop would spawn a new demon). Her destruction is not malice — it is the dissolution without which renewal cannot come.

In Tantric Shakta theology, Kali is the supreme reality itself — the formless brought into fearsome form. To devotees who can face her terror, she is the most direct path to liberation: she destroys the ego, not the soul.
Kāla is time; Kālī is the feminine of time — time-as-mother. All cycles of arising and dissolving are her movement. In her fullest theology she is identical with Brahman-Nirguna in feminine voice.

facets.facet_name: The Returning Savior (End of the Kali Yuga)
facets.core_claim: At the end of the Kali Yuga, when dharma is at its lowest, Kalki will appear on a white horse, sword in hand, to destroy the kingdom of adharma and inaugurate the next Satya Yuga. The Hindu expression of the universal returning-savior archetype.

Two hundred Watchers under Shemyaza descended to Mount Hermon, swore a mutual oath to do the deed together so no single one would bear the guilt, and took human wives. For this transgression of the divine order they were bound in the abyss until the day of judgment.

The Watchers taught humanity arts the divine order had withheld: metallurgy (Azazel), enchantments (Shemyaza), astrology (Baraqʾel), pharmacology (Kasdeya), the cutting of roots. From the Enochic perspective, these are not gifts but corruptions. From a Promethean perspective, the parallel is exact: gods bring knowledge to humans against the high god's will, and are punished for it. The Enochic tradition frames it as transgression; Gnostic and Promethean traditions frame the same act as liberation.

Azazel taught humans to make swords, daggers, shields, and breastplates, and taught women to adorn themselves with cosmetics and bracelets. The smith-god / weapon-teacher archetype, in its negatively-valenced Enochic form.

Raphael binds Azazel hand and foot and casts him into the pit Dudael in the desert, with rough and jagged rocks piled on him. He is to remain there until the final judgment, when he will be cast into the fire. The bound-rebel-god motif.

On Yom Kippur the high priest lays Israel's sins on the head of a goat and releases it 'to Azazel' in the wilderness. On one reading, the sins return annually to the angelic figure whose fall introduced corruption — symbolic restoration of cosmic balance via the wilderness power.

The Nephilim are physical giants — described as so much larger than ordinary humans that Israelite spies in Canaan say 'we were like grasshoppers in our own sight, and so we were in theirs.' The category includes Rephaim, Anakim, Emim, Zamzummim, and Gibborim ('mighty ones,' Genesis 6:4).

The Nephilim, growing beyond all measure, consumed the produce of all human labor, then consumed humans, then consumed each other. The corruption of the earth by their violence is the explicit Enochic cause of the Flood. The Flood is God's reset of a creation overrun by the offspring of the Watchers.

facets.facet_name: Spirits of the Dead Nephilim — Origin of Demons
facets.core_claim: 1 Enoch 15:8–11: when the Nephilim are killed in the Flood, their hybrid spirits cannot enter the rest of the dead, because they are neither fully spirit nor fully flesh. They roam the earth as 'evil spirits' — and this, in the Enochic theory, is the origin of every demon, every unclean spirit, and every possession recorded in later tradition. The Gospels' demons are, on this reading, the disembodied Nephilim still wandering.

Enoch is taken bodily to heaven without tasting death. He tours the cosmos under angelic guidance, is shown the Watchers in their prison, intercedes (unsuccessfully) on their behalf, and is given vast astronomical and eschatological knowledge to bring back to humanity. The ascended-seer archetype.

In 3 Enoch and Merkavah mysticism, Enoch is transformed into Metatron, the highest of the angels — given seventy names, enthroned at God's right hand, called 'the lesser YHWH' (yhwh ha-katan). A controversial figure in rabbinic tradition: the elevation of a human to near-divine status sits uneasily with strict monotheism. The pattern of human → divine intermediary central to Christology in Christian tradition.

facets.facet_name: Head of the Divine Council on Mount Lel
facets.core_claim: El sits on his throne at the source of the two rivers, head of the puḥru (assembly) of the gods, allotting kingship and offspring.
El is bāniyu binwāti, 'creator of created things' — the originating father, though no longer the active world-shaper.
In the Legend of Kirta, El grants a son to the desperate king. He is the source of dynastic legitimacy.

facets.facet_name: Mother of the Seventy Sons of El
facets.core_claim: Asherah is consort of El, mother of the seventy sons of god — the divine council itself.
Asherah is venerated as a sacred tree or wooden pole (the asherah-pole). Kuntillet Ajrud and Khirbet el-Qom inscriptions invoke 'YHWH and his Asherah.'
Baal rides the clouds, wields the thunder-mace, brings the rains that make the land fertile.
Baal defeats Yam (Sea / Chaos) with the mace Ayamur and is acclaimed king. The IE dragon-slaying myth in West Semitic form.
Baal is killed by Mot (Death), descends to the underworld, and is restored — the seasonal pattern of vegetation.
Mot is the gaping mouth that swallows even gods. His destruction by Anat allows the rains to return.
Yam claims kingship over the gods and is defeated by Baal. The cosmic-serpent/water-chaos archetype.
Anat slaughters her enemies wading in blood; descends in grief and rage to find Baal; tears Mot apart with her own hands. Eros and Thanatos in one figure.
The rpum are the deified royal dead of Ugarit, summoned to ritual feasts (marziḥu) to bless the living king and the land. Their cult is centered on Mt Hermon / Bashan.
In one of the two main registers, the rpum / Rephaim are the giant warrior-kings of the pre-Israelite Levant.

The Ugaritic rpum cult is geographically centered on Bashan and Mt Hermon — exactly the territory the Bible assigns to King Og (Deut 3) and exactly where 1 Enoch 6 places the descent of the Watchers. This is a single religious-geographic complex viewed from three angles.

facets.facet_name: Mother of the Tuatha Dé Danann
facets.core_claim: Danu is the ancestress of the Tuatha Dé Danann — the divine race of pre-Christian Ireland.

Danu is the Celtic reflex of PIE *deh₂nu, 'the flowing one' — the same goddess-name that survives as the hydronyms Danube, Don, Dnieper, Dniester, Donets, and that names the Vedic Dānu, mother of the Dānavas.
The Dagda is 'Eochaid the All-Father,' chieftain of the Tuatha Dé, possessor of the cauldron of plenty.

facets.facet_name: The Club That Kills and Revives
facets.core_claim: The Dagda's lorg mór kills with one end and revives with the other — destruction and restoration in a single instrument.
Lugh declares to the Tuatha Dé that he masters every craft — when each is named he claims it, until they admit him as Samildánach.
At Mag Tuired, Lugh slings a stone through Balor's evil eye, slaying his own monstrous grandfather. The IE chaos-slayer pattern in Celtic form.

facets.facet_name: Triple Goddess of War and Fate
facets.core_claim: The Morrígan appears as raven over the battlefield, choosing who falls. Her triple form (Badb, Macha, Nemain) personifies war's many faces.
The Morrígan offers herself as lover to Cú Chulainn; refused, she becomes his adversary. The sovereignty-goddess who tests the hero.
The Fomorians are the older, monstrous race that the Tuatha Dé defeat at Mag Tuired to establish the cultivated order of Ireland.
The Fomorians appear as one-eyed, one-armed, one-legged monstrous giants from beneath the sea — the chaos-form of the older order.
Balor's single eye destroys what it looks upon — kept closed by lieutenants. His grandson Lugh slays him. The Kronos-Zeus pattern in Celtic form.

Through his thirteen wives Kashyapa fathers the devas (via Aditi), daityas (via Diti), danavas (via Danu), nāgas (via Kadru), and many other classes of being. Every contending race shares him as common father.
Kashyapa is one of the seven primordial seers (Saptarṣi) whose vision originates the Vedas.
Aditi is the mother of Varuna, Mitra, Bhaga, Indra (in some lists), Vishnu (in later layers) — the unbounded source of the bright gods.
Aditi's name literally means 'unbounded' — the privative of 'bound.' She is the formless background against which the bounded devas appear.
Diti, the 'bound' counterpart to Aditi, bears the daityas. After Indra kills her sons she vows revenge; her revenge is thwarted but produces the Maruts.
Dānu in Rigveda is the personification of the primordial waters, PIE *deh₂nu, directly cognate with Old Irish Danu, Avestan dānu, and the river-names Danube / Don / Dnieper / Dniester / Donets.
RV 1.32: 'The mother of Vṛtra, Dānu, lay like a cow with her calf.' The first attested IE dragon-mother.
Dānu, by Kashyapa, is the mother of the Dānavas — the asura-clan that opposes the devas.
Daksha gives his thirteen daughters to Kashyapa. Every class of cosmic being is therefore his grandchild — devas, asuras, daityas, danavas, nāgas, garuḍas alike.
The Daityas are the older, mighty divine race repeatedly defeated by the devas — the Vedic Titans.
Unlike Christian devils, the Daityas include moral exemplars — Prahlada the supreme devotee, Bali the just king. The asura tradition cannot be collapsed into pure evil.

The Dānavas are descended from Dānu through Kashyapa. They are called amānuṣa ('unhuman'). Their structural parallel — and linguistic cognate — with the Celtic Tuath Dé Danann is one of the strongest goddess-cognate cases in IE comparative mythology.
Maya Dānava, the chief architect of the asuras, surpasses even Vishvakarma in skill. The Dānavas dwell in Bila Svarga (subterranean paradises).
Vṛtra is the coiled serpent who encloses the mountains and withholds the waters. Indra's slaying of him with the vajra is the foundational IE combat-myth.
Vṛtra's defining function is enclosure — he 'wraps' the waters. Slaying him releases life-giving flow. The IE dragon as obstacle-to-fertility.
Numbers 13:33 makes the spies say of the Anakim: 'we were in our own sight as grasshoppers, and so we were in their sight.' Deuteronomy 2:11 explicitly identifies them as a sub-group of the Rephaim.
Numbers 13:22 names them: Sheshai, Ahiman, Talmai — the three giant rulers of Hebron. Caleb destroys them (Joshua 15:14).

facets.facet_name: Iy-ʿAnaq in the Egyptian Execration Texts
facets.core_claim: The Egyptian Middle Kingdom Execration Texts (c. 19th–18th c. BCE) curse 'Iy-ʿanaq' — a Canaanite people whose three rulers (Erum, Abiyamimu, and another) bear Semitic names. Most scholars (Albright, Wright, Mazar) identify these as the same Anakim later confronted by Israel. Papyrus Anastasi I (13th c. BCE) describes Canaanite warriors of exceptional stature on Egyptian travel routes. This is the strongest extra-biblical historical evidence for the giant-clan tradition.

Deuteronomy 3:11: 'For only Og king of Bashan remained of the remnant of the Rephaim. Behold, his bedstead was a bedstead of iron... nine cubits its length, and four cubits its breadth.' Talmud Niddah 61a makes him the grandson of Shemhazai the fallen Watcher — explicitly threading him into the Genesis 6 / Enochic lineage.

facets.facet_name: Giant King at Mt Hermon / Bashan
facets.core_claim: Og's kingdom in Bashan — overlapping the Golan, Mt Hermon, and the territory of the Ugaritic rpum cult — is precisely the place where 1 Enoch 6 locates the descent of the Watchers. The three traditions converge on a single geography.
Moabites called them Emim, Ammonites called them Zamzummim, Israelites called them Rephaim. They include the Anakim (Deut 2:11). Goliath and his brothers are their Philistine remnant (1 Chr 20).
In Isaiah 14:9, Psalm 88:10, Job 26:5, the word rephaim denotes the shades of the dead. The dual sense — giants AND deified dead — is inherited directly from the Ugaritic rpum.
*Manu is the first man and first priest, who establishes the cosmic order by sacrificing his twin *Yemo.

*Yemo is sacrificed by *Manu; from his dismembered body the cosmos is constructed. This is the PIE prototype of cosmos-from-sacrificed-being — surviving in Norse Ymir, Vedic Puruṣa, and (via Tocharian transmission?) Chinese Pangu.
Because *Yemo is the first to die, he becomes the king of the dead. Reflexes: Vedic Yama, Avestan Yima (in his post-fall aspect), Norse Ymir (whose body is the world of the dead).

RV 10.90: the gods sacrifice Puruṣa, and from his body arise the worlds and the four varnas. The Rigvedic version of the PIE Manu/Yemo cosmogony — and the most explicit, datable instance of the cosmos-from-sacrificed-being pattern.
In later Sāṃkhya, Puruṣa is the pure consciousness that contemplates Prakṛti (nature/matter). The cosmogonic figure becomes a metaphysical principle.

Ṛta is not a deity but the order against which deities are measured. From PIE *h₂er- 'to fit / join properly,' it is cognate with Avestan aša (cosmic order) and conceptually parallel to Egyptian ma'at — three independent attestations of impersonal cosmic order as the highest principle.
Idjair dwells in the Milky Way and is the father of all Wandjinas, withdrawn from direct world-making and acting through his son Wallungunder.

facets.facet_name: Creator of Earth and First Humans (the Gyorn Gyorn)
facets.core_claim: Wallungunder, son of Idjair, created the earth and all life on it, and created the first humans — the Gyorn Gyorn (Gwion Gwion) people.
Wallungunder returned to Idjair and brought back more Wandjinas to give the Gyorn Gyorn people the Law to live by.

facets.facet_name: Rain-Maker and Fertility-Giver of the Worrorra
facets.core_claim: Namarali brings the wet-season rains, the lightning, and the cyclones that renew the land. The body covered in dots represents falling rain.
The Wandjina has no mouth because verbal speech is unnecessary for a being whose power acts directly by will. Those who break the Law may be punished by flood, lightning, or cyclone.
Primary Wandjina figure of the Ngarinyin people.
Primary Wandjina figure of the Wunambal people; the Wunambal name for Wandjina generally is Gulingi.
Wunggurr is the Kimberley reflex of the broader pan-Aboriginal Rainbow Serpent creator-being.
facets.interpretation_notes: The Rainbow Serpent parallel to the cosmic-serpent figures of Vedic / Canaanite / Mesopotamian myth is real, but distinct: the Rainbow Serpent is creative-generative rather than a chaos-being to be slain. The structural slot is different.

facets.core_claim: Wunggurr-places are deep water-holes; the Rainbow Serpent dwells in waters.

facets.facet_name: Creator and Punisher of the Beautiful Giants
facets.core_claim: The Great Spirit created an earlier race of 'beautiful giants' between the Sierra Nevada and the Rockies. When they mistreated a disfigured child the Great Spirit made the land hot and desolate and allowed enemies to conquer them. Two survived: Paiute and his wife, ancestors of the people.
In later versions of the Paiute oral tradition, the Si-Te-Cah are described as red-haired giant cannibals who preyed on the Paiute and were ultimately exterminated at Lovelock Cave.

Allied tribes trapped the last Si-Te-Cah in Lovelock Cave, filled the entrance with brush, and set it alight, killing the survivors. The pattern of trapping-and-destroying-the-elder-race appears across many giant-traditions.

Semyaza is the leader who proposes the descent and binds the 200 Watchers by a mutual oath on Mount Hermon (1 Enoch 6:3-6). The mountain itself is named Hermon ('curse / oath') because of this binding.
1 Enoch 8:3: 'Semyaza taught enchantments, and root-cuttings.'
Per Book of Giants 4Q530, Semyaza (Shemhazai) is father of the giant twins Ohyah and Hahyah. Talmud Niddah 61a extends the line: Shemhazai → Ahijah → Og of Bashan.

facets.facet_name: Teacher of Metallurgy, Weapons, and Cosmetics
facets.core_claim: 1 Enoch 8:1: 'And Azazel taught men to make swords, and knives, and shields, and breastplates, and made known to them the metals of the earth and the art of working them, and bracelets, and ornaments, and the use of antimony, and the beautifying of the eyelids.' The corruption of humanity by giving them technology and adornment.
1 Enoch 10:4-8: God commands Raphael to bind Azazel hand and foot and cast him into the darkness of Dudael, where he is to remain until the day of the great judgment.

facets.facet_name: Teacher-of-Arts Bound by the Gods (Prometheus Parallel)
facets.core_claim: Azazel and Prometheus occupy the same structural slot: both give humanity transformative arts/technology, both are punished by binding. The valence is inverted: Prometheus is celebrated by Greek tradition as benefactor of humanity, Azazel is condemned by Enochic tradition as corrupter. The same act receives opposite moral judgments — one of the most interesting cases in comparative mythology.
1 Enoch 8:3: 'Araqiel the signs of the earth.'

facets.facet_name: Teacher of Astrology / Signs of the Stars
facets.core_claim: 1 Enoch 8:3: 'Kokabiel the constellations.' The prototype of forbidden astrological knowledge.
1 Enoch 8:3: 'Baraqiel astrology / the signs of the lightnings.'
Per Book of Giants 4Q530 frag 7, Baraqel is father of the giant Mahaway. 4Q203 frag 8: 'Baraqel, my father, was with me.'
1 Enoch 8:3: 'Armaros the resolving of enchantments.'

facets.facet_name: Teacher of the Courses of the Moon
facets.core_claim: 1 Enoch 8:3: 'Sariel the courses of the moon.'

facets.facet_name: Teacher of the Signs of the Sun
facets.core_claim: 1 Enoch 8:3: 'Samsiel the signs of the sun.'
Each commanded a unit of ten subordinate Watchers; together with the leading chiefs they account for all 200 descenders.
Book of Giants 4Q530 col ii lines 15-20: Ohyah sees the Great Holy One descend, take his seat on the throne, and open the books of judgment — a vision strikingly parallel to Daniel 7:9-10.
Ohyah is a son of Shemhazai (Semyaza), the chief Watcher. Talmud Niddah 61a continues the line to Og of Bashan.

Book of Giants 4Q530 col ii lines 6-12: Hahyah sees a great garden with many trees and roots; fire and water destroy them all except one tree of three branches — interpreted as the preservation of Noah and his three sons.
Hahyah is the twin of Ohyah, also son of Shemhazai.

When the giants cannot interpret Ohyah and Hahyah's dreams, they send Mahaway to the translated Enoch. Mahaway flies on wings (Manichaean version explicit). Enoch gives him two tablets — one denouncing the giants, the second pronouncing judgment. Mahaway is the only giant who travels between earth and heaven.
Per Book of Giants 4Q203 frag 8: 'Baraqel, my father, was with me' — Mahaway naming his Watcher-father.

Book of Giants 4Q530 col ii line 2 and 4Q531 frag 22 name Gilgamesh as one of the giants — the only known mention of Gilgamesh in pre-modern non-cuneiform literature. Direct evidence that the Mesopotamian heroic tradition was absorbed into Second Temple Jewish apocalyptic.
Book of Giants 4Q203 frag 3: Hobabish's name is the Aramaic adaptation of Humbaba, the cedar-forest monster of the Gilgamesh epic.
A named giant in the Book of Giants; narratively less prominent than the others.

Goliath of Gath challenges Israel for 40 days; David kills him with a sling-stone (1 Sam 17). Height given as four cubits and a span (~6'9") in 4QSamᵃ and LXX, or six cubits and a span (~9'9") in the Masoretic Text.

facets.facet_name: Descended from the Rephaim of Gath
facets.core_claim: 1 Chronicles 20:8 places Goliath and his brothers 'in the lineage of the giants' (Rephaim) of Gath — the Philistine remnant of the giant-line.

facets.facet_name: The Three Sons of Anak at Hebron
facets.core_claim: Sheshai, Ahiman, and Talmai are the three named sons of Anak at Hebron (Num 13:22). Caleb drives them out (Josh 15:14). Their names are Hurrian by affinity — a striking ethnographic detail that complicates the question of Anakim ethnicity.

1 Chr 20:4-8 names four Rephaim-descended Philistine giants slain by David's men: Ishbi-benob (whose spear weighed 300 shekels of bronze), Saph / Sippai, Lahmi the brother of Goliath, and the unnamed six-fingered six-toed giant. The last remnant of the Rephaim line in Iron Age Israel.

facets.facet_name: The First Mighty Man on Earth
facets.core_claim: Genesis 10:8-12: Nimrod was 'the first on earth to be a mighty man,' 'a mighty hunter before the LORD.' Founder of Babel, Erech (Uruk), and Akkad — the Mesopotamian power centers. The first post-flood political power.

facets.facet_name: Builder of the Tower of Babel
facets.core_claim: Rabbinic tradition makes Nimrod the architect of the Tower of Babel (Gen 11) — the post-flood collective attempt to ascend to heaven, punished by the confusion of languages. Direct structural parallel to the Quinametzin who 'aspired to reach the gods' and were destroyed, and to Otus and Ephialtes piling Pelion on Ossa to reach Olympus.
Rabbinic tradition develops Nimrod as the king who threw Abraham into the fiery furnace, the prototype of the tyrant-ruler set against the people of God. Possibly named from Hebrew mrd 'rebel.'
The Quinametzin lived during a previous Sun-era (sources differ: Sun of Earth, Sun of Rain, Sun of Water). They were the dominant beings of the prior cosmic age.

Aztec tradition credits the Quinametzin with building the Pyramid of Cholula (by Xelhua, a 20-foot giant) and the city of Teotihuacan. The 'giants built the megaliths' motif is widespread across giant-traditions globally.

facets.facet_name: Destroyed by the Gods for Their Sins
facets.core_claim: The Quinametzin were destroyed by flood, earthquake, jaguars, or hurricane (sources vary) because of their sins: aspiring to reach the gods, sins of the flesh, drunkenness, failure to venerate the gods. The structural parallel to Genesis 6 is so close that Spanish chroniclers explicitly identified the two traditions.

Ehecatl (god of wind) takes the bones of the giants from Mictlan to create present-day humanity. Today's humans owe their reduced stature to Ehecatl having dropped and broken the giants' bones. A creation-from-the-bones-of-the-prior-race motif unique to Mesoamerica but structurally analogous to the cosmos-from-Ymir / -Tiamat / -Puruṣa pattern.

facets.facet_name: Builder of the Great Pyramid of Cholula
facets.core_claim: Xelhua, a 20-foot-tall Quinametzin, builds the Pyramid of Cholula (the largest pyramid in the world by volume) and founds seven pre-Columbian cities.

Per Sahagún's Florentine Codex Book 12, Tzilacatzin was an Otomi warrior from Tlatelolco who stood over 10 feet tall and held off Pedro de Alvarado's Spanish forces with rocks the size of watermelons during the siege of Tenochtitlan. Rare case of a giant tradition with a named, datable, near-contemporary individual.

The twelve Titans rule the world before being overthrown by the Olympians (children of Kronos) in the ten-year Titanomachy. Same structural slot as the Vedic asuras, Norse Jötnar, Irish Fomorians, biblical Nephilim/Rephaim, Aztec Quinametzin.
After their defeat, the Titans are imprisoned in Tartarus, guarded by the Hekatonkheires. The cosmic-rebel-bound-in-pit pattern.
The Titans are children of Uranus (Sky) and Gaia (Earth) — the IE sky-father / earth-mother primordial coupling.
